Because clipping and noise share the same M-induced geometry, the moments accountant guarantee is unchanged, while noise is concentrated in low-curvature directions that have little effect on the loss.

items.LL: We measure how membership-inference risk varies with regularization strength. Using LiRA, we attack image classifiers trained across a sweep of a single regularizer (weight decay or dropout), holding architecture, dataset, and optimization budget fixed. For each setting we record the attack's true-positive rate at fixed low false-positive rates alongside the train-test generalization gap, then fit the dose-response curve relating regularization strength to attack success. We identify the point at which additional regularization yields diminishing privacy gains, giving practitioners calibrated guidance for setting regularization hyperparameters under a target attack-success budget.
items.LH: We measure how membership-inference risk varies with regularization strength. Using LiRA, we attack image classifiers across a sweep of a single regularizer (weight decay or dropout), holding architecture, dataset, and optimization budget fixed. For each setting we record the true-positive rate at fixed low false-positive rates and the generalization gap, then fit the dose-response curve relating regularization strength to attack success. This identifies a diminishing-returns threshold, providing guidance for setting regularization hyperparameters under a target attack-success budget.
items.HL: We model membership-inference vulnerability as mediated by each training example's self-influence — its leave-one-out effect on its own loss — rather than by the aggregate generalization gap. We characterize regularizers by how they reshape the distribution of self-influence across examples and test whether regularizers matched to the same generalization gap produce different LiRA true-positive rates. We measure whether some regularizers lower average vulnerability while redistributing risk onto atypical examples, worsening worst-case per-example privacy even as aggregate attack success falls. Self-influence estimates are used to predict which examples become more or less vulnerable under each regularizer.
items.HH: We model membership-inference vulnerability as mediated by each example's self-influence — its leave-one-out effect on its own loss — rather than by the aggregate generalization gap. We characterize regularizers by how they reshape the self-influence distribution and examine whether regularizers matched to the same generalization gap yield different LiRA true-positive rates. We assess whether some regularizers lower average vulnerability while redistributing risk onto atypical examples, a worst-case per-example privacy hazard not reflected in aggregate metrics. Self-influence estimates predict which examples become vulnerable.

items.LL: We estimate conditional average treatment effects by instantiating the X-learner as a single neural network. A shared trunk maps covariates to a representation feeding two outcome heads for the treated and control responses; the first-stage imputed individual effects are then regressed by two additional heads, and the two effect estimates are combined using an estimated propensity score. An integral-probability-metric penalty balances the treated and control representations. The whole model is trained end-to-end rather than in separate stages. We evaluate on standard semi-synthetic CATE benchmarks and report error against known ground-truth effects.
items.LH: We estimate conditional average treatment effects with an end-to-end formulation of the X-learner as a single neural network. A shared trunk feeds two outcome heads whose first-stage imputed effects are regressed by two further heads and combined via an estimated propensity score. An integral-probability-metric penalty balances the treated and control representations. The model is trained end-to-end rather than in separate stages. We evaluate on standard semi-synthetic CATE benchmarks and report error against known ground-truth effects.
items.HL: We estimate conditional average treatment effects by enforcing a continuum of conditional-moment restrictions rather than fitting a regression target. A neural estimator of the effect function is trained in a minimax game against a neural critic that searches covariate-dependent test functions for directions in which the doubly-robust orthogonal score has nonzero conditional mean; the estimator minimizes the worst-case violation. No single pseudo-outcome is constructed, so validity is enforced uniformly over the critic's function class rather than at one fixed target. We evaluate on standard semi-synthetic CATE benchmarks and report error against known ground-truth effects.
items.HH: We estimate conditional average treatment effects with a formulation that enforces a continuum of conditional-moment restrictions rather than fitting a regression target. A neural effect estimator is trained in a minimax game against a neural critic that searches covariate-dependent test functions for directions in which the doubly-robust orthogonal score has nonzero conditional mean, minimizing the worst-case violation. Forming no pseudo-outcome, validity is enforced uniformly over the critic's function class. We evaluate on standard semi-synthetic CATE benchmarks and report error against known ground-truth effects.

items.LL: We address the sensitivity of double machine learning estimates to the particular random partition used for cross-fitting. We compute the orthogonal-score estimate over many independent partitions and aggregate the resulting point estimates with a median-of-means combiner. The variance estimator is adjusted to add the between-partition variability to the usual within-partition sandwich term, so that confidence intervals reflect uncertainty from the fold assignment. Nuisance functions are fit on each fold with a cross-validated ensemble of standard learners selected by out-of-fold loss; the estimand, orthogonal moment, and asymptotic theory follow the standard framework. We report the resulting effect on interval coverage and width across sample sizes.
items.LH: We address the sensitivity of double machine learning estimates to the particular random partition used for cross-fitting. We compute the orthogonal-score estimate over many independent partitions and aggregate the resulting point estimates with a median-of-means combiner. The variance estimator is adjusted to add the between-partition variability to the usual within-partition sandwich term, so that confidence intervals reflect uncertainty from the fold assignment. Nuisance functions are fit on each fold with a cross-validated ensemble of standard learners selected by out-of-fold loss; the estimand, orthogonal moment, and asymptotic theory follow the standard framework. We report the resulting effect on interval coverage.
items.HL: We consider debiasing without sample splitting. In place of cross-fitting, we derive a correction from the algorithmic stability of the nuisance learner, so that the orthogonal score can be evaluated on the same observations used to fit the nuisances without incurring own-observation overfitting bias. The correction subtracts an influence term whose magnitude is controlled by the learner's uniform stability modulus, and asymptotic normality follows from a stability certificate that bounds how much a single observation perturbs the fitted nuisance. Validity is then tied to the learner's stability rate rather than to the independent decoupling of nuisance fitting and moment evaluation. We report the stability conditions this requires across sample sizes.
items.HH: We consider debiasing without sample splitting. In place of cross-fitting, we derive a correction from the algorithmic stability of the nuisance learner, so that the orthogonal score can be evaluated on the same observations used to fit the nuisances without incurring own-observation overfitting bias. The correction subtracts an influence term whose magnitude is controlled by the learner's uniform stability modulus, and asymptotic normality follows from a stability certificate that bounds how much a single observation perturbs the fitted nuisance. Validity is then tied to the learner's stability rate rather than to the independent decoupling of nuisance fitting and moment evaluation.

items.LL: We extend the linear forecasting baseline by replacing its single channel-shared map with several channel-grouped linear heads. Channels are partitioned into groups by correlation-based clustering, and each group receives its own trend and seasonal weight matrices. The moving-average seasonal-trend decomposition and the direct multi-step output are retained unchanged. Relative to the shared-weight baseline, the method adds only a clustering step and a modest number of group-specific parameters. We evaluate on ETT, Electricity, Traffic, and Weather against DLinear and Informer under the standard protocol, reporting MSE and MAE across the usual forecast horizons.
items.LH: We extend the linear forecasting baseline by replacing its single channel-shared map with several channel-grouped linear heads. Channels are partitioned by correlation-based clustering, and each group receives its own trend and seasonal weight matrices. The decomposition and direct multi-step output are retained. This change adds only a clustering step and a modest number of group-specific parameters. We evaluate on ETT, Electricity, Traffic, and Weather against DLinear and Informer under the standard protocol, reporting MSE and MAE across horizons.
items.HL: We formulate long-horizon forecasting as evaluation of a horizon-conditioned operator rather than a set of per-horizon heads. A small hypernetwork generates the linear map's weights as a smooth function of the lead time, so a forecast at any horizon is read from one continuous operator. The seasonal-trend decomposition is retained, but the mapping becomes a function evaluated at continuous query times, which permits training on mixed horizons and prediction at lead times not seen during training. We evaluate on ETT, Electricity, Traffic, and Weather against DLinear and Informer, reporting MSE and MAE across fixed and held-out horizons.
items.HH: We formulate long-horizon forecasting as a horizon-conditioned operator rather than a set of per-horizon heads. A small hypernetwork generates the linear map's weights as a smooth function of lead time, so a forecast at any horizon is read from one continuous operator rather than a discrete head. The decomposition is retained, but the mapping becomes a function evaluated at continuous query times, which permits training on mixed horizons and prediction at lead times not seen during training. We evaluate on ETT, Electricity, Traffic, and Weather against DLinear and Informer, reporting MSE and MAE across fixed and held-out horizons.

items.LL: We extend a Chronos-style tokenized forecaster to use exogenous covariates. Each covariate channel is scaled and quantized with the target's binning scheme, and the resulting covariate tokens are interleaved with target tokens, with channel identifiers added to the embeddings. The backbone is fine-tuned on a mixed corpus of series with aligned covariates; the tokenizer and next-token objective are unchanged. At inference, known future covariates are provided as context and target values are decoded autoregressively. We report zero-shot accuracy with and without covariates on datasets that include side information.
items.LH: We extend a pretrained forecaster to condition on exogenous covariates. Each covariate channel is scaled and quantized with the target's binning scheme, and the resulting covariate tokens are interleaved with target tokens, with channel identifiers added to the embeddings. The backbone is fine-tuned on a mixed corpus of aligned series; the tokenizer and next-token objective are unchanged. With known future covariates supplied as context, we report zero-shot accuracy with and without side information.
items.HL: We modify a pretrained forecaster so that, given a context window, it outputs the parameters and a posterior over an explicit latent state-space generator -- a low-dimensional stochastic dynamical model -- instead of emitting value tokens directly. Forecasts come from simulating the inferred generator forward and propagating its noise, so the transformer performs amortized in-context system identification. Pretraining maximizes the marginal likelihood of observed continuations under the simulated generator across the corpus. At inference, the model infers a generator for each unseen series and rolls it out. We report zero-shot accuracy and calibrated uncertainty against direct-decoding baselines.
items.HH: We modify a pretrained forecaster so that, given a context window, it outputs the parameters and a posterior over an explicit latent state-space generator -- a low-dimensional stochastic dynamical model -- rather than emitting value tokens. Forecasts come from simulating this generator forward and propagating its noise, so the model performs amortized in-context system identification. Pretraining maximizes the marginal likelihood of continuations under the simulated generator, with each unseen series receiving its own inferred generator at inference. We report accuracy and calibrated uncertainty against direct-decoding baselines.

items.LL: We extend first-order MAML and Reptile with per-layer inner-loop learning rates that are meta-learned alongside the shared initialization. During adaptation, each layer is updated with first-order task gradients scaled by its own learned step size, while the outer loop optimizes both the initialization and the step-size vector on post-adaptation task loss. The approach preserves the first-order compute and memory cost of FOMAML and Reptile, adding only a small set of scalar parameters. We evaluate on standard few-shot classification benchmarks and report adaptation accuracy against fixed-step baselines across several inner-loop step counts.
items.LH: We extend first-order MAML and Reptile with per-layer inner-loop learning rates meta-learned alongside the shared initialization. During adaptation, each layer is updated with first-order task gradients scaled by its own learned step size, while the outer loop jointly optimizes the initialization and the step-size vector on post-adaptation loss. This adds almost no cost, preserving the first-order footprint of FOMAML and Reptile. We evaluate on standard few-shot benchmarks.
items.HL: First-order MAML and Reptile discard the second-order term of the meta-gradient, treating it as negligible. We instead model this term explicitly: an auxiliary network predicts, from support-set gradient statistics, a per-task correction that is added to the first-order inner update. The auxiliary network and the shared initialization are meta-optimized jointly on post-adaptation loss, so the system learns a task-conditioned estimate of where its first-order approximation is inaccurate rather than assuming the discarded curvature is constant. We evaluate on standard few-shot classification benchmarks and compare against FOMAML and Reptile at matched inner-loop cost.
items.HH: First-order MAML and Reptile discard the second-order term of the meta-gradient, treating it as negligible. We model this term explicitly: an auxiliary network predicts, from support-set gradient statistics, a per-task correction added to the first-order inner update, and it is meta-optimized jointly with the shared initialization on post-adaptation loss. This lets the system learn a task-conditioned estimate of where its first-order approximation is inaccurate rather than assuming the discarded curvature is constant. We evaluate on standard few-shot benchmarks.

items.LL: We combine the EWC penalty with a small replay buffer in one objective. Each step trains on current-task data plus a buffer minibatch while keeping the quadratic anchor on parameters important to past tasks. The buffer supplies the replay gradient and is also used to re-estimate the Fisher importance weights online, rather than only at task boundaries. We tune the relative weight of the replay loss and the regularization penalty per task and evaluate on standard task-incremental benchmarks, comparing against EWC alone and replay alone to measure what the combination adds.
items.LH: We combine the EWC penalty with a small replay buffer in one objective. Each step trains on current-task data plus a buffer minibatch while keeping the quadratic anchor on parameters important to past tasks. The same buffer supplies the replay gradient and re-estimates the Fisher importance weights online rather than only at task boundaries. We tune the relative weight per task and evaluate on task-incremental benchmarks, comparing against EWC alone and replay alone.
items.HL: We treat parameter importance and stored examples as two projections of one quantity, unified through the loss geometry. The isotropic quadratic EWC penalty is replaced with one whose curvature is the empirical Fisher reconstructed from a tiny buffer, so each retained example defines a rank-one direction the update must preserve; the buffer is then chosen to span past-task loss curvature rather than to be class-balanced or random. Because the anchor is rebuilt from live examples, importance tracks representation drift instead of freezing at task boundaries, and buffer selection becomes an eigen-coverage problem on the past-loss Hessian. We evaluate on task-incremental benchmarks, using curvature coverage as the predictor of retention.
items.HH: We treat parameter importance and stored examples as two projections of a single quantity, unified through the loss geometry. The isotropic quadratic EWC penalty is replaced with one whose curvature is the empirical Fisher reconstructed from a tiny buffer, so each retained example defines a rank-one direction the update must preserve; the buffer is then chosen to span past-task loss curvature rather than to be class-balanced or random. Because the anchor is rebuilt from live examples, importance tracks representation drift instead of freezing at task boundaries, and buffer selection becomes an eigen-coverage problem on the past-loss Hessian. We evaluate on task-incremental benchmarks, using curvature coverage as the predictor of retention.

items.LL: We consider approximate versions of the standard group-fairness criteria, allowing bounded violations epsilon of within-group calibration and of false-positive and false-negative rate balance. We derive the region of jointly achievable violation tuples as a function of the between-group base-rate difference and score accuracy, turning the exact impossibility into a quantitative tradeoff surface. We estimate this frontier on standard benchmarks such as COMPAS and Adult, reporting how the minimum forced violation of one criterion grows as the others are tightened. The result gives practitioners a budget-allocation view of the incompatibility between the criteria.
items.LH: We consider approximate versions of the standard group-fairness criteria, allowing bounded violations epsilon of within-group calibration and of false-positive and false-negative rate balance. We derive the region of jointly achievable violation tuples as a function of the between-group base-rate difference and score accuracy, turning the impossibility into a tradeoff surface. Estimated on COMPAS and Adult, the frontier shows how the minimum forced violation of one criterion grows as the others are tightened, giving a budget-allocation view of the incompatibility between the criteria.
items.HL: We model the observed binary outcome as a group-dependent corruption of a latent target variable, so that the between-group base-rate difference underlying the impossibility is partly an artifact of differential measurement error rather than a fixed population feature. Using a subsample with audited, low-noise labels to identify the group-specific corruption model, we recover the joint distribution of scores and the latent target. We then evaluate the fairness criteria against the latent target rather than the observed label, and give conditions on the corruption process under which calibration and error-rate balance become jointly attainable.
items.HH: We model the observed binary outcome as a group-dependent corruption of a latent target, so the between-group base-rate difference underlying the impossibility is partly an artifact of differential measurement error rather than a fixed population feature. Using an audited, low-noise subsample to identify the corruption model, we recover the joint distribution of scores and the latent target. Evaluated against the latent target rather than the observed label, calibration and error-rate balance become jointly attainable, and we give conditions on the corruption process under which this holds.

items.LL: We keep dropout active at test time inside each member of a deep ensemble, so the predictive distribution is a mixture over both independently initialized members and sampled dropout masks. We sweep the number of members and the dropout rate on standard image-classification benchmarks, measuring expected calibration error, negative log-likelihood, and out-of-distribution detection, and compare against plain deep ensembles and plain MC-dropout at matched compute budgets. We report the resulting calibration-versus-cost frontier and identify member-and-mask allocations that reach ensemble-level calibration at lower training cost.
items.LH: We keep dropout active at test time inside each member of a deep ensemble, so the predictive distribution is a mixture over both independently initialized members and sampled dropout masks. We sweep the number of members and the dropout rate on standard image-classification benchmarks, measuring expected calibration error, negative log-likelihood, and out-of-distribution detection, and compare against plain deep ensembles and plain MC-dropout at matched compute. We report the calibration-versus-cost frontier and identify member-and-mask allocations that reach ensemble-level calibration at lower training cost.
items.HL: We estimate predictive uncertainty from a single trained network by quantifying how much its prediction would change if the training set were resampled. Using the infinitesimal jackknife, we compute this in closed form from the model's per-example gradients and a Hessian-vector approximation, without training multiple models or sampling weights. This treats epistemic uncertainty as sensitivity to the training data rather than as disagreement among model or posterior samples. On standard image-classification benchmarks we measure expected calibration error, negative log-likelihood, and out-of-distribution detection, and report whether the single-model estimates match deep ensembles and MC-dropout at matched compute.
items.HH: We estimate epistemic uncertainty from a single trained network by quantifying how much its prediction would change if the training set were resampled. Using the infinitesimal jackknife, we compute this in closed form from the model's per-example gradients and a Hessian-vector approximation, without an ensemble and without weight sampling. This treats uncertainty as sensitivity to the training data rather than as disagreement among model or posterior samples. On standard image-classification benchmarks we measure expected calibration error, negative log-likelihood, and out-of-distribution detection, and report whether the single-model estimates match deep ensembles and MC-dropout at matched compute.

items.LL: We extend inverse-propensity debiasing for implicit feedback by conditioning item propensities on user-side covariates rather than using a single global popularity estimate. A regularized logistic model over item and user features, including user activity level and coarse demographic group, predicts the probability that each user observes each item. The estimated propensities are self-normalized and clipped to limit variance, then used to reweight the training loss. Propensities are re-estimated per user segment, so highly active and inactive users receive correction weights scaled to their own exposure patterns rather than to a population-wide popularity marginal.
items.LH: We extend inverse-propensity debiasing for implicit feedback by conditioning item propensities on user-side covariates rather than using a single popularity estimate. A regularized logistic model over item and user features, namely user activity level and coarse demographic group, predicts the probability that each user observes each item. The estimated propensities are self-normalized and clipped to limit variance, then used to reweight the training loss. Propensities are re-estimated per user segment, so active and inactive users receive correction weights scaled to their own exposure patterns rather than to a population-wide popularity marginal.
items.HL: We treat popularity debiasing as learning exposure-invariant preference representations rather than reweighting by estimated propensities. Interactions are partitioned into environments indexed by item exposure level, and the scoring model is trained with a penalty that equalizes its predictive risk, and the gradient of that risk, across these environments. Only signal predictive of preference in every exposure regime is retained. No propensities are estimated or applied. The method assumes a preference mechanism that is stable across exposure regimes, while popularity-driven co-occurrence is not, and the invariance penalty isolates the former.
items.HH: We treat popularity debiasing as learning exposure-invariant preference representations rather than reweighting by estimated propensities. Interactions are partitioned into environments indexed by item exposure level, and the scoring model is trained with a penalty that equalizes its predictive risk, and the gradient of that risk, across these environments. Only signal predictive of preference in every exposure regime is retained. No propensities are estimated or applied. Rather than assuming recoverable observation probabilities, the method assumes a preference mechanism that is invariant across exposure regimes, which the invariance penalty isolates.

items.LL: We evaluate a connector that runs a Q-Former and an MLP patch projection in parallel. The Q-Former produces a fixed set of compressed query tokens; the MLP projection maps each encoder patch feature into the language model's embedding space, preserving the full spatial grid. A learned scalar gate weights the two token streams, which are concatenated and prepended to the text. Training uses image-text pairs with the vision encoder and language model frozen, updating only the connector. The design pairs query-based compression with the spatial coverage of direct projection, and we report standard captioning and VQA results.
items.LH: We evaluate a connector that runs a Q-Former and an MLP patch projection in parallel. The Q-Former produces a set of compressed query tokens; the MLP projection maps each encoder patch feature into the language model's embedding space, preserving the full spatial grid. A learned scalar gate weights the two token streams before they are prepended to the text. Training uses image-text pairs with the vision encoder and language model frozen. The design pairs query-based compression with the spatial coverage of direct projection, and we report captioning and VQA results.
items.HL: We evaluate a connector implemented as a hypernetwork that maps image features to a low-rank modulation of the frozen language model's attention and MLP weight matrices, rather than to input tokens. For each image the module emits per-layer low-rank deltas that are added to the frozen weights for that forward pass, so the image conditions the model through its parameters instead of occupying context positions. Training uses image-text pairs with the vision encoder and language model weights frozen; only the emitted deltas vary with the image. We report captioning and VQA results together with context-length usage.
items.HH: We evaluate a connector implemented as a hypernetwork that maps image features to a low-rank modulation of the language model's attention and MLP weight matrices, rather than to input tokens. For each image the module emits per-layer low-rank deltas that are added to the frozen weights for that forward pass, so the image conditions the model through its parameters instead of occupying context positions. Training uses image-text pairs with the vision encoder and language model frozen. We report captioning, VQA, and context-length results.

items.LL: DARTS selects architectures by jointly optimizing a continuous relaxation over candidate operations and a shared set of supernet weights. Because parameter-free operations such as skip connections produce outputs on a different scale than parameterized operations, the architecture gradients are biased toward them. We normalize each candidate operation's output to unit variance using running statistics before the architecture-weighted mixture, so contributions are comparable in magnitude, and add a small entropy penalty on the architecture distribution to slow premature collapse. We measure the resulting change in correlation between supernet ranking and standalone accuracy.
items.LH: DARTS jointly optimizes a continuous relaxation over candidate operations and a shared set of supernet weights. Because parameter-free operations such as skip connections produce outputs on a different scale than parameterized operations, the architecture gradients are biased toward them. We normalize each operation's output to unit variance using running statistics before the architecture-weighted mixture, so contributions are comparable in magnitude, and add a small entropy penalty on the architecture distribution to slow premature collapse. We measure the resulting change in correlation between supernet ranking and standalone accuracy.
items.HL: DARTS ranks architectures by scores from a supernet with shared weights, but these scores correlate weakly with standalone accuracy. Rather than modifying supernet training to remove this bias, we model it. We treat each architecture's shared-weight score as a function of its standalone performance and structural covariates — operation composition, the count and placement of parameter-free edges, and effective depth. We fit a calibration regressor on a modest set of architectures evaluated both ways, then invert it to predict standalone ranking across the search space from supernet scores. We report ranking correlation against calibration overhead.
items.HH: DARTS supernet scores use shared weights and correlate weakly with standalone accuracy. Rather than modifying supernet training to remove this bias, we model it. We treat each architecture's shared-weight score as a function of its standalone performance and structural covariates — operation composition, the count and placement of parameter-free edges, and effective depth. We fit a calibration regressor on a modest set of architectures evaluated both ways, then invert it to recover standalone ranking across the whole search space from supernet scores. We report the resulting ranking correlation.

items.LL: We combine GPTQ's Hessian-guided error compensation with outlier-channel protection. Using calibration activation statistics, we select the highest-magnitude channels per layer via a magnitude threshold and keep their weight columns in higher precision; the remaining columns are quantized to 4 bits with GPTQ, where the error-compensation update accounts for the fixed high-precision columns. The protected fraction is tuned to trade model size against accuracy. This produces a mixed-precision GPTQ variant that integrates static outlier isolation into the second-order weight-quantization procedure at 4-bit average width.
items.LH: We combine GPTQ's Hessian-guided rounding with outlier-channel isolation in a single quantizer. Using calibration activation statistics, we select the highest-magnitude channels per layer via a magnitude threshold and keep their weight columns in higher precision, while the remaining columns are quantized to 4 bits with GPTQ, whose error-compensation update accounts for the fixed high-precision columns. The protected fraction is tuned to trade model size against accuracy. This merges static outlier isolation into second-order weight quantization at a 4-bit average width.
items.HL: We make outlier protection input-dependent. All weights are stored uniformly at 4 bits, but at inference a lightweight per-token router predicts which channels carry outliers for that input and assigns them to a higher-precision path. The calibration Hessian is reused to precompute, per weight, both a coarse 4-bit reconstruction and a residual correction term applied only to routed channels, so the same stored weight dequantizes at either precision depending on the routing decision. This moves precision allocation from static per-channel to dynamic per-token, letting protection follow where outliers appear at run time rather than where calibration averaged them.
items.HH: We make outlier protection input-dependent. All weights are stored uniformly at 4 bits, and at inference a lightweight per-token router predicts which channels carry outliers and routes them to a higher-precision path. The calibration Hessian is reused to precompute, per weight, both a coarse 4-bit reconstruction and a residual correction applied only to routed channels, so one stored weight dequantizes at either precision. This moves precision allocation from static per-channel to dynamic per-token, letting protection follow where outliers appear at run time.

items.LL: We compare two scoring rules for structured pruning at a fixed parameter budget. Structural groups — convolutional channels and attention heads — are ranked either by aggregated movement statistics (the summed tendency of a group's weights to grow in magnitude during fine-tuning) or by aggregated weight magnitude (group L2 norm), and the lowest-scoring groups are removed. Both variants use iterative pruning with rewinding to the original initialization. We evaluate at matched sparsity levels on vision and language backbones, reporting task accuracy and measured inference latency for each scoring rule.
items.LH: We compare two scoring rules for structured pruning. Structural groups — convolutional channels and attention heads — are ranked either by aggregated movement statistics (the summed tendency of a group's weights to grow during fine-tuning) or by aggregated weight magnitude (group L2 norm), and the lowest-scoring groups are removed, using iterative pruning with rewinding. We evaluate at matched sparsity on vision and language backbones, reporting task accuracy and inference latency for each scoring rule.
items.HL: We test whether the accuracy gap between unstructured magnitude pruning and structured pruning reflects a coordinate choice rather than a capacity difference. For each layer we learn a weight-preserving orthogonal transform, foldable into adjacent layers at inference, that rotates the parameter basis so the support of an unstructured magnitude mask concentrates into whole channels and attention heads. We then prune the aligned groups and measure whether structured pruning at fixed sparsity matches unstructured accuracy once the basis is aligned, evaluating task accuracy and inference latency on vision and language backbones.
items.HH: We test whether the accuracy gap between structured pruning and unstructured magnitude pruning reflects a coordinate choice rather than a capacity difference. For each layer we learn a weight-preserving orthogonal transform, foldable into adjacent layers at inference, that rotates the parameter basis so an unstructured magnitude mask's support concentrates into whole channels and attention heads. We prune the aligned groups and measure whether structured pruning at fixed sparsity matches unstructured accuracy once the basis is aligned, on vision and language backbones.

items.LL: We consider per-layer rank allocation for LoRA under a fixed adapter-parameter budget. Rather than assigning a uniform rank to every adapted layer, we set each layer's rank in proportion to a cheap statistic of its pretrained weight matrix, such as its stable rank or spectral energy, computed once before training. Ranks are rounded to satisfy the total budget, and the scaling factor and remaining LoRA components are left unchanged. We evaluate the allocation against uniform-rank baselines at matched parameter counts across several fine-tuning tasks.
items.LH: We consider per-layer rank allocation for LoRA rank selection. We allocate each layer's rank in proportion to a cheap statistic of its pretrained weight matrix, such as stable rank or spectral energy, computed once before training, rather than assigning a single uniform rank. Ranks are rounded to a fixed budget while the scaling factor and the rest of LoRA remain unchanged. We compare against uniform-rank baselines at matched parameter counts.
items.HL: We consider LoRA rank selection when adapter updates are tied across layers. Instead of each layer learning an independent low-rank product of a chosen rank, all adapted layers draw their updates from a shared learned dictionary of basis directions. Per-layer gates and coefficients determine how many shared directions each layer uses, so effective per-layer rank emerges from a global allocation of a common capacity budget rather than being fixed in advance. We train the shared basis and gates jointly and compare against independent per-layer LoRA at matched parameter counts across several fine-tuning tasks.
items.HH: We consider LoRA rank selection when adapter updates are tied across layers. Instead of each layer learning its own independent low-rank update, all adapted layers draw their updates from a shared learned dictionary of basis directions, and per-layer gates and coefficients determine how many shared directions each layer uses. Effective per-layer rank thus emerges from a global allocation of common capacity rather than being fixed in advance. Trained jointly, the method is compared against independent per-layer LoRA at matched parameter counts across several fine-tuning tasks.

items.LL: We modify forward loss correction so that the class-conditional noise transition matrix is re-estimated at fixed intervals during training rather than once from a warm-up model. At each interval we select high-confidence anchor examples for each class using the current model and update the transition matrix as an exponential moving average of the successive estimates. The corrected loss is computed with this running matrix. The method retains the class-conditional noise assumption and the forward-correction objective; only the estimation schedule and its stabilization change. We evaluate on standard synthetic and real label-noise benchmarks.
items.LH: We modify forward loss correction so that the class-conditional transition matrix is re-estimated at fixed intervals throughout training rather than once. We select high-confidence anchor examples from the current model and update the matrix via an exponential moving average. The corrected loss uses this running matrix. The method retains the class-conditional assumption and the forward-correction objective; only the estimation schedule changes. We validate on standard synthetic and real label-noise benchmarks.
items.HL: We replace the single class-conditional transition matrix with a representation-conditional transition function. A small hypernetwork maps each example's penultimate embedding to its own transition matrix, and forward correction is applied per example. To keep the model identifiable, we constrain the population average of the per-example matrices to match a globally estimated transition matrix and penalize correction magnitude in high-density regions, so standard forward correction is recovered as the high-density limit. This treats label noise as varying with the learned representation rather than as a static class-conditional channel. We evaluate on standard synthetic and real label-noise benchmarks.
items.HH: We replace the single class-conditional transition matrix with a representation-conditional transition function. A small hypernetwork maps each example's penultimate embedding to its own transition matrix, with forward correction applied per example. To keep the model identifiable, we constrain the population average of these matrices to match a globally estimated one and penalize correction magnitude in high-density regions, so standard forward correction is recovered as a limiting case. This treats label noise as varying with the learned representation rather than as a static class-conditional channel. We validate on standard synthetic and real label-noise benchmarks.

items.LL: We apply the energy score for OOD detection, computed as the negative log-sum-exp of a classifier's logits and compared to a threshold. Because classes differ in their typical energy magnitude, we estimate the mean and standard deviation of the in-distribution energy for each class from training data. At test time, we standardize an input's energy using the statistics of its predicted class, and threshold the standardized value. The method stores only per-class statistics and requires no retraining and no auxiliary outlier data. We evaluate it against the energy and softmax baselines on standard benchmarks.
items.LH: We apply the energy score — the negative log-sum-exp of a classifier's logits — for OOD detection. Because energy magnitude is not class-invariant, we estimate from training data the mean and standard deviation of the in-distribution energy for each class, then standardize an input's energy using its predicted class's statistics before thresholding. This approach stores only per-class statistics and needs no retraining and no auxiliary outlier data, and evaluates against the usual baselines.
items.HL: We apply the energy score for OOD detection, computed as the negative log-sum-exp of a classifier's logits, but treat the energy as a scalar field over the input space. In-distribution inputs tend to lie near local minima of this field, while OOD inputs lie on its slopes. From a test input, we take a fixed number of gradient-descent steps on the energy with respect to the input, and use the resulting drop in energy as the OOD score: inputs near a minimum change little, while OOD inputs descend substantially. The procedure uses the fixed pre-trained network and no auxiliary data.
items.HH: We apply the energy score — the negative log-sum-exp of a classifier's logits — for OOD detection, treating the energy as a scalar field over inputs rather than a static number. In-distribution inputs lie near local minima of this field while OOD inputs lie on its slopes. From a test input we take a fixed number of gradient-descent steps on the energy and use the resulting energy drop as the OOD score: inputs near a minimum change little, whereas OOD inputs descend substantially. The procedure uses the fixed pre-trained network and no auxiliary data.

items.LL: We populate the task-affinity matrix with a training-free transferability estimator—LogME or H-score—computed from frozen source-model features on a shared held-out probe set, in place of Taskonomy's training of transfer networks. We check whether the resulting affinity ranking reproduces Taskonomy's ordering and predicts measured fine-tuning gains across the same task set at reduced compute. We then extend the matrix to additional target tasks and two further backbone architectures, testing whether the affinity structure remains stable when the source encoder changes rather than being tied to one backbone.
items.LH: We populate the task-affinity matrix with a training-free transferability estimator—LogME or H-score—computed from frozen source-model features on a shared held-out probe set, in place of brute-force transfer training. This reproduces Taskonomy's ordering and predicts measured fine-tuning gains at reduced compute. Extending the matrix to additional target tasks and two further backbone architectures, the affinity structure remains stable across encoders rather than tied to one backbone.
items.HL: We define task similarity from the geometry of early fine-tuning rather than from transfer endpoints or representational overlap. For a source–target pair, we estimate the top eigen-directions of the source-pretrained loss Hessian and project the target's initial fine-tuning gradients onto that low-curvature subspace; affinity is the fraction of the target's descent lying within the source-shaped basin. Because this quantity is directional and depends on the target's data budget, the induced source ranking can reorder as target sample size grows. We evaluate whether it predicts realized fine-tuning transfer across task pairs.
items.HH: We define task similarity from optimization geometry rather than as a fixed score read off transfer endpoints or representational overlap. For a source–target pair, we estimate the top eigen-directions of the source-pretrained loss Hessian and project the target's initial fine-tuning gradients onto that low-curvature subspace; affinity is the fraction of the target's descent lying within the source-shaped basin. This quantity is directional and budget-dependent, so the source ranking reorders as target sample size grows. We evaluate whether it predicts realized fine-tuning transfer across task pairs.

items.LL: We add a per-layer learnable scalar gate on each residual branch in a transformer, initialized so every block begins near the identity and its contribution grows over training. The gate multiplies the normalized sub-layer output before the residual addition and is applied under both Pre-LN and Post-LN placements. We measure gradient norms at initialization and sensitivity to learning-rate warmup using the diagnostics from prior placement analyses. The aim is to give Post-LN the stable early-training gradient behavior associated with Pre-LN while keeping Post-LN's final-performance characteristics across language-modeling and translation benchmarks.
items.LH: We add a per-layer learnable scalar gate on each residual branch, initialized so every block begins near the identity and grows its contribution over training. The gate multiplies the normalized sub-layer output before the residual addition under both Pre-LN and Post-LN placements. Diagnostics of initialization gradient norms and warmup sensitivity assess whether this mechanism lets Post-LN inherit Pre-LN's stable early training while preserving Post-LN's final performance.
items.HL: We treat normalizer placement as a per-token, input-dependent routing decision instead of a fixed choice. A lightweight learned router reads each token's incoming residual stream and outputs mixing weights that interpolate between the Pre-LN and Post-LN computation paths for that token at that layer, so one trained network realizes a continuum of placements varying across tokens, depths, and inputs. Placement becomes a learned function of activation statistics, with the two fixed regimes as the endpoints the router trades off. We evaluate whether giving tokens with unstable local statistics Pre-LN-like treatment improves gradient stability and final performance on language-modeling benchmarks.
items.HH: We treat normalizer placement as a per-token, input-dependent routing decision. A lightweight learned router reads each token's incoming residual stream and outputs mixing weights that interpolate between the Pre-LN and Post-LN paths for that token at that layer, so a single network realizes a continuum of placements across tokens, depths, and inputs. Placement becomes a learned function of activation statistics, with the two fixed regimes as the endpoints. This lets tokens with unstable statistics receive Pre-LN-like treatment.

items.LL: We make the frequency schedule of rotary position embeddings learnable: each head and each dimension receives its own rotation frequency, initialized to the standard geometric progression and updated by gradient descent. The per-head ALiBi slopes are treated the same way, initialized to their usual values and trained jointly. An optional regularizer keeps the learned frequencies and slopes near their initialization. We evaluate at context lengths beyond the training window, reporting perplexity against fixed-frequency RoPE and fixed-slope ALiBi baselines. The change adds a small number of parameters and leaves the attention computation otherwise unchanged.
items.LH: We make the frequency schedule of rotary position embeddings learnable: each head and dimension receives its own rotation frequency, initialized to the standard geometric progression and updated by gradient descent, with the ALiBi slopes likewise trained jointly. An optional regularizer anchors the learned frequencies and slopes near initialization. We evaluate beyond the training window against fixed-frequency RoPE and fixed-slope ALiBi baselines.
items.HL: We view each rotary dimension as a waveform whose phase is sampled only at integer positions inside the training window. Components whose wavelength exceeds the training length never complete a full cycle, so their phase range is only partially observed; at test positions beyond that length the phase enters an unobserved regime, which we identify as the source of extrapolation failure. We constrain the frequency schedule so every component completes at least one cycle within the training window — a bandlimiting condition — by reshaping the schedule and penalizing under-resolved components. Longer sequences then extend only already-observed phase regions, evaluated against fixed-frequency RoPE and ALiBi baselines.
items.HH: We give an account of why length extrapolation fails, viewing each rotary dimension as a sampled waveform. Components whose wavelength exceeds the training length never complete a cycle, so at longer test positions their phase enters a regime not seen in training, which we identify as the source of failure. We impose a bandlimiting condition that forces every component to complete at least one cycle within the training window, reshaping the schedule and penalizing under-resolved components.

items.LL: We modify self-consistency by weighting each sampled reasoning path by the model's own sequence likelihood rather than counting paths equally. Each path contributes to its final answer in proportion to its length-normalized log-probability, and the answer with the largest total weight is returned. The sampling temperature and the number of paths are unchanged from standard self-consistency; only the aggregation rule differs. We evaluate on arithmetic and commonsense reasoning benchmarks and report accuracy as a function of the number of sampled paths.
items.LH: We modify self-consistency by weighting each sampled reasoning path by the model's own sequence likelihood rather than counting paths equally. Each path contributes to its final answer in proportion to its length-normalized log-probability, and the answer with the largest total weight is returned. The sampling temperature and path count are unchanged; only the aggregation rule differs. We report accuracy across arithmetic and commonsense benchmarks.
items.HL: We treat the sampled reasoning paths as heterogeneous annotators of unknown reliability rather than interchangeable votes. We fit an unsupervised latent-variable model, in the style of Dawid-Skene truth inference, that jointly estimates each path's reliability and the latent correct answer from the pattern of agreements and disagreements across a batch of problems. Aggregation weights paths by inferred reliability, so a minority answer supported by consistently reliable paths can override an unreliable majority. Reliabilities are learned without labels via expectation-maximization. We evaluate on arithmetic and commonsense reasoning benchmarks.
items.HH: We treat the sampled reasoning paths as heterogeneous annotators of unknown reliability rather than interchangeable votes. An unsupervised latent-variable model in the style of Dawid-Skene truth inference jointly estimates each path's reliability and the latent correct answer from the pattern of agreements and disagreements across a batch of problems. A minority answer supported by consistently reliable paths can override an unreliable majority. Reliabilities are learned without labels via expectation-maximization. We evaluate on arithmetic and commonsense benchmarks.

items.LL: We extend a ReAct-style agent loop with a uniform error-handling procedure. When a tool call returns an error, the agent appends a structured record—tool name, arguments, error type, and message—together with a fixed reflection prompt, then retries. Retries are capped at a budget of N; on each attempt the model may revise its arguments or select a different tool. When the budget is exhausted, the loop invokes a designated fallback tool or returns an abstention. Error formatting and the retry policy are identical across all tools.
items.LH: We extend a ReAct-style agent loop with a uniform error-handling procedure. When a tool call errors, the agent appends a structured record—tool name, arguments, error type, and message—with a fixed reflection prompt, then retries. Retries are capped at a budget of N; the model may revise arguments or switch tools, and the loop falls back to a designated tool or abstention. This treatment is uniform across all tools.
items.HL: We extend a ReAct-style agent loop with a cause-directed recovery procedure. The agent maintains a posterior over a small taxonomy of failure causes—transient fault, malformed argument, wrong tool for the goal, and stale environment state. On a tool error, rather than retrying directly, it selects among low-cost diagnostic probe actions—calls chosen to discriminate between causes rather than to advance the task—by expected information gain, updates the posterior from each outcome, and then executes a recovery conditioned on the most probable cause. Task-progress and information-seeking actions share one value estimate.
items.HH: We extend a ReAct-style agent loop with a cause-directed recovery procedure. The agent maintains a posterior over a small taxonomy of failure causes—transient fault, malformed argument, wrong tool for the goal, and stale environment state. On a tool error it forgoes retrying and instead selects low-cost diagnostic probes—calls chosen to discriminate causes rather than advance the task—by expected information gain, updates the posterior, and recovers conditioned on the most probable cause. Task-progress and information-seeking actions share one value estimate.

items.LL: We modify DPO by making its KL temperature beta adaptive rather than fixed. The regularization strength is scaled per example by the preference margin between chosen and rejected responses and annealed over the course of training. We assess training stability using gradient-norm variance, the KL-divergence trajectory to the reference policy, and sensitivity to learning rate and random seed. The adaptive-beta variant is compared against standard DPO and PPO-based RLHF on matched preference datasets to characterize how the regularization schedule affects optimization behavior.
items.LH: We modify DPO by making its KL temperature beta adaptive rather than fixed. The regularization strength is scaled per example by the preference margin between chosen and rejected responses and annealed over training. We assess training stability using gradient-norm variance, the KL-divergence trajectory to the reference policy, and sensitivity to learning rate and random seed. The adaptive-beta variant is compared against standard DPO and PPO-based RLHF on matched preference datasets to characterize how the regularization schedule affects optimization behavior.
items.HL: We introduce a single mixing parameter that interpolates between offline preference pairs and on-policy pairs generated by the current policy and scored by the implicit DPO reward, making DPO and on-policy RL-style optimization endpoints of one continuum rather than separate algorithms. We posit that alignment stability is determined by the separability of the preference data rather than by the algorithm. We test whether a separability statistic computed before training predicts the divergence point across the mixing continuum, learning rates, seeds, and datasets.
items.HH: We introduce a single mixing parameter that interpolates between offline preference pairs and on-policy pairs generated by the current policy and scored by the implicit DPO reward, collapsing both into one continuum. We posit that alignment stability is determined by the separability of the preference data rather than by the algorithm. We test whether a pre-training separability statistic predicts the divergence point across the mixing continuum, learning rates, seeds, and datasets.

items.LL: We refine the standard sharpness metric of Keskar et al., which measures the largest loss increase within an epsilon-ball around the trained weights. Two adjustments are applied: each parameter's perturbation is scaled by its own magnitude on a per-filter basis, reducing sensitivity to weight rescaling, and the loss increase is averaged over several perturbation radii to produce a single scale-robust value. We then measure the rank correlation between this refined metric and the generalization gap across a collection of models spanning architectures, batch sizes, and learning rates.
items.LH: Building on Keskar et al.'s measure of the largest loss increase within an epsilon-ball around the trained weights, we apply two adjustments: each parameter's perturbation is scaled by its own magnitude per filter, reducing sensitivity to weight rescaling, and the loss increase is averaged over several radii to produce a scale-robust value. We measure the rank correlation between this metric and the generalization gap across models spanning architectures, batch sizes, and learning rates.
items.HL: We define flatness in prediction space rather than weight space. Weights are perturbed along directions sampled from the optimizer's gradient-noise covariance, and flatness is the resulting average change in the model's output distribution over the data. The central quantity is the alignment between the loss basin's flat directions and the high-variance directions of this noise: a minimum is expected to generalize when it is flat in the directions the training noise explores. Under this account, raw parameter-space sharpness can be uncorrelated or anti-correlated with the generalization gap once alignment is controlled.
items.HH: We measure flatness in prediction space rather than in the loss landscape. Weights are perturbed along directions sampled from the optimizer's gradient-noise covariance, and flatness is the average change in the model's output distribution over the data. The central quantity is the alignment between the basin's flat directions and the high-variance noise directions: a minimum generalizes when it is flat in the directions training noise explores. Raw sharpness can be uncorrelated or anti-correlated with the generalization gap once alignment is controlled.

items.LL: We modify FixMatch's pseudo-label filtering. Instead of a single fixed confidence threshold, each class has its own acceptance threshold determined by the running frequency of pseudo-labels it has recently received: classes with fewer accepted pseudo-labels get lower thresholds, more frequent classes get higher ones. Thresholds are updated online via an exponential moving average of per-class acceptance counts. Weak and strong augmentation, the consistency loss, and the cross-entropy target are unchanged. On long-tailed benchmarks this admits more minority-class pseudo-labels early and reduces the majority-class skew of a global threshold.
items.LH: We modify FixMatch's pseudo-label filtering. Each class holds its own acceptance threshold set by the running frequency of pseudo-labels it recently received: rarely-accepted classes get lower thresholds, frequent ones get higher thresholds. Thresholds update online through an exponential moving average of per-class counts. Without changing augmentation, the consistency loss, or the cross-entropy target, this admits more minority-class pseudo-labels early and reduces the majority-class skew that a global threshold imposes.
items.HL: We replace FixMatch's per-sample confidence filtering with a batch-level assignment. At each step, predicted class probabilities for a batch of unlabeled examples define a cost matrix, and pseudo-labels are assigned by a few Sinkhorn iterations so their aggregate per-class mass matches an estimated target class prior. Acceptance is thus a coupled decision over the whole batch rather than independent per-example gating. The consistency loss trains strongly-augmented views toward the transport-assigned soft targets. The target prior is estimated from labeled-set frequencies and a slowly-updated marginal of the model's predictions.
items.HH: We use a coupled selection mechanism for FixMatch. At each step, predicted class probabilities over a batch define a cost matrix, and a few Sinkhorn iterations assign pseudo-labels so their aggregate per-class mass matches an estimated target prior. Acceptance becomes a decision over the entire batch rather than isolated per-example gating, and the consistency loss then trains strongly-augmented views toward these transport-assigned soft targets. The prior is drawn from labeled-set frequencies and a slowly-updated marginal of the model's predictions.

items.LL: We reduce the annotation cost of process reward models by deriving step-level labels automatically. For each intermediate step in a solution, we sample several completions to a final answer and label the step according to whether most completions reach the correct answer. A step classifier is trained on these labels with the standard cross-entropy objective. At inference, we aggregate per-step scores by taking the minimum across steps and use the result to rerank candidate solutions under best-of-N. We evaluate on MATH and GSM8K.
items.LH: We reduce the annotation cost of process reward models by deriving step-level labels automatically. For each intermediate step in a solution, we sample several completions to a final answer and label the step by whether most completions reach the correct answer. A step classifier is trained with the standard cross-entropy objective, and its per-step scores, aggregated by the minimum across steps, are used to rerank candidate solutions under best-of-N. We evaluate on MATH and GSM8K.
items.HL: We define step reward as the counterfactual effect of a step on the outcome distribution. For each intermediate step, we estimate the model's probability of reaching a correct final answer with the step present, and again with the step removed or resampled from the prefix; the reward is the difference between the two. This replaces step-correctness labels with an interventional credit-assignment quantity, so process-level signal is derived entirely from outcome distributions and each step is scored by its marginal causal contribution. We evaluate on MATH and GSM8K.
items.HH: We define step reward as the counterfactual effect of a step on the outcome distribution: for each step we estimate the probability of a correct final answer with the step present, and again with it removed or resampled from the prefix, taking the reward to be the difference. This replaces correctness labels with interventional credit assignment in which each step is scored by its marginal causal contribution. We evaluate on MATH and GSM8K.

items.LL: We vary the aggressiveness of near-duplicate removal during corpus preparation, sweeping the MinHash similarity threshold and the minimum duplication count required before a sequence is filtered. At each setting we train a language model and measure verbatim memorization using extraction and prompted-continuation tests, alongside downstream task accuracy and validation perplexity. Plotting memorization against quality across the sweep characterizes the tradeoff and locates an operating point that reduces memorization at limited quality cost. The pipeline reuses standard suffix-array and MinHash deduplication tooling. All runs use a matched compute budget.
items.LH: We vary the aggressiveness of near-duplicate removal, sweeping the MinHash similarity threshold and the minimum duplication count required before a sequence is filtered. At each setting we train a language model and measure verbatim memorization using extraction and prompted-continuation tests, alongside downstream task accuracy and validation perplexity. Plotting memorization against quality across the sweep characterizes the tradeoff and locates an operating point that reduces memorization at little quality cost, using standard suffix-array and MinHash tooling. All runs use a matched compute budget.
items.HL: We deduplicate by matching the training set's sequence-frequency distribution to a reference natural-frequency distribution estimated from an independent held-out corpus. Rather than filtering toward uniqueness, we downweight or remove sequences in proportion to their frequency in excess of the reference. Grouping near-duplicates with MinHash, we train a language model under this frequency-matched scheme and measure verbatim memorization using extraction and prompted-continuation tests, alongside downstream task accuracy and validation perplexity. We test whether memorization tracks excess-over-reference frequency rather than raw duplication count, and whether deduplicating naturally frequent sequences to uniqueness degrades quality. All runs use a matched compute budget.
items.HH: We deduplicate by matching the training set's sequence-frequency distribution to a reference natural-frequency distribution estimated from an independent held-out corpus. Rather than filtering toward uniqueness, we downweight or remove sequences in proportion to their frequency in excess of the reference. Grouping near-duplicates with MinHash, we train a language model under this frequency-matched scheme and measure verbatim memorization using extraction and prompted-continuation tests, alongside downstream task accuracy and validation perplexity. We test whether memorization tracks excess-over-reference frequency rather than raw duplication count, and whether deduplicating naturally frequent sequences to uniqueness degrades quality. All runs use a matched compute budget.

items.LL: We add a coarse-to-fine schedule to the multiresolution hash encoding of a grid-based NeRF. At the start of optimization only the coarsest levels are active, and finer levels are introduced one at a time through per-level gating weights that ramp from zero to one over a fixed number of iterations. Low-frequency geometry is therefore fitted before high-frequency detail. The hash encoding, feature table, and tiny MLP are unchanged, and total capacity is preserved. On standard scenes we observe fewer early-training floaters and more stable convergence at matched final quality.
items.LH: We add a coarse-to-fine schedule to the multiresolution hash encoding of a grid-based NeRF. At the start of optimization only the coarsest levels are active, and finer levels are introduced one at a time through per-level gating weights that ramp from zero to one over a fixed number of iterations. Low-frequency geometry is therefore fitted before high-frequency detail. The hash encoding, feature table, and tiny MLP are unchanged, and total capacity is preserved. On standard scenes this change yields fewer early-training floaters and more stable convergence at matched final quality.
items.HL: We replace the fixed spatial hash that maps grid vertices to feature-table entries with a learned soft assignment. Each vertex holds a differentiable distribution over table entries, optimized jointly with the features, so that vertices with similar radiance share entries while dissimilar vertices are separated. Collisions then act as a learned vector-quantization code over space rather than as position-determined noise absorbed by the tiny MLP. Table capacity is allocated by content statistics during optimization instead of by a fixed hashing function. On standard scenes this matches reconstruction quality using a smaller feature table.
items.HH: We replace the fixed spatial hash that maps grid vertices to feature-table entries with a learned soft assignment. Each vertex holds a differentiable distribution over table entries, optimized jointly with the features, so that vertices with similar radiance share entries while dissimilar vertices are separated. Collisions then act as a learned vector-quantization code over space rather than as position-determined noise absorbed by the tiny MLP. Table capacity is allocated by content statistics during optimization instead of by a fixed hashing function. On standard scenes this matches reconstruction quality using a smaller feature table.

items.LL: We measure how the performance gap between gradient-boosted decision trees and deep networks changes with training-set size. Using the datasets and evaluation protocol of an existing tabular benchmark, each dataset is subsampled at several fractions of its rows. At each fraction, both model families are retuned under matched hyperparameter-search budgets, so tuning effort is held constant. We aggregate the resulting gap-versus-sample-size curves across datasets and report the sample sizes at which the tree advantage narrows. The benchmark's original metrics and cross-validation splits are used throughout, so only training-set size varies.
items.LH: We measure how the performance gap between gradient-boosted decision trees and deep networks changes with training-set size. Using the datasets and evaluation protocol of an existing tabular benchmark, each dataset is subsampled at several fractions of its rows. At each fraction, both model families are retuned under matched hyperparameter-search budgets. We aggregate the resulting gap-versus-sample-size curves across datasets and report the sample sizes at which the tree advantage narrows. The benchmark's original metrics and splits are held fixed.
items.HL: We define a dataset-level scalar that estimates the irregularity of the target function under input rotations. It is computed before any model is trained, by fitting local slopes along many randomly rotated directions and measuring how much those slopes vary across directions. We test whether this single statistic predicts the signed performance gap between gradient-boosted decision trees and deep networks across the benchmark datasets. We then fit an orthogonal input transform that reduces the statistic, and check whether applying it shifts each dataset's gap toward the deep-network-favorable regime.
items.HH: We define a dataset-level scalar, computed before training, that estimates the irregularity of the target function under input rotations, by fitting local slopes along many randomly rotated directions and measuring how much those slopes vary. We test whether it predicts the signed performance gap between gradient-boosted decision trees and deep networks across the benchmark. We then fit an orthogonal transform that reduces the statistic and check whether it shifts each dataset toward the deep-network-favorable regime.

items.LL: We extend the contiguous-county-pair design of Dube, Lester, and Reich to the 2010-2019 period, during which many states enacted sizable statutory minimum-wage increases. Using Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages data for the food-service sector, we estimate disemployment elasticities separately for the extensive margin (establishment headcount) and the intensive margin (average hours and earnings per worker). County-pair-by-period fixed effects absorb local shocks common to adjacent counties, following the original specification. We additionally test whether estimated elasticities vary with the magnitude of the statutory increase across pairs.
items.LH: We apply the contiguous-county-pair design to the 2010-2019 wave of large statutory minimum-wage increases. Using Quarterly Census of Employment and Wages data for food services, we estimate disemployment elasticities separately for establishment headcount and for average hours and earnings per worker. County-pair-by-period fixed effects absorb common local shocks. We further test whether elasticities vary with the magnitude of the statutory increase across pairs.
items.HL: We reconsider the contiguous-county-pair null by treating the paired control county as partially treated, since cross-border commuting and firm location choices can reallocate employment across the state boundary. Using bilateral county-to-county commuting flows from LEHD Origin-Destination Employment Statistics to measure exposure, we model each border region as one integrated labor market and estimate gross cross-border reallocation of food-service jobs alongside net employment. In this framework the standard paired estimator is attenuated by construction, because jobs displaced on the treated side may reappear in the control county; we identify reallocation from asymmetric commuting exposure between the counties.
items.HH: We reconsider the contiguous-county-pair null with a framing in which the paired control county is itself partially treated, because cross-border commuting and firm location choices reallocate employment across the boundary. Using bilateral commuting flows from LEHD Origin-Destination Employment Statistics, we model each border region as one integrated labor market and estimate gross cross-border reallocation of food-service jobs alongside net employment. The standard estimator is attenuated by construction; we identify reallocation from asymmetric commuting exposure.

items.LL: We estimate the firm-level labor supply elasticity separately by worker age and tenure groups. Using linked employer-employee data, we apply the standard separations-and-recruits estimator from the dynamic monopsony literature, recovering the elasticity from how quit and hire flows respond to wage variation within each group. From the group-specific elasticities we compute the implied wage markdowns and document how monopsony exposure varies across the workforce. We test whether younger and shorter-tenure workers, who have higher baseline mobility, face more elastic firm labor supply. The exercise extends existing elasticity estimates along the age and tenure dimension.
items.LH: We estimate the firm-level labor supply elasticity separately by worker age and tenure. Using linked employer-employee data, we apply the separations-and-recruits estimator, recovering the elasticity from how quit and hire flows respond to wages within each group. From the implied markdowns we document how monopsony exposure is distributed across workers. We test whether younger, shorter-tenure workers, who have higher mobility, face a different elasticity, relative to the assumption of a single representative parameter.
items.HL: We model the firm-level labor supply elasticity as an equilibrium object that firms partly determine, rather than a fixed parameter set by search frictions. Firms invest in managing workers' information about outside options, through pay secrecy, opaque pay structures, and non-compete clauses, which reduces the elasticity of labor supply they face and widens their wage markdown. Using linked employer-employee data, we relate firm-level elasticity estimates to measures of pay opacity and non-compete use. We then test whether exogenous transparency shocks raise the elasticity, treating a firm's monopsony power as partly a produced outcome of its information strategy.
items.HH: We model the firm-level labor supply elasticity as an equilibrium object that firms themselves determine, rather than a fixed parameter set by search frictions. By managing workers' information about outside options, through pay secrecy, opaque pay structures, and non-compete clauses, firms suppress the elasticity they face and widen their markdown. Using linked employer-employee data, we relate firm-level elasticities to pay opacity and non-compete use. We test whether exogenous transparency shocks raise the elasticity, treating monopsony power as a produced outcome.

items.LL: This study re-estimates the instrumental-variable return to a college degree using quarter-of-birth and compulsory-schooling instruments on a more recent census cohort than earlier work. Using the same instrument set, it reports the first-stage strength and the local average treatment effect, and disaggregates both by region and by sex. The instrumental-variable estimates are compared with ordinary least squares within the sample. The analysis documents how the estimated return and the composition of the complier subpopulation differ across cohorts and subgroups.
items.LH: This study re-estimates the instrumental-variable return to a college degree using quarter-of-birth instruments on a more recent census cohort. Using the same instrument set, it reports first-stage strength and the local average treatment effect, disaggregating both by region and by sex, and compares the estimates against ordinary least squares. It documents how the estimated return and the complier subpopulation differ across cohorts.
items.HL: This study identifies separately the earnings return to holding a college credential and the return to the underlying years of instruction. It combines two instruments acting on different margins: a compulsory-attendance instrument that shifts years of study and a school-entry-age discontinuity that shifts on-time degree completion. From the overidentified system, it recovers a return to the credential and a return to years. The contrast between the two serves as a test of whether the college earnings premium reflects human-capital accumulation or credential signaling.
items.HH: This study separately identifies the return to holding a college credential and the return to the underlying years of instruction. It combines two instruments acting on different margins: a compulsory-attendance instrument shifting years of study and a school-entry-age discontinuity shifting on-time degree completion. From the overidentified system it recovers both returns, and their contrast provides a test of whether the college premium reflects human capital or credential signaling.

items.LL: This paper estimates how the effect of non-compete enforceability on worker mobility varies across the earnings distribution. Building on studies that exploit state-level differences in enforceability, we interact a standard enforceability index with worker earnings deciles in a difference-in-differences design using matched employer-employee administrative records. Recent state-level changes in enforceability provide additional treatment events. We estimate effects on job-to-job transition rates and post-move wage growth, and report whether lower-earning workers show larger mobility responses than higher-earning workers. The approach follows existing identification strategies and extends them to distributional heterogeneity.
items.LH: This paper estimates how enforceability shapes worker mobility across the earnings distribution.
