We interact an enforceability index with worker earnings deciles in a difference-in-differences design using matched employer-employee administrative records, with recent state-level changes providing treatment events. We estimate effects on job-to-job transition rates and post-move wage growth, asking whether lower-earning workers show larger mobility responses. The analysis addresses distributional heterogeneity in the effect of enforceability.
items.HL: This paper defines the causal object as workers' perceived enforceability rather than statutory enforceability, treating the legal regime as a noisy proxy for beliefs that also depend on employer signaling and local legal culture. Using matched employer-employee data, we elicit workers' subjective enforceability beliefs and run an information intervention that corrects those beliefs while holding the statutory regime fixed. We estimate whether belief corrections change job-to-job transition rates and test whether mobility responds to perceived rather than actual enforceability, including among workers whose contracts are legally unenforceable.
items.HH: This paper defines the causal object as workers' perceived enforceability rather than statutory enforceability, with the legal regime a noisy proxy for beliefs shaped by employer signaling and local legal culture. Using matched employer-employee data, we elicit subjective beliefs and run an information intervention that corrects them while holding the statute fixed. We test whether belief corrections move job-to-job transitions and whether mobility tracks perceived rather than actual enforceability, even where contracts are legally unenforceable.

items.LL: This paper extends the commuting-zone robot-exposure design of Acemoglu and Restrepo (2020) by adding a second automation input to the exposure index. Alongside industrial robots, we measure adoption of computer-numerically-controlled and other dedicated automation machinery from industry-level capital data and allocate it to commuting zones using baseline industry composition. We re-estimate employment-to-population and wage effects with the combined exposure measure and the same foreign-adoption instrument, and we report heterogeneity across worker education groups. We assess whether the broadened measure yields elasticities consistent in sign and magnitude with the robot-only estimates.
items.LH: This paper augments the commuting-zone design of Acemoglu and Restrepo (2020) with a second automation input: adoption of computer-numerically-controlled and dedicated automation machinery from industry capital data, allocated via baseline industry composition. We re-estimate employment-to-population and wage effects with the combined measure and the same foreign-adoption instrument, and report heterogeneity across education groups. We assess whether these elasticities align with the robot-only estimates.
items.HL: This paper studies how local labor markets adjust to automation by defining exposure from the ex ante task-automatability of each commuting zone's baseline industry and occupation mix, independent of realized robot installations. Building on Acemoglu and Restrepo (2020), we test whether employment-to-population and wage adjustment responds to this anticipated automatability, and whether local reallocation of labor and capital occurs in advance of, and separately from, actual robot adoption. We treat forward-looking responses to expected automation as the operative mechanism, and compare the timing and magnitude of adjustment against those predicted by contemporaneous realized robot exposure.
items.HH: Building on Acemoglu and Restrepo (2020), this paper defines exposure from the ex ante task-automatability of each commuting zone's baseline industry and occupation mix and asks whether adjustment responds to anticipated automatability rather than actual adoption. We test whether labor and capital reallocate in advance of, and separately from, robot installation, treating forward-looking expectations as the operative mechanism, and contrast this timing against contemporaneous realized exposure.

items.LL: We extend the event-study child-penalty decomposition by estimating penalties separately across mothers' pre-birth education, occupation, and industry groups. Using the standard specification around first birth, we decompose earnings effects into participation, hours, and the hourly wage rate. Linked employer-employee data let us divide the wage-rate component into between-firm transitions toward lower-paying or more flexible employers and within-firm pay changes at the same employer. We report how the relative weight of these margins varies across skill and occupation groups and how it evolves over a longer post-birth horizon. Estimates use administrative panel data.
items.LH: We estimate child penalties separately across mothers' pre-birth education, occupation, and industry groups. Using the standard specification around first birth, we decompose earnings into participation, hours, and the hourly wage rate. Linked employer-employee data let us split the wage-rate component into between-firm transitions toward lower-paying or flexible employers and within-firm pay changes at the same employer. We report how these margins vary across skill groups and over a longer horizon. Estimates use administrative panel data.
items.HL: We model the earnings divergence around first birth as partly the realized value of decisions made before parenthood. Women anticipating children may sort in advance into occupations, employers, and human-capital tracks with flatter wage growth. To separate this anticipatory sorting from the post-birth causal effect, we use exogenous variation in fertility realization and timing: unplanned births, IVF success versus failure, and biological fecundity differences. This lets us reallocate part of the measured penalty to pre-parenthood choices and to firms that price expected childbearing into early wage trajectories. Estimates use administrative panel data.
items.HH: We model the earnings divergence around first birth as partly the realized value of decisions made before parenthood. Women anticipating children sort in advance into occupations, employers, and tracks with flatter wage growth. We exploit exogenous variation in fertility — unplanned births, IVF success versus failure, and biological fecundity differences — to separate anticipatory sorting from the post-birth effect. This reallocates the penalty to pre-parenthood choices and to firms pricing expected childbearing. Estimates use administrative panel data.

items.LL: We extend the production-function approach to markup estimation by replacing the Cobb-Douglas specification with a translog production function. This allows the output elasticity of the variable input to differ across firms and over time within each industry, rather than being fixed by industry. Firm-level markups are computed as the ratio of the firm-and-year-specific output elasticity to the input's revenue share. We then re-derive the aggregate markup series and its reallocation decomposition, and compare the level and trend against the Cobb-Douglas benchmark to gauge sensitivity to the functional-form restriction.
items.LH: We estimate markups in the production-function approach by replacing Cobb-Douglas with a translog production function. This allows the variable input's output elasticity to vary across firms and over time rather than remaining fixed by industry. Markups are computed as the ratio of the firm-and-year-specific elasticity to the revenue share. We then re-derive the aggregate markup series and its reallocation decomposition, comparing against the Cobb-Douglas benchmark to gauge sensitivity to the functional-form restriction.
items.HL: We relax the assumption of competitive input markets in the production-function approach to markup estimation. The variable-input first-order condition is treated as identifying the product of the output markup and an input-side markdown, rather than the markup alone. We add a moment based on the firm-specific input-supply elasticity, estimated from variation in local input availability and firm-level input-supply shifters, to separately identify the markdown. Markups are then recovered net of buyer power, and the elasticity-to-revenue-share wedge is decomposed into a selling-side and a buying-side component at the firm level.
items.HH: We relax the assumption of competitive input markets in the production-function approach to markup estimation. The variable-input first-order condition identifies not the markup alone but the product of the output markup and an input-side markdown. We add a moment based on the firm-specific input-supply elasticity — estimated from variation in local input availability and firm-level input-supply shifters — to separately identify the markdown. Markups are recovered net of buyer power, and the elasticity-to-revenue-share wedge is decomposed into selling-side and buying-side components.

items.LL: We extend the standard merger-simulation pipeline by adding micro-moments built from consumer survey data on second choices to the demand estimation step. These moments discipline diversion ratios directly, rather than relying only on aggregate price variation, and are appended to the generalized method of moments objective alongside the usual share conditions and price instruments. After re-estimating the random-coefficients logit demand, we compute unilateral price effects with the standard static Bertrand-Nash simulation. We report how the tighter identification of substitution changes predicted price increases and their standard errors across a set of candidate mergers.
items.LH: We add micro-moments built from consumer survey data on second choices to merger simulation. These moments discipline diversion ratios directly, and are appended to the generalized method of moments objective alongside the usual share conditions and price instruments. After re-estimating the random-coefficients logit demand, we compute unilateral price effects with static Bertrand-Nash simulation. We report how this tighter identification of substitution changes predicted price increases and their standard errors across candidate mergers.
items.HL: We model diversion as an equilibrium outcome of consumer consideration-set formation rather than a fixed ratio of estimated demand elasticities. Consumers evaluate only products in a limited consideration set, and the probability a product is considered depends on firm choices such as shelf placement and advertising allocation. Because a merged firm reallocates these consideration-shaping investments across its combined portfolio, effective diversion changes with the merger itself. The simulation jointly solves for prices and consideration-shaping decisions, so predicted unilateral price effects depend on how consolidation reshapes what consumers consider, not only on pre-merger elasticities.
items.HH: We model diversion as an equilibrium outcome of consumer consideration-set formation rather than a fixed ratio of estimated elasticities. Consumers evaluate only products in a limited consideration set, whose membership depends on firm choices such as shelf placement and advertising allocation. Because a merged firm reallocates these consideration-shaping investments across its combined portfolio, effective diversion shifts with the merger itself. Our simulation jointly solves prices and consideration-shaping decisions, so unilateral price effects hinge on how consolidation reshapes what consumers consider, not merely on pre-merger elasticities.

items.LL: This paper adds a life-cycle structure to the two-asset HANK model of Kaplan, Moll, and Violante (2018). Overlapping generations replace the perpetual-youth assumption, and households age deterministically while facing age-varying earnings and mortality. Liquid and illiquid holdings, portfolio shares, and marginal propensities to consume are allowed to vary over the life cycle, with the model calibrated to age-specific wealth and consumption data. We decompose the direct and indirect components of monetary transmission by age and express the aggregate response as a weighted average over the population's age distribution, assessing how demographic composition affects the strength of the indirect channel.
items.LH: This paper adds a life-cycle structure to the two-asset HANK model of Kaplan, Moll, and Violante (2018). Overlapping generations replace the perpetual-youth assumption, and households age deterministically while facing age-varying earnings and mortality. Liquid and illiquid holdings, portfolio shares, and marginal propensities to consume are allowed to vary over the life cycle, with the model calibrated to age-specific wealth and consumption data. We decompose the direct and indirect components of monetary transmission by age and express the aggregate response as a weighted average over the population's age distribution, assessing how demographic composition affects the strength of the indirect channel.
items.HL: This paper makes the effective liquidity of the illiquid asset endogenous to the monetary-policy stance in the two-asset HANK model of Kaplan, Moll, and Violante (2018). The transaction cost governing illiquid-asset adjustment depends on aggregate liquidity conditions and the interest-rate environment rather than being a fixed parameter. Because this cost determines which households are wealthy hand-to-mouth, the boundary of the high-MPC region shifts with policy: accommodative policy lowers the cost and moves households out of the constrained region, while contractionary policy raises it. The marginal-propensity-to-consume distribution thus becomes a function of the policy path, yielding state-dependent and potentially asymmetric transmission.
items.HH: This paper makes the effective liquidity of the illiquid asset endogenous to the monetary-policy stance in the two-asset HANK model of Kaplan, Moll, and Violante (2018). The transaction cost governing illiquid-asset adjustment depends on aggregate liquidity conditions and the interest-rate environment rather than being fixed. Because this cost determines which households are wealthy hand-to-mouth, the boundary of the high-MPC region shifts with policy: accommodative policy lowers the cost and moves households out of the constrained region, while contractionary policy raises it. The marginal-propensity-to-consume distribution becomes a function of the policy path, producing state-dependent and potentially asymmetric transmission.

items.LL: This project extends the rebate-timing design used to estimate the marginal propensity to consume out of transitory income. Using the randomized timing of rebate disbursement for identification, we estimate the MPC within finer household strata, defined by liquid-asset quartile interacted with income tercile, and broaden the outcome measure beyond nondurable goods to include selected durables and services. The analysis retains the buffer-stock and liquidity-constraint interpretation of the original studies. We then test whether the concentration of spending among low-liquidity households persists across the expanded strata and consumption categories.
items.LH: This project extends the rebate-timing design used to estimate the marginal propensity to consume out of transitory income. Using the randomized timing of rebate disbursement for identification, we estimate the MPC within finer household strata, defined by liquid-asset quartile interacted with income tercile, and broaden the outcome measure beyond nondurable goods to include selected durables and services. We retain the buffer-stock and liquidity-constraint interpretation while testing whether the concentration of spending among low-liquidity households persists across the expanded strata and categories.
items.HL: This project reconsiders what the marginal propensity to consume out of a rebate measures. Rather than treating the rebate as a shock pre-classified as transitory, we model the spending response as an outcome of household belief-updating about income persistence. We pair the rebate variation with elicited or experimentally varied subjective beliefs about how persistent the household's own income process is, and model consumption as responding to the household's posterior over persistence. The framework implies that identically sized transfers produce different MPCs depending on the beliefs they induce, so the transitory/permanent partition is treated as endogenous rather than fixed.
items.HH: This project reconsiders what the marginal propensity to consume out of a rebate measures. Rather than treating the rebate as a shock pre-classified as transitory, we model the spending response as an outcome of household belief-updating about income persistence. We pair the rebate variation with elicited or experimentally varied subjective beliefs about how persistent the household's own income process is, and model consumption as responding to the household's posterior over persistence. Identically sized transfers are predicted to produce different MPCs depending on the beliefs they induce, so the transitory/permanent partition is treated as endogenous rather than fixed.

items.LL: We extend the hybrid New Keynesian Phillips curve to incorporate staggered nominal wage setting alongside price setting. Wages reset under a constant Calvo probability, and a fraction of wage setters follow a backward-looking indexation rule symmetric to the rule-of-thumb price setters. This yields a hybrid wage Phillips curve in which wage inflation depends on expected and lagged wage inflation and the gap between the marginal rate of substitution and the real wage. The price and wage equations are estimated jointly on aggregate data, adding one constant wage-adjustment-frequency parameter to the standard specification.
items.LH: We extend the hybrid New Keynesian Phillips curve to incorporate staggered nominal wage setting alongside price setting. Wages reset under a constant Calvo probability, and a fraction of wage setters follow a backward-looking indexation rule symmetric to the rule-of-thumb price setters. This yields a hybrid wage Phillips curve in which wage inflation depends on expected and lagged wage inflation and the gap between the marginal rate of substitution and the real wage. The price and wage equations are estimated jointly on aggregate data, adding one constant wage-adjustment-frequency parameter to the standard specification.
items.HL: We replace the two exogenous constant-hazard adjustment processes with a single firm-level information-processing constraint that jointly governs wage and price rigidity. Each firm allocates a fixed attention capacity between monitoring cost and productivity conditions relevant to its price and labor-market conditions relevant to the wages it posts, so the timing and size of wage and price adjustments are co-determined and respond to the volatility of aggregate shocks. Rigidity is state-dependent and correlated across wages and prices rather than fixed and separable, implying a Phillips curve slope that varies with the volatility regime.
items.HH: We replace the two exogenous constant-hazard adjustment processes with a single firm-level information-processing constraint that jointly governs wage and price rigidity. Each firm allocates a fixed attention capacity between monitoring cost and productivity conditions relevant to its price and labor-market conditions relevant to the wages it posts, so the timing and size of wage and price adjustments are co-determined and respond to the volatility of aggregate shocks. Rigidity is state-dependent and correlated across wages and prices, implying a Phillips curve slope that varies with the volatility regime rather than remaining constant.

items.LL: We extend high-frequency identification of monetary policy shocks by widening the instrument set used within the narrow FOMC announcement window. Alongside the short-rate futures used in existing measures, we include longer-maturity Treasury futures at the 5- and 10-year points. We re-estimate the factor structure on this wider cross-section and test whether a third factor is needed beyond the target and path factors. The resulting surprises are then used as external instruments in a proxy vector autoregression, and the estimated macroeconomic responses are compared with those from the original narrower instrument set.
items.LH: We extend high-frequency identification of monetary policy shocks by widening the instrument set used within the narrow FOMC announcement window. We add longer-maturity 5- and 10-year Treasury futures to the usual short-rate futures and re-estimate the factor structure, testing whether a third factor is needed beyond the target and path factors. The resulting surprises are used as external instruments in a proxy vector autoregression, and the estimated macroeconomic responses are compared with those from the original narrower instrument set.
items.HL: We modify high-frequency identification by constructing the FOMC-window surprise from the option-implied risk-neutral distribution of future short rates, using interest-rate options and swaptions, rather than from point prices alone. We decompose the announcement response into a first-moment component, the change in the expected rate, and a second-moment component, the change in the dispersion of the distribution. The second-moment component is treated as a distinct monetary-policy uncertainty shock. Both the level shock and the uncertainty shock are used as separate external instruments in a proxy vector autoregression to estimate their macroeconomic effects.
items.HH: We modify high-frequency identification by constructing the FOMC-window surprise from the option-implied risk-neutral distribution of future short rates, using interest-rate options and swaptions, rather than from point prices alone. We decompose the announcement response into a first-moment component, the change in the expected rate, and a second-moment component, the change in the dispersion of the distribution. The second-moment component is treated as a distinct monetary-policy uncertainty shock. Both the level shock and the uncertainty shock are used as separate external instruments in a proxy vector autoregression to estimate their macroeconomic effects.

items.LL: We consider student-proposing deferred acceptance under two tie-breaking regimes for students in the same priority class. In the first, ties at every school are broken by a single common lottery draw per student; in the second, an independent lottery is drawn at each school. The matching algorithm is unchanged. We compare the regimes on strategy-proofness, which both preserve, and on student welfare and the fraction of students assigned to a top-k choice. Using preference data from one district under standard priority structures, we report the differences in assignment outcomes between the single and multiple tie-breaker rules.
items.LH: We consider student-proposing deferred acceptance under two tie-breaking regimes for students in the same priority class: a single common lottery draw per student, and an independent lottery drawn at each school. The matching algorithm is unchanged. Both regimes preserve strategy-proofness, and we compare them on student welfare and the fraction of students assigned to a top-k choice. Using district preference data, we report the differences in assignment outcomes between the single and multiple tie-breaker rules.
items.HL: We treat each school's priority ordering as an instrument chosen by the designer rather than a fixed input. Priorities are selected from an admissible class that respects hard legal constraints, such as sibling and disability protections, to optimize a district-level distributional objective; the matching is then computed by student-proposing deferred acceptance over those priorities. Because students cannot affect how priorities are set, student strategy-proofness is retained. Stability is defined relative to the designed priorities. We characterize which distributional objectives, such as socioeconomic integration, are attainable this way and report the resulting outcomes under this priority-design approach.
items.HH: We treat each school's priority ordering as an instrument chosen by the designer rather than a fixed input. Priorities are selected from an admissible class that respects hard legal constraints, such as sibling and disability protections, to optimize a district-level distributional objective, with the matching then computed by student-proposing deferred acceptance over those priorities. Because students cannot affect how priorities are set, strategy-proofness is retained. We characterize which distributional objectives, such as socioeconomic integration, are attainable this way and report the resulting outcomes under this priority-design approach.

items.LL: We extend the uniform reserve price in GSP sponsored-search auctions to segment-specific reserves. Queries are partitioned into segments by observable features (category, device, time of day), and each segment's value distribution is estimated from historical bids. Within a segment, the reserve is set at the Myersonian point where the estimated virtual valuation equals zero, holding the independent-private-values assumption and the GSP allocation rule fixed. We evaluate the revenue difference relative to a single global reserve using the locally envy-free equilibrium, and quantify the estimation error arising from finite bid samples.
items.LH: We consider segment-specific reserves for GSP sponsored-search auctions in place of a single global reserve. Queries are partitioned by observable features (category, device, time of day), and each segment's value distribution is estimated from historical bids. Within a segment, the reserve is set at the Myersonian virtual-valuation-zero point, holding independent private values and the GSP rule fixed. Using the locally envy-free equilibrium, we evaluate the revenue difference relative to a global reserve, and quantify the finite-sample estimation error.
items.HL: We treat the reserve price in GSP sponsored-search auctions as a dynamic policy rather than a static price. In a repeated interaction, advertisers anticipate that current bids inform the platform's future reserve estimates and shade their bids, so the value distribution the platform infers is endogenous to its reserve policy. We model reserve setting as the equilibrium of a dynamic game and derive the optimal policy under dynamic incentive-compatibility constraints, with forward-looking bidders internalizing the ratchet effect. The resulting long-run revenue-maximizing reserve differs from the per-period Myersonian reserve computed from observed bids.
items.HH: We treat the reserve price in GSP sponsored-search auctions as a dynamic policy rather than a static price. Advertisers anticipate that current bids inform future reserve estimates and shade their bids, so the value distribution the platform infers is endogenous to its own reserve policy. We model reserve setting as a dynamic-game equilibrium and derive the optimal policy under dynamic incentive-compatibility constraints, with forward-looking bidders internalizing the ratchet effect. The long-run revenue-maximizing reserve differs from the per-period Myersonian reserve.

items.LL: We consider the static kidney-exchange model with 2- and 3-way cycle constraints and replace its binary compatible/incompatible preference with a graded compatibility score for each feasible patient-donor pairing. The score reflects the degree of HLA mismatch and serves as a proxy for expected graft survival. We then compute the matching that maximizes the total compatibility score over the pool, subject to the same feasibility and cycle-length constraints as the original mechanism. Using simulated pools calibrated to blood-type and HLA-frequency distributions, we compare the resulting match quality and match rate against the standard compatibility-count objective.
items.LH: We replace the binary compatibility preference in the static kidney-exchange model with a graded compatibility score for each feasible patient-donor pairing, reflecting the degree of HLA mismatch as a proxy for expected graft survival. We then maximize the total compatibility score subject to the same 2- and 3-way cycle-length constraints. On simulated pools calibrated to blood-type and HLA-frequency distributions, we compare the resulting match quality and match rate against the compatibility-count objective.
items.HL: We recast the static single-round clearing as a dynamic problem in which patient-donor pairs enter and exit the pool stochastically over time, and the mechanism repeatedly decides whether to execute an available exchange now or defer it to let the pool thicken. We define welfare as discounted match value net of the mortality and dropout hazard pairs face while waiting, and characterize the matching-versus-waiting policy that maximizes it under a Markovian arrival model, retaining the 2- and 3-way cycle-length constraints. On simulated pools calibrated to blood-type and HLA-frequency distributions, we compare this policy against immediate greedy clearing.
items.HH: We frame kidney exchange, previously cleared as a fixed pool in a single round, as a dynamic problem in which pairs enter and exit stochastically, and the mechanism repeatedly decides whether to execute an available exchange now or defer it to let the pool thicken. We define welfare as discounted match value net of the mortality and dropout hazard pairs face while waiting, and characterize the matching-versus-waiting policy that maximizes it under a Markovian arrival model, retaining the 2- and 3-way cycle-length constraints. On simulated pools calibrated to blood-type and HLA-frequency distributions, this policy differs from immediate greedy clearing.

items.LL: We extend the combinatorial-clock auction with a usage-based congestion charge. Licenses are allocated through the existing clock phase, supplementary-bids round, and core-selecting payment rule. After allocation, each license-holder pays an ex-post per-MHz-population fee in markets whose measured utilization exceeds a set threshold; the fee schedule is calibrated administratively from observed traffic. The congestion charge does not enter the bid language or the clock's price discovery, so the auction's incentive and computational properties are preserved. We evaluate the fee's effect on peak loading using utilization data from cleared bands.
items.LH: We add a usage-based congestion charge to the combinatorial-clock auction. Licenses are allocated through the existing clock phase, supplementary-bids round, and core-selecting payment rule. After allocation, each license-holder pays an ex-post per-MHz-population fee in markets whose measured utilization exceeds a threshold; the fee schedule is calibrated administratively from observed traffic. The charge does not enter the bid language or price discovery, and preserves the auction's incentive and computational properties. We evaluate its effect on peak loading.
items.HL: We redefine the auction's object from exclusive license packages to congestion-priority packages. Bidders submit package bids over access-priority tiers indexed by time-location states of a shared band. The deferred-acceptance clock ascends on congestion shadow prices rather than exclusion prices, and payments equal each bidder's integrated marginal congestion externality. Allocation clears continuously as demand realizes rather than once at auction, so the discovered clock price serves directly as the congestion price. We retain the deferred-acceptance structure to preserve strategy-proofness and tractability, and evaluate clearing and revenue against a static-license benchmark.
items.HH: We define the auction's object as congestion-priority packages rather than exclusive license packages. Bidders submit package bids over access-priority tiers indexed by time-location states of a shared band. The deferred-acceptance clock ascends on congestion shadow prices rather than exclusion prices, and payments equal each bidder's integrated marginal congestion externality. Allocation clears continuously as demand realizes rather than once, so the discovered clock price is itself the congestion price. We retain the deferred-acceptance structure to preserve strategy-proofness and tractability, and evaluate clearing and revenue against a static-license benchmark.

items.LL: We estimate group-time average treatment effects ATT(g,t) using the doubly-robust moment that pairs an outcome-regression model for untreated outcomes with a propensity model for cohort membership, and we fit both nuisance functions with cross-fitted machine-learning learners to accommodate high-dimensional covariates. The ATT(g,t) are aggregated into event-study and overall summaries following the standard framework, and simultaneous confidence bands are obtained from a cluster-robust multiplier bootstrap. Conditional parallel trends, no anticipation, and valid not-yet-treated or never-treated comparison groups are maintained; the contribution is the nuisance estimation and inference procedure.
items.LH: We estimate group-time average treatment effects ATT(g,t) for staggered difference-in-differences using the doubly-robust moment pairing an untreated-outcome regression with a cohort-membership propensity model, fitting both nuisance functions with cross-fitted machine-learning learners. This accommodates high-dimensional covariates. The ATT(g,t) are aggregated into event-study and overall summaries, with simultaneous confidence bands from a cluster-robust multiplier bootstrap. Conditional parallel trends, no anticipation, and valid comparison groups are maintained.
items.HL: We model each unit's adoption date as the outcome of a latent duration process whose hazard depends on observed covariates and on a unit-specific factor allowed to correlate with the size of that unit's own treatment effect. Group-time treatment effects are identified by augmenting the comparison-group moment with a selection-correction (control-function) term constructed from the estimated adoption hazard, keeping the estimator consistent when units time adoption in response to anticipated gains. This replaces the assumption that not-yet-treated units are valid controls under conditional parallel trends with an explicit model of endogenous timing; the standard estimator is recovered when the correction term is zero.
items.HH: For staggered difference-in-differences, we model each unit's adoption date as a latent duration process whose hazard depends on covariates and on a unit-specific factor allowed to correlate with the size of that unit's own treatment effect. Group-time effects are identified by augmenting the comparison-group moment with a selection-correction (control-function) term built from the estimated adoption hazard, so that the estimator stays consistent even when units time adoption in response to anticipated gains. This replaces conditional parallel trends with an explicit model of endogenous timing, recovering the standard estimator only as the case where the correction vanishes.

items.LL: We consider variance estimation for robust bias-corrected regression-discontinuity inference. In place of the plug-in analytic variance, we compute a wild-bootstrap variance over the same local-polynomial fits, resampling residuals separately on each side of the cutoff to approximate the sampling distribution of the bias-corrected estimator. Confidence intervals follow from bootstrap quantiles or the bootstrap variance. The mean-squared-error-optimal bandwidth, the polynomial order, and the leading-bias correction are unchanged. We assess coverage in small samples and under heteroskedasticity, settings where the analytic variance can be inaccurate.
items.LH: We consider variance estimation for robust bias-corrected RD inference. In place of the plug-in analytic variance, we compute a wild-bootstrap variance over the same local-polynomial fits, resampling residuals separately on each side of the cutoff to recover the bias-corrected estimator's sampling distribution. Confidence intervals follow from bootstrap quantiles or the bootstrap variance, while the bandwidth, polynomial order, and bias correction are left unchanged. We assess coverage in small samples and under heteroskedasticity, where the analytic variance can be inaccurate.
items.HL: We consider choosing the RD bandwidth and bias correction to minimize the expected regret of the decision the estimate informs, rather than the estimator's mean squared error or coverage error. Given a loss function over a binary policy action triggered when the effect crosses a cost threshold, the bias-variance trade-off is re-weighted asymmetrically: near the decision boundary, bias that would flip the action is penalized more than variance; far from it, wider bandwidths are tolerated. Bandwidth, polynomial order, and bias correction are jointly selected to minimize estimated expected decision loss, so the tuning parameters depend on the policy context rather than the estimand alone.
items.HH: We choose the RD bandwidth and bias correction to minimize the expected regret of the decision the estimate informs, rather than the estimator's statistical accuracy. Given a loss over a binary policy action triggered when the effect crosses a cost threshold, the bias-variance trade-off is re-weighted asymmetrically: near the boundary, bias that would flip the action is penalized more than variance; far from it, wider bandwidths are tolerated. Bandwidth, polynomial order, and bias correction become functions of the policy context, not the estimand.

items.LL: We consider how the predictor-importance weights in the synthetic control method are selected. Rather than minimizing in-sample mean squared prediction error over the full pre-treatment window, we partition that window into a training block, used to solve for the donor weights, and a validation block, used to score prediction error. Predictor weights are chosen to minimize validation-period error. The donor-weight estimator, the convex-combination constraints, and the placebo permutation inference are left unchanged. Matching quality is thereby evaluated on held-out pre-treatment periods, which limits overfitting of the pre-treatment fit.
items.LH: We consider how the predictor-importance weights in the synthetic control method are selected. Rather than minimizing in-sample mean squared prediction error over the full pre-treatment window, we partition that window into a training block, used to solve for the donor weights, and a validation block, used to score prediction error, choosing predictor weights that minimize validation-period error. The donor-weight estimator, convex-combination constraints, and placebo inference are unchanged. This procedure concerns how these models are fit and how overfitting of the pre-treatment fit is addressed.
items.HL: We consider inference in the synthetic control method when the pre-treatment matching problem admits many donor-weight vectors that fit the treated unit almost equally well. Instead of reporting a single fitted counterfactual, we characterize the set of weight vectors achieving pre-treatment fit within a specified tolerance, map this set to the corresponding collection of post-treatment counterfactual trajectories, and report the induced interval of treatment effects. Inference targets this set, asking whether a hypothesized effect, such as zero, is consistent with at least one admissible synthetic control. The estimand is thus a set of counterfactual paths.
items.HH: We consider inference in the synthetic control method. When the pre-treatment matching problem admits many donor-weight vectors that fit the treated unit almost equally well, we characterize the set of weight vectors achieving pre-treatment fit within a specified tolerance, map this set to the corresponding post-treatment counterfactual trajectories, and report the induced interval of treatment effects. Inference targets this set, asking whether a hypothesized effect, such as zero, is consistent with at least one admissible synthetic control. Weight non-uniqueness is thus treated as the object of inference rather than as a nuisance.

items.LL: We refine the counterfactual step of the standard bunching estimator. The fixed-order polynomial fit to the earnings density away from the kink is replaced by a cross-validated flexible spline, and the width of the excluded region around the kink is set by an automated bandwidth rule rather than chosen by inspection. Model-selection and estimation uncertainty are carried into the compensated elasticity's standard errors via a bootstrap that resamples individuals and re-runs the full selection procedure. Elasticities are reported separately for demographic subgroups estimated through the same pipeline.
items.LH: We refine the counterfactual step of the bunching estimator. The fixed-order polynomial is replaced by a cross-validated flexible spline, and the excluded region's width is set by an automated bandwidth rule rather than by inspection. Model-selection and estimation uncertainty are carried into the compensated elasticity's standard errors through a bootstrap that resamples individuals and re-runs the full selection procedure. Subgroup elasticities are delivered from a single unified pipeline.
items.HL: We recast bunching as the stationary outcome of a dynamic adjustment process. Individuals with stochastic earnings pay state-dependent costs to relocate, so the excess mass at the kink is the ergodic distribution of an optimal-stopping problem. The compensated elasticity is identified jointly with the distribution of adjustment costs from the rates at which individuals enter and exit the kink neighborhood across panel waves, rather than from the static cross-sectional excess mass. The counterfactual density is then an equilibrium object of the estimated model rather than an extrapolated smooth curve.
items.HH: We model bunching as the stationary outcome of a dynamic adjustment process. Individuals with stochastic earnings pay state-dependent costs to relocate, so the excess mass is the ergodic distribution of an optimal-stopping problem. The compensated elasticity is identified jointly with the distribution of adjustment costs from the rates at which individuals enter and exit the kink neighborhood across panel waves, rather than from static cross-sectional mass. The counterfactual density is thus an equilibrium object of the estimated model rather than an extrapolated smooth curve.

items.LL: This paper extends the Rotemberg-weight framework for shift-share instruments to settings with time-varying industry shares. We derive the panel analog of the industry-level weight decomposition and provide standard errors that account for serial correlation in shares within regions and for the reuse of shares across periods. We also give the corresponding pre-trend and correlated-share tests. As a result, the share-exogeneity diagnostics currently available for a single cross-section can be applied when shares are updated across successive waves of data, with inference adjusted for the repeated use of overlapping shares.
items.LH: This paper extends the Rotemberg-weight framework for shift-share instruments to time-varying industry shares. We derive the panel analog of the industry-level weight decomposition and provide standard errors that account for serial correlation in shares within regions and for the reuse of shares across periods. We also supply the corresponding pre-trend and correlated-share tests. This lets share-exogeneity diagnostics available for a single cross-section apply when shares are updated across waves, with inference adjusted for the repeated use of overlapping shares.
items.HL: This paper models the initial industry shares as equilibrium outcomes of a spatial sorting process rather than as exogenous regressors. We derive the bias of the Bartik estimator as an explicit function of the sorting model's parameters, which we estimate from a first stage relating location choices to fundamentals excluded from the outcome equation. Using these estimates, we construct a control-function correction that delivers a consistent estimator when the shares are endogenous. Identification then rests on an exclusion restriction on the sorting fundamentals rather than on exogeneity of the shares themselves.
items.HH: This paper models the initial industry shares as equilibrium outcomes of a spatial sorting process rather than as exogenous regressors. We write the bias of the Bartik estimator as an explicit function of the sorting model's parameters, which we estimate from a first stage relating location choices to fundamentals excluded from the outcome equation. We construct a control-function correction that delivers consistency even when the shares are endogenous. Identification then rests on an exclusion restriction on the sorting fundamentals rather than on share exogeneity.

items.LL: We test whether the salience of the cost basis moderates the disposition effect. In the standard account, investors code gains and losses relative to the purchase price. We add that display prominence of the cost basis shifts the strength of this coding: when the purchase price is shown prominently in the trading interface, the tendency to realize gains and defer losses should increase; when it is hidden, the effect should attenuate. We estimate the change in the disposition-effect coefficient by experimentally varying cost-basis display and by exploiting cross-platform differences in field trading data.
items.LH: We test whether the salience of the cost basis moderates the disposition effect. Investors code gains and losses relative to the purchase price, and display prominence shifts the strength of this coding. When the purchase price is shown prominently in the trading interface, the tendency to realize gains and defer losses increases; when it is hidden, the effect attenuates. We estimate the change in the disposition-effect coefficient by experimentally varying cost-basis display and exploiting cross-platform differences in field trading data.
items.HL: We test whether the disposition effect is driven by belief-based expectations rather than the purchase price. We treat the reference point as the investor's endogenous expectation of the sale outcome, formed from beliefs about future price. Under expectation-based reference dependence, gains and losses are coded relative to the recently held expected return, so belief-updating drives the effect. This predicts that shifting an investor's return expectations changes, and can reverse, the propensity to realize gains and hold losses: under optimistic expectations, holding a winner becomes the loss-averse choice. We estimate the change in the disposition-effect coefficient by experimentally varying return expectations.
items.HH: We test whether the disposition effect is driven by belief-based expectations rather than the purchase price. The reference point is treated as the investor's endogenous expectation of the sale outcome, formed from beliefs about future price. Under expectation-based reference dependence, gains and losses are coded relative to the recently held expected return, so belief-updating drives the effect. This predicts that shifting return expectations changes, and can reverse, the propensity to realize gains and hold losses: under optimism, holding a winner becomes the loss-averse choice. We estimate the change in the disposition-effect coefficient by experimentally varying return expectations.

items.LL: This study offers participants a menu of commitment savings contracts that differ in stringency, varying the early-withdrawal penalty and the lock-up length. For each participant we elicit the present-bias parameter beta and the degree of awareness of it using incentivized intertemporal choice tasks. We then estimate whether more sophisticated present-biased individuals select stricter contracts, and whether stricter contracts increase deposits among those who adopt them. The analysis reports how contract stringency interacts with measured present bias in predicting both take-up and subsequent saving behavior.
items.LH: This study offers participants a menu of commitment savings contracts that differ in stringency, varying the early-withdrawal penalty and the lock-up length. For each participant we elicit the present-bias parameter beta and awareness of it through incentivized intertemporal choice tasks. We test whether more sophisticated present-biased individuals select stricter contracts, and whether stricter contracts raise deposits among adopters. The design reports how contract stringency interacts with present bias in predicting take-up and saving.
items.HL: This study offers participants a menu of commitment savings contracts and models demand as arising from reference-dependent preferences. A binding contract is treated as moving the saver's reference point to the target balance, so falling short of it registers as a loss. For each participant we elicit the loss-aversion parameter lambda and the present-bias parameter beta separately. We then estimate whether take-up and follow-through track loss aversion rather than present bias, and whether individuals with beta near one still demand commitment, reporting how each parameter predicts take-up and subsequent saving.
items.HH: This study offers participants a menu of commitment savings contracts and models demand as arising from reference-dependent preferences. A binding contract is treated as moving the saver's reference point to the target balance, so falling short registers as a loss. For each participant we elicit the loss-aversion parameter lambda and the present-bias parameter beta separately. The design tests whether take-up and follow-through track loss aversion rather than present bias, and whether individuals with beta near one still demand commitment, reporting how each parameter predicts take-up and subsequent saving.

items.LL: We use administrative contribution records from firms that set their automatic-enrollment default at different rates, such as 3% versus 6%, to estimate how the clustering of employees at the default varies with the level at which the default is set. We estimate participation gains and the share of employees contributing exactly the default rate, and test whether these patterns differ by employee income tier and age. The analysis quantifies how the magnitude of the default contribution effect scales as the default level changes across plans.
items.LH: We use administrative contribution records from firms that set their automatic-enrollment default at different rates, such as 3% versus 6%, to estimate how the clustering of employees at the default varies with the default level. We estimate whether participation gains and the share contributing exactly the default scale with the default level, and whether the pattern differs by employee income tier and age.
items.HL: We test whether the automatic-enrollment default operates as an informational signal about the appropriate saving rate rather than through inertia alone. Combining administrative contribution data with an experiment that presents the default either as an explicit sponsor recommendation or as an arbitrary administrative placeholder, and eliciting employees' beliefs about the appropriate rate, we estimate whether the default effect is mediated by inferred recommendation. The design tests whether the effect attenuates when the default is disclosed as arbitrary and whether it shifts stated beliefs about saving, not only contributions.
items.HH: We test whether the automatic-enrollment default operates as an informational signal about the appropriate saving rate. Combining administrative contribution data with an experiment that presents the default either as an explicit sponsor recommendation or as an arbitrary administrative placeholder, and eliciting employees' beliefs about the appropriate rate, we estimate whether the default effect is mediated by inferred recommendation. We test whether the effect attenuates when the default is disclosed as arbitrary and whether it shifts stated beliefs about saving, not only contributions.

items.LL: We extend the tax-salience experiment of Chetty, Looney, and Kroft (2009) by posting tax-inclusive prices across grocery categories that face different sales-tax rates. Within the standard framework, demand responses at each rate identify the underreaction parameter that captures how much consumers discount taxes added at the register. Comparing this parameter across low- and high-tax categories yields a dose-response estimate of whether inattention is a fixed fraction or instead scales with the size of the tax. The design uses the same posted-price manipulation and register-level demand data.
items.LH: We extend the tax-salience experiment of Chetty, Looney, and Kroft (2009) by posting tax-inclusive prices across grocery categories facing different sales-tax rates. The underreaction parameter captures how much consumers discount register-added taxes. Comparing it across low- and high-tax categories delivers a dose-response estimate of whether inattention scales with tax size.
items.HL: We extend the tax-salience setting of Chetty, Looney, and Kroft (2009) by treating consumer attention as a finite resource allocated across the components of a posted price, rather than as a fixed discount attached to each tax. In a grocery field experiment we make one component—the sales tax—salient through tax-inclusive posting, and measure the price elasticity of demand for a non-manipulated component, such as the base price or a second embedded tax. If attention is budgeted, raising the salience of one attribute lowers responsiveness to the others, an attentional crowd-out we estimate directly.
items.HH: We extend the tax-salience setting of Chetty, Looney, and Kroft (2009) by treating consumer attention as a finite resource allocated across the components of a posted price. In a grocery field experiment we make one component—the sales tax—salient through tax-inclusive posting and measure the price elasticity of demand for a non-manipulated component, such as the base price or a second embedded tax. If attention is budgeted, making one attribute salient reduces responsiveness to the others, an attentional crowd-out we estimate.

items.LL: The six randomized microcredit evaluations are extended by estimating treatment-effect heterogeneity along baseline borrower characteristics — prior business ownership, entrepreneurial ability proxies, and initial firm size — using pooled individual-level data and pre-specified subgroups. Additional follow-up waves let us observe whether early business investment later translates into profit or income gains. The unit of analysis remains the borrowing household, and the outcome set matches the original trials. The aim is more precise conditional average effects and a test of whether the modest pooled averages conceal returns concentrated among more experienced entrepreneurs.
items.LH: The six randomized microcredit evaluations are extended by estimating treatment-effect heterogeneity along baseline borrower characteristics — prior business ownership, entrepreneurial ability proxies, and initial firm size — using pooled individual-level data and pre-specified subgroups. Additional follow-up waves let us observe whether early investment later translates into profit or income. Keeping the borrowing household as the unit and the original outcomes, we test whether the modest pooled averages conceal returns concentrated among experienced entrepreneurs.
items.HL: We attribute the modest average effects to measuring credit at the household level in thin local markets. Microcredit expansion is modeled as a general-equilibrium reallocation: borrower firms capture demand from non-borrowing competitors in the same village product market, so borrower gains and non-borrower losses net toward zero. Using saturation-randomized designs that vary credit-access intensity across local markets, we take the market rather than the household as the unit of analysis and measure competitive displacement, entry, exit, and price responses among microenterprises. The prediction is a positive borrower-level effect alongside a near-zero market-level effect.
items.HH: We attribute the modest average effects to measuring credit at the household level. We model microcredit expansion as a general-equilibrium reallocation: borrower firms capture demand from non-borrowing competitors in the same village product market, so gains and losses net toward zero. Using saturation-randomized designs that vary credit-access intensity across markets, we take the market rather than the household as the unit and measure displacement, entry, exit, and price responses. We predict a positive borrower-level effect alongside a near-zero market-level effect.

items.LL: This study adds an in-kind treatment arm to a standard unconditional cash transfer trial. Poor households in a new set of study communities are randomized to receive either cash or a food basket of equal market value, alongside a control group. We measure total consumption expenditure, dietary diversity, and food security at an 18-month follow-up. The analysis estimates whether equally valued cash and in-kind transfers produce statistically equivalent consumption gains, extending existing unconditional-transfer evaluations to a direct within-trial modality comparison in this setting.
items.LH: This study compares cash against in-kind aid. Poor households in new study communities are randomized to cash or a food basket of equal market value, alongside a control group. We track consumption expenditure, dietary diversity, and food security at an 18-month follow-up. The analysis estimates whether equally valued cash and in-kind transfers yield equivalent consumption gains, a within-trial modality comparison.
items.HL: This study models the mechanism behind in-kind transfers as protection against redistributive pressure within kin and social networks, rather than as a restriction on recipient choice. Because in-kind goods are less fungible, they are harder for the network to claim and can act as a commitment device that retains resources in the recipient household. Households are randomized to equally valued cash or in-kind transfers, with sharing obligations measured beforehand. We test whether in-kind raises own-household consumption more than cash where obligations are strong, tracking both consumption and transfers made to others.
items.HH: This study models in-kind transfers as shielding resources from redistributive pressure within kin and social networks. Because in-kind goods are less fungible, they act as a commitment device that keeps resources inside the recipient household. Households are randomized to equally valued cash or in-kind transfers, with sharing obligations measured beforehand. We test whether in-kind raises own-household consumption more than cash where obligations are strong, tracking both consumption and transfers made to others.

items.LL: Miguel and Kremer's deworming trial and its long-run follow-ups estimate average labor-market returns to childhood treatment. Using the existing long-run follow-up data from the deworming project, this study estimates whether those returns vary with baseline helminth-infection intensity. The specification interacts treatment assignment with pre-treatment worm burden on adult earnings, hours worked, and consumption, accounting for the program's documented cross-school spillover structure. The analysis tests whether the earnings and consumption gains concentrate among children who carried the heaviest infection load before treatment.
items.LH: Using the existing long-run follow-up data from the deworming project, this study estimates whether the long-run labor-market returns to childhood treatment vary with baseline helminth-infection intensity, interacting treatment assignment with pre-treatment worm burden on adult earnings, hours worked, and consumption while accounting for the documented cross-school spillover structure. The analysis tests whether gains concentrate among the heaviest-infected children.
items.HL: Miguel and Kremer's deworming trial and its long-run follow-ups attribute long-run labor-market returns to childhood treatment to accumulated individual human capital. Using the same long-run follow-up data, this study models the returns as a function of the local disease-transmission environment. It constructs a measure of cumulative exposure to reduced community worm transmission — community deworming saturation weighted by its persistence over subsequent years — and estimates adult earnings, hours, and consumption on this environmental-exposure measure alongside own treatment status. The analysis tests whether returns track environmental exposure and attenuate where the worm reservoir rebounded.
items.HH: Using the same long-run follow-up data, this study models the long-run returns to childhood deworming treatment as a function of the local disease-transmission environment. It constructs a measure of cumulative exposure to reduced community worm transmission — community deworming saturation weighted by its persistence over subsequent years — and estimates adult earnings, hours, and consumption on this environmental-exposure measure alongside own treatment status. The analysis tests whether returns track environmental exposure and attenuate where the worm reservoir rebounded.

items.LL: This project re-estimates the settler-mortality instrumental-variables model using disaggregated institutional sub-indices — constraints on the executive, property-rights protection, and contract enforcement — entered separately to identify which institutional dimension carries the long-run effect on income per capita. The country sample is expanded to include additional former colonies, and the first stage is replicated with an alternative historical mortality series. The identification strategy and exclusion restriction follow the original design. The aim is finer resolution on the mediating institutional channel and broader empirical coverage.
items.LH: This project re-estimates the settler-mortality instrumental-variables model using disaggregated institutional sub-indices — constraints on the executive, property-rights protection, and contract enforcement — entered separately to identify which dimension carries the long-run income effect. The sample is expanded to additional former colonies, and the first stage is replicated with an alternative mortality series. The focus is on how the classic channel is measured.
items.HL: This project models the persistent channel behind long-run development as the social closure of the colonial elite rather than the formal rules captured by institutional indices. Under this account, high settler mortality produced a narrow extractive elite that consolidated through intermarriage and landholding into a closed lineage network reproducing itself across formal regime changes, so measured institutions are a partly endogenous downstream outcome. Using surname-concentration and intergenerational elite-persistence measures as the mediator, it tests whether they carry the settler-mortality effect on income even when contemporary institutional-quality indices are held constant.
items.HH: This project models the persistent object behind long-run development as the social closure of the colonial elite rather than institutions as the channel. High settler mortality produced a narrow extractive elite that consolidated through intermarriage and landholding into a closed lineage network reproducing itself across formal regime changes, making measured institutions a partly endogenous downstream outcome. Using surname-concentration and intergenerational elite-persistence measures as the mediator, it tests whether they carry the settler-mortality effect on income even when contemporary institutional-quality indices are held constant.

items.LL: This study extends the transaction-cost account of mobile money and risk-sharing by examining heterogeneity in the consumption-smoothing effect. Using a household panel, it partitions negative shocks into idiosyncratic events, such as illness or job loss, and covariate events, such as regional harvest failure, and interacts mobile money access with the geographic distance to a household's potential remittance senders. The estimated pattern of interest is whether the reduction in consumption sensitivity is larger for idiosyncratic shocks and for households whose senders are more dispersed, the cases in which lower transfer costs are expected to bind most.
items.LH: This study examines heterogeneity in the consumption-smoothing effect within the transaction-cost account of mobile money and risk-sharing. Using a household panel, it partitions negative shocks into idiosyncratic events, such as illness or job loss, and covariate events, such as regional harvest failure, and interacts mobile money access with the distance to a household's potential remittance senders. The reduction in consumption sensitivity is expected to be larger for idiosyncratic shocks and for households whose senders are more dispersed.
items.HL: This study models mobile money's effect on risk-sharing as operating through the observability of balances and transfers rather than through transfer costs. Because mobile records make holdings and receipts inferable to kin, adoption raises the expected claims a household faces from its network, the redistributive kinship tax, which can induce strategic concealment and reduce net insurance for some households even as transfer costs fall. Using a household panel, it tests whether adoption predicts concealment behaviors, such as off-account balances or secondary numbers, and whether post-shock net transfers received minus given diverge across households by their exposure to network claims.
items.HH: This study models mobile money's effect on risk-sharing as operating through observability rather than transfer costs. Because mobile records make holdings and receipts inferable to kin, adoption raises the expected claims a household faces from its network, the redistributive kinship tax, which can reduce net insurance for some households even as transfer costs fall. It tests whether adoption predicts concealment behaviors, such as off-account balances or secondary numbers, and whether post-shock net transfers received minus given diverge.

items.LL: This project extends the commuting-zone exposure design to the 2008–2019 period. Using import growth in other high-income countries as an instrument for local exposure to Chinese import competition, it estimates effects on manufacturing employment and wages across U.S. commuting zones. The specification follows the standard shift-share reduced form. Estimates are disaggregated by worker age cohort to document whether younger and older workers within the same exposed commuting zone show different employment and wage adjustments over the updated sample window.
items.LH: This project applies the commuting-zone exposure design to the 2008–2019 period. Using import growth in other high-income countries as an instrument, it estimates manufacturing employment and wages across U.S. commuting zones with the standard shift-share reduced form. It disaggregates estimates by worker age cohort to document whether younger and older workers within the same exposed commuting zone adjust differently over the updated sample window.
items.HL: This project recasts local exposure to import competition as a function of a commuting zone's position in the domestic production network rather than its own industry mix. Using inter-regional input-output linkages, it constructs indirect exposure that propagates to upstream supplier regions and downstream customer regions with little direct import competition. It estimates manufacturing employment and wage outcomes as a function of both direct and network-propagated exposure across U.S. commuting zones, separating the labor-market response transmitted through domestic input-output linkages from own-industry exposure.
items.HH: This project recasts local exposure to import competition as a function of a commuting zone's position in the domestic production network rather than its own industry mix. Using inter-regional input-output linkages, it constructs indirect exposure propagating to upstream supplier and downstream customer regions with little direct import competition. It estimates manufacturing employment and wage outcomes across U.S. commuting zones on both direct and network-propagated exposure, separating the response transmitted through domestic input-output linkages from own-industry exposure.

items.LL: We estimate the structural gravity equation on a country-pair panel using exporter-time and importer-time fixed effects to absorb the multilateral resistance terms. Bilateral trade costs are specified as a log-linear function of distance interacted with common language, contiguity, and shared currency, allowing the distance elasticity to vary across pair types. The multilateral resistances are treated as nuisance terms captured by the fixed effects. We estimate by Poisson pseudo-maximum-likelihood to accommodate zero flows and heteroskedasticity, and report how the estimated distance elasticity differs across the observable pair characteristics.
items.LH: We estimate the structural gravity equation on a country-pair panel using exporter-time and importer-time fixed effects to absorb the multilateral resistance terms. Bilateral trade costs are specified as a log-linear function of distance interacted with common language, contiguity, and shared currency, allowing the distance elasticity to vary across pair types. The multilateral resistances are treated as nuisance terms captured by the fixed effects. We estimate by Poisson pseudo-maximum-likelihood, and the estimated distance elasticity is heterogeneous across pair types.
items.HL: We relax the assumption that bilateral trade costs are exogenous and separable from the multilateral resistances. In our specification, trade costs are partly endogenous to the resistance terms: trade-facilitating investments respond to a country's remoteness, so costs and multilateral resistance are jointly determined. The resistances thus become structural objects that shift trade costs rather than nuisance price indices to be differenced out, implying the fixed-effects approximation absorbs economically meaningful variation. We estimate a fixed-point system that iterates between the cost equation and the resistance equations, identified from the differential exposure of remote countries to trade-facilitation shocks.
items.HH: We relax the assumption that the multilateral resistances are nuisance price indices to be differenced away. In this specification, trade costs are partly endogenous to the resistance terms: trade-facilitating investments respond to a country's remoteness, so costs and multilateral resistance are jointly determined. The resistances become structural objects that shift trade costs, implying the standard fixed-effects approximation discards economically meaningful variation. We estimate a fixed-point system that iterates between the cost and resistance equations, identified from the differential exposure of remote countries to trade-facilitation shocks.

items.LL: This paper extends the heterogeneous-firms trade model by letting each firm draw a fixed export cost jointly with its productivity. Selection into exporting is then determined by a threshold in the productivity–fixed-cost plane rather than by a single productivity cutoff. We characterize the selection frontier and derive comparative statics for export participation and the exporter productivity premium with respect to variable and fixed trade costs. When fixed export costs are homogeneous across firms, the results reduce to the standard single-cutoff formulation. We calibrate the model to firm-level export data and report the fit.
items.LH: This paper extends the heterogeneous-firms trade model by letting each firm draw a fixed export cost jointly with its productivity. Selection is then governed by a threshold in the productivity–fixed-cost plane rather than a single productivity cutoff. We characterize this selection frontier and derive comparative statics for export participation and the exporter productivity premium with respect to variable and fixed trade costs. When fixed export costs are homogeneous, the results nest the single-cutoff case. We calibrate the model to firm-level export data and report the fit.
items.HL: This paper studies selection into exporting when a firm's export profitability increases with the mass of domestic firms already exporting, through a shared export-infrastructure externality such as logistics density, buyer information, and intermediary thickness. Export participation becomes a coordination problem: given fundamentals can support multiple self-consistent export cutoffs, so aggregate export intensity is history-dependent. We characterize the equilibrium set, show that identical fundamentals can sustain high- and low-export equilibria, and derive conditions under which small changes in trade costs trigger discontinuous jumps in participation. We calibrate the model to firm-level export data and report the fit.
items.HH: This paper studies selection into exporting when a firm's export profitability rises with the mass of domestic firms already exporting, through a shared export-infrastructure externality such as logistics density, buyer information, and intermediary thickness. Export participation becomes a coordination problem in which identical fundamentals support multiple self-consistent cutoffs, so aggregate export intensity is history-dependent. We characterize the equilibrium set, show that the same fundamentals can sustain high- and low-export equilibria, and show that small trade-cost changes can trigger discontinuous jumps in participation. We calibrate the model to firm-level export data and report the fit.

items.LL: We extend the establishment-level evaluation of enterprise-zone hiring and investment credits to a panel of recent zone designations across several states. Using a boundary-discontinuity design, we compare establishments located just inside zone borders to those just outside, estimating effects on employment growth and firm entry. We allow the effects to vary with credit generosity, local unemployment, and industry tradability. We test whether more generous credits or slacker local labor markets yield employment gains that pooled estimates, which report a null, may average away. Standard errors are clustered at the zone boundary.
items.LH: We extend the establishment-level evaluation of enterprise-zone hiring and investment credits to a panel of recent zone designations across several states. Using a boundary-discontinuity design, we compare establishments just inside zone borders to those just outside, estimating effects on employment growth and firm entry, and allowing the effects to vary with credit generosity, local unemployment, and industry tradability. This tests whether the pooled null may mask employment gains that standard evaluations do not detect. Standard errors are clustered at the zone boundary.
items.HL: We model enterprise-zone credits as a subsidy to labor-market matching frictions rather than to firm location or capital. Using linked employer-employee administrative records, we estimate the effect of credits on the rate at which previously nonemployed zone residents transition into employment, and on the durability and wage growth of those matches, holding total establishment employment fixed. Under this framing, credits reallocate hiring toward local nonemployed residents and raise match quality without increasing aggregate headcount. Net-job-count evaluations that measure only total employment therefore do not detect this margin.
items.HH: We model enterprise-zone credits as a subsidy to labor-market matching frictions rather than to firm location or capital. Using linked employer-employee administrative records, we estimate the effect of credits on the rate at which previously nonemployed zone residents transition into employment, and on the durability and wage growth of those matches, holding total establishment employment fixed. Under this framing, credits reallocate hiring toward local nonemployed residents and raise match quality without increasing headcount. Net-job-count evaluations that measure only total employment do not detect this margin.

items.LL: We estimate the elasticity of income reported across the corporate and personal tax bases with respect to the corporate-personal rate differential, using a recent tax-return panel that adds several statutory rate changes. We relate the share of income reported in each base to the contemporaneous differential, with controls for income level and macroeconomic conditions. We estimate the wedge elasticity separately by firm-size bracket and industry sector, and test whether it has shifted over time and whether it varies across sectors with differing mixes of pass-through and C-corporation organization.
items.LH: We estimate how income moves across the corporate and personal tax bases. Using a recent tax-return panel that adds several statutory rate changes, we relate each base's income share to the contemporaneous rate differential, controlling for income level and macroeconomic conditions. We estimate the wedge elasticity separately by firm-size bracket and industry sector, and test whether it has shifted over time and whether it varies across sectors with differing pass-through and C-corporation mixes.
items.HL: We model the income-shifting elasticity as a function of agents' beliefs about the persistence of the current corporate-personal wedge, so the response to a rate change depends on whether it is perceived as transitory or permanent. Because shifting entails a fixed reorganization cost, agents solve an optimal-stopping problem, moving a base only when the expected discounted wedge gain exceeds that cost, producing an inaction region and history dependence. We estimate the persistence-belief and adjustment-cost parameters from differential responses to reforms that were pre-announced, temporary, or accompanied by signals about future rates, identifying the belief channel from the announcement-versus-enactment gap.
items.HH: We model income shifting as driven by agents' beliefs about how persistent the corporate-personal wedge will be, so responses depend on whether a change is seen as transitory or permanent. Because shifting carries a fixed reorganization cost, agents solve an optimal-stopping problem, moving only when the expected discounted gain exceeds that cost, yielding an inaction region and history dependence. We estimate the belief and adjustment-cost parameters from responses to pre-announced, temporary, or signaled reforms, identifying the belief channel from the announcement-versus-enactment gap.

items.LL: Using random assignment from the Oregon Health Insurance Experiment, this study estimates whether Medicaid's effects on utilization and financial protection vary with enrollees' baseline predicted health-care need. Predicted need is constructed from pre-lottery diagnoses and prior utilization.
