Applying the lottery instrument for enrollment within need strata, we estimate treatment effects on outpatient visits, prescriptions, emergency-department use, and out-of-pocket spending. The analysis extends the original average estimates to a conditional-average-treatment-effect profile across the need distribution, indicating which subpopulations account for the aggregate utilization and spending responses documented in the original experiment.
items.LH: Using random assignment from the Oregon Health Insurance Experiment, we estimate whether Medicaid's effects on utilization and financial protection vary with enrollees' baseline predicted health-care need, built from pre-lottery diagnoses and prior utilization. Applying the lottery instrument within need strata, we estimate treatment effects on outpatient visits, prescriptions, emergency-department use, and out-of-pocket spending. This conditional-treatment-effect profile indicates which subpopulations account for the aggregate utilization and spending responses.
items.HL: This study treats the coverage effect from the Oregon Health Insurance Experiment as a market-equilibrium object rather than an individual treatment effect. Lottery-induced demand from the treatment group changes local provider capacity, wait times, and practice patterns, which also alter care received by the untreated control group. Using geographic variation in lottery-induced coverage density across local health-care markets, we identify supply-side responses and estimate spillovers onto uninsured non-enrollees. Under this framing, the individual lottery contrast recovers a demand shift net of common market changes. We estimate the market-level dose-response linking coverage density to utilization and outcomes for treated and untreated residents.
items.HH: We treat the coverage effect from the Oregon experiment as a market-equilibrium object rather than an individual treatment effect. Lottery-induced treatment-group demand changes local provider capacity, wait times, and practice patterns, which also alter care received by the untreated control group. Using geographic variation in lottery-induced coverage density across local health-care markets, we identify supply-side responses and estimate spillovers onto uninsured non-enrollees. Under this framing, the individual lottery contrast recovers a demand shift net of common market changes. We estimate the market-level dose-response linking coverage density to outcomes for treated and untreated residents.

items.LL: Using administrative claims data with plausibly exogenous variation in coinsurance rates, we estimate the price elasticity of medical demand under cost-sharing. Rather than reporting one aggregate figure, we estimate the elasticity separately by service category—outpatient, inpatient, prescription drugs, preventive, and mental health care—and by patient income and baseline-health subgroups. The analysis quantifies how the utilization response is distributed across service types and populations, and assesses whether the aggregate elasticity near -0.2 reflects a uniform response or one concentrated in particular services and groups, identifying which service categories drive the aggregate response.
items.LH: Using administrative claims data with plausibly exogenous variation in coinsurance rates, we estimate the price elasticity of medical demand under cost-sharing, separately by service category—outpatient, inpatient, prescription drugs, preventive, and mental health care—and by patient income and baseline-health subgroups. The aggregate figure near -0.2 may reflect a response concentrated in particular services and groups rather than a uniform one. The analysis identifies which service categories drive the aggregate response.
items.HL: We model the utilization response to cost-sharing as governed by limited attention to nonlinear benefit design rather than the true marginal price. Consumers act on salient cost cues—whether they have crossed the deductible, or recent out-of-pocket bills—instead of the actuarial price they face. Two implications follow: non-price interventions that alter cost salience should shift utilization while holding the marginal price fixed, and the measured price elasticity is a composite of price sensitivity and attention allocation. We test these predictions with variation in cost salience independent of price.
items.HH: We model the utilization response to cost-sharing as governed by limited attention to nonlinear benefit design rather than the true marginal price. Consumers act on salient cost cues—whether they have crossed the deductible, or recent out-of-pocket bills—rather than the actuarial price they face. Two implications follow: non-price interventions that alter cost salience should shift utilization while holding the marginal price fixed, and the measured price elasticity is a composite of price sensitivity and attention allocation. We test these predictions with variation in cost salience independent of price.

items.LL: Using more recent Health Care Cost Institute claims, we re-estimate the association between local hospital market concentration and negotiated private prices. We add insurer market concentration as a covariate and stratify the concentration–price gradient by service line, including diagnostic imaging, laboratory tests, and inpatient surgery. The aim is to characterize which categories of care show the steepest concentration–price association, and to report whether the concentration coefficient remains stable across service lines once insurer concentration is held fixed.
items.LH: Using recent Health Care Cost Institute claims, we re-estimate the association between local hospital market concentration and negotiated private prices. Adding insurer market concentration as a covariate and stratifying the concentration–price gradient by service line—diagnostic imaging, laboratory tests, inpatient surgery—we characterize which categories of care drive the association and whether the concentration coefficient remains stable across service lines once insurer concentration is held fixed.
items.HL: We model negotiated commercial hospital prices as anchored to a hospital-specific reference point: each hospital's own Medicare payment rate. In this specification, local market concentration governs the size of the negotiated markup over that anchor rather than the price level directly. We test for bunching of commercial-to-Medicare price ratios at focal multiples and estimate whether the concentration gradient loads on the proportional markup over the anchor. Price formation is treated as reference-dependent rather than as bargaining over total surplus.
items.HH: We model negotiated commercial hospital prices as anchored to a hospital-specific reference point—each hospital's own Medicare rate. In this specification, local concentration governs not the price level but the markup over that anchor. We test for bunching of commercial-to-Medicare ratios at focal multiples and estimate whether the concentration gradient loads on the proportional markup. Price formation is treated as reference-dependent rather than as bargaining over surplus.

items.LL: This project extends the retrospective cost-effectiveness evaluation of the SO2 allowance market to the regional NOx Budget Trading Program. Using the standard marginal-abatement-cost framework, it estimates realized cost savings relative to a counterfactual uniform emission-rate standard, comparing observed allowance prices with engineering estimates of source-level marginal abatement costs. The observed allowance price path is decomposed into contributions from fuel switching, control-technology cost declines, and electricity demand variation. The analysis reports how closely realized prices track the engineering marginal cost curve across compliance periods and source categories, and whether the divergence documented for SO2 recurs in this second program.
items.LH: This project extends the retrospective cost-effectiveness evaluation of the SO2 allowance market to the regional NOx Budget Trading Program. Using the standard marginal-abatement-cost framework, it re-estimates realized cost savings relative to a counterfactual uniform emission-rate standard, comparing observed allowance prices with engineering marginal-cost estimates. The allowance price path is decomposed into contributions from fuel switching, control-technology cost declines, and demand variation, and the analysis reports how closely realized prices track the engineering cost curve across compliance periods and source categories.
items.HL: This project models the allowance price not as a direct measure of a known marginal abatement cost schedule but as the equilibrium of a market in which firms hold private, uncertain beliefs about their own abatement costs. Under this framing the permit market functions as a cost-discovery institution, and the gap between realized allowance prices and engineering cost estimates reflects belief updating rather than market thinness. Measured cost savings then combine allocative efficiency with the social value of revealed cost information, which a uniform standard cannot elicit. The prediction is tested through cross-sectional dispersion in source compliance choices and its narrowing over time.
items.HH: This project models the allowance price as the equilibrium of a market in which firms hold private, uncertain beliefs about their own costs, rather than as a direct trace of a known marginal abatement cost schedule. Under this framing the permit market functions as a cost-discovery institution, and the gap between realized prices and engineering estimates reflects belief updating rather than market thinness. Measured cost savings combine allocative efficiency with the social value of revealed information, a rent no uniform standard can elicit. This is tested through cross-sectional dispersion in compliance choices.

items.LL: We extend the DICE integrated assessment model by replacing the constant consumption discount rate in the Ramsey equation with a deterministically declining term structure of forward rates. The schedule is calibrated to the empirical dispersion in long-run real interest rates, consistent with certainty-equivalent (gamma) discounting. We recompute the social cost of carbon under this time-varying schedule and report how the estimate shifts relative to constant-rate runs. Results are reported for both the descriptive and prescriptive parameterizations associated with Nordhaus and Stern, across a standard range of damage-function specifications.
items.LH: We modify the DICE integrated assessment model by replacing the constant consumption discount rate in the Ramsey equation with a deterministically declining term structure of forward rates, calibrated to the empirical dispersion in long-run real interest rates (certainty-equivalent, or gamma, discounting). We recompute the social cost of carbon under this time-varying schedule. We report results for the descriptive and prescriptive parameterizations of Nordhaus and Stern across standard damage-function specifications.
items.HL: We modify the DICE integrated assessment model by replacing the deterministic social discount rate with a consumption-based stochastic discount factor. Each future climate damage is valued at a risk-adjusted rate determined by its covariance with aggregate consumption—its climate beta—rather than by a single chosen rate. Under this formulation the social cost of carbon is the price of a state-contingent climate liability: damages that co-move with consumption carry a positive risk premium, while damages that hedge consumption risk are discounted less. We estimate the resulting social cost of carbon across a standard range of damage-function specifications and consumption-risk calibrations.
items.HH: We modify the DICE integrated assessment model by replacing the deterministic social discount rate with a consumption-based stochastic discount factor. Each future climate damage is valued at a risk-adjusted rate set by its covariance with aggregate consumption—its climate beta. Under this formulation the social cost of carbon is the price of a state-contingent climate liability, so damages co-moving with consumption carry a risk premium while hedging damages are discounted less. We estimate this across standard damage-function specifications and consumption-risk calibrations.

items.LL: This study extends existing estimates of the pollution–productivity relationship from ozone to fine particulate matter (PM2.5). Using daily output records from piece-rate manual laborers linked to nearby air-monitor readings, it applies a within-worker fixed-effects specification and estimates the contemporaneous same-day dose-response. The regressions control for temperature, humidity, and seasonality. The analysis tests whether the output elasticity with respect to PM2.5 is comparable in magnitude to the established ozone elasticity, including at concentrations below current regulatory thresholds.
items.LH: This study extends estimates of the pollution–productivity relationship from ozone to fine particulate matter (PM2.5). Using daily output records from piece-rate manual laborers linked to nearby air-monitor readings, it applies a within-worker fixed-effects specification and estimates the contemporaneous same-day dose-response, controlling for temperature, humidity, and seasonality. It tests whether the PM2.5 elasticity is comparable in magnitude to the established ozone elasticity, including at concentrations below regulatory thresholds.
items.HL: This study models pollution's effect on productivity as operating through the rate of on-the-job human-capital accumulation, rather than only through contemporaneous physiological impairment. Under this specification, exposure concentrated during a worker's early skill-formation period lowers the trajectory of subsequent productivity, producing deficits that persist after air quality improves. The design compares later-tenure output, measured during clean-air periods, across worker cohorts whose initial training months coincided with differing pollution levels, separating a persistent stock component from the reversible same-day flow effect.
items.HH: This study models pollution's effect on productivity as operating through the rate of on-the-job human-capital accumulation, rather than only through reversible, same-day physiological impairment. Under this specification, exposure concentrated during a worker's early skill-formation period lowers the trajectory of subsequent productivity, producing deficits that persist after air quality improves. The design compares later-tenure output, measured during clean-air periods, across cohorts whose initial training months coincided with differing pollution levels, separating a persistent stock component from the reversible flow effect.

items.LL: We re-estimate metropolitan housing-supply elasticities on a post-2010 panel and disaggregate each metro's elasticity to the county and municipal level, computing the land-unavailability term and the regulatory term at the finer geography rather than for the metro as a whole. We then test whether these sub-metro elasticities predict the cross-sectional dispersion of house-price responses to demand shocks better than the aggregate metro elasticity, applying the same instrumental-variable strategy at the sub-metro level. The exercise extends the standard elasticity measure to a smaller unit of analysis.
items.LH: We re-estimate metropolitan housing-supply elasticities on a post-2010 panel and disaggregate each metro's elasticity to the county and municipal level, computing the land-unavailability term and the regulatory term at the finer geography rather than for the metro as a whole. We test whether these sub-metro elasticities predict the cross-sectional dispersion of house-price responses to demand shocks better than the aggregate metro elasticity, applying the same instrumental-variable strategy at the sub-metro level.
items.HL: We model housing-supply elasticity as a state-dependent, endogenous outcome of local political economy rather than a fixed geographic-plus-regulatory parameter. We treat regulatory stringency as a latent variable that responds to house-price appreciation itself—incumbent homeowners tighten land-use rules as their housing wealth rises—so measured elasticity declines endogenously during booms. We estimate a simultaneous system in which prices and the restrictiveness index are jointly determined, using shocks to the homeowner share and to the electorate's age structure as instruments, and test whether the apparent constancy of Saiz elasticities masks a feedback loop that amplifies demand shocks beyond what fixed constraints imply.
items.HH: We model housing-supply elasticity as a state-dependent, endogenous outcome of local political economy rather than a fixed geographic-plus-regulatory parameter. We treat regulatory stringency as a latent variable that responds to house-price appreciation itself—incumbent homeowners tighten land-use rules as their wealth rises—so measured elasticity declines endogenously during booms. Estimating a simultaneous system with shocks to the homeowner share and the electorate's age structure as instruments, we test whether the apparent constancy of Saiz elasticities masks a feedback loop that amplifies demand shocks beyond what fixed constraints imply.

items.LL: Chetty, Hendren and Katz (2016) estimate that each additional year of childhood exposure to a lower-poverty neighborhood raises adult earnings and college attendance, with effects concentrated among children who move before age 13. We extend this design to a wider set of adult outcomes—physical and mental health, mortality, and family formation—using the same voucher-assignment instrument and age-at-move variation. We estimate per-year exposure slopes separately for each outcome domain and test whether the gradient is stable across domains or attenuates for outcomes realized later in adulthood.
items.LH: We extend the design of Chetty, Hendren and Katz (2016) to a wider set of adult outcomes—physical and mental health, mortality, and family formation—using the same Moving to Opportunity voucher-assignment instrument and age-at-move variation. We estimate per-year exposure slopes separately for each outcome domain and test whether the gradient is stable or attenuates for later-realized outcomes.
items.HL: Chetty, Hendren and Katz (2016) model neighborhood effects as cumulative childhood exposure to a lower-poverty place, roughly linear in years before age 13. We instead treat the gradient as a network-rewiring effect: the operative mechanism is the disruption of a child's existing peer and institutional ties and their re-anchoring to higher-opportunity peers. Using school records, sibling co-moves, and linked peer networks, we measure the share of pre-move ties severed and the opportunity level of newly formed ties, then test whether outcomes track network-reset intensity holding cumulative low-poverty exposure fixed.
items.HH: Chetty, Hendren and Katz (2016) model neighborhood effects as cumulative childhood exposure to a lower-poverty place. We instead treat the gradient as a network-rewiring effect, driven by the disruption of a child's existing peer and institutional ties and their re-anchoring to higher-opportunity peers. Using school records, sibling co-moves, and linked peer networks, we measure the share of pre-move ties severed and the opportunity level of newly formed ties, then test whether outcomes track network-reset intensity holding cumulative low-poverty exposure fixed.

items.LL: Using the tax-record-linked administrative panel and quasi-experimental teacher-switching design of Chetty, Friedman and Rockoff, we extend the set of long-run outcomes linked to teacher value-added beyond earnings and college attendance to include homeownership, retirement-account participation, and cross-state geographic mobility. We estimate the effect of a one-standard-deviation increase in teacher value-added on each outcome, applying the same drift-adjusted value-added measure and forecast-bias checks. We then test whether the magnitude of these effects differs across students' baseline socioeconomic status by interacting value-added with parental income quartile.
items.LH: Prior work has linked teacher value-added mainly to test scores and near-term earnings. Using the tax-record-linked panel and quasi-experimental teacher-switching design of Chetty, Friedman and Rockoff, we extend the set of long-run outcomes to homeownership, retirement-account participation, and cross-state geographic mobility. Applying the same drift-adjusted value-added measure and forecast-bias checks, we estimate the effect of a one-standard-deviation increase in teacher value-added on each. We then examine how these effects differ across students' baseline socioeconomic status by parental income quartile.
items.HL: Using the tax-record-linked administrative panel and quasi-experimental teacher-switching design of Chetty, Friedman and Rockoff, we decompose the effect of teacher value-added on adult earnings into an absolute human-capital component and a relative-position component reflecting a worker's rank within their local labor market. We identify the two by exploiting cross-cohort and cross-market variation in the share of the workforce exposed to high-value-added teaching. A human-capital account predicts individual earnings returns invariant to the exposed share; a positional account predicts returns that attenuate as that share rises.
items.HH: Using the tax-record-linked panel and quasi-experimental teacher-switching design of Chetty, Friedman and Rockoff, we decompose the earnings effect of teacher value-added into an absolute human-capital component and a relative-position component reflecting a worker's rank within their local labor market. Exploiting cross-cohort and cross-market variation in the share of the workforce exposed to high-value-added teaching, we test the prediction that, unlike human capital, positional returns attenuate as exposure rises.

items.LL: This study augments the Fama-French five-factor model with a sixth factor: a long-short portfolio sorted on prior 2-to-12-month returns (momentum), constructed using the same breakpoints and value-weighting as the existing factors. The six-factor model is estimated by time-series regressions on a set of anomaly-sorted test portfolios, and its fit is compared to the five-factor model using GRS test statistics and the average magnitude of estimated intercepts. The specification is re-estimated in international and emerging-market samples and across subperiods to assess robustness. The exercise quantifies how much the added factor reduces residual pricing errors.
items.LH: This study augments the Fama-French five-factor model with a sixth factor: a long-short portfolio sorted on prior 2-to-12-month returns (momentum), constructed using the same breakpoints and value-weighting as the existing factors. The six-factor model is estimated by time-series regressions on anomaly-sorted test portfolios, and its fit is compared to the five-factor model using GRS statistics and intercept magnitudes. The specification is re-estimated in international and emerging-market samples and across subperiods.
items.HL: This study models the size, value, profitability, and investment premia as the price impact of correlated institutional demand under downward-sloping demand curves, rather than as compensation for covariance with common risk factors. Using quarterly institutional holdings data, it estimates demand elasticities and characteristic-weighted demand tilts for institutions whose mandates load on the same firm characteristics. The characteristic spreads are then expressed as reduced-form functions of these demands, and the cross-section of premia is regressed on the intensity of correlated demand to test it against the factor-covariance account. Data requirements and estimation are comparable to standard holdings-based analyses.
items.HH: This study models the size, value, profitability, and investment premia as the price impact of correlated institutional demand under downward-sloping demand curves, not compensation for covariance with common risk factors. Using quarterly institutional holdings data, it estimates demand elasticities and characteristic-weighted demand tilts for institutions whose mandates load on the same characteristics. The characteristic spreads are recast as reduced-form functions of these demands, and the cross-section of premia is regressed on correlated-demand intensity, tested against the factor-covariance account.

items.LL: We extend the within-firm estimator of Khwaja and Mian (2008) by interacting the bank liquidity shock with bank capitalization. Using the multi-bank borrower sample with firm fixed effects, we add a term allowing the supply elasticity to vary with the lending bank's capital ratio. This measures whether better-capitalized banks pass liquidity shocks through to lending less strongly. We then relate the estimated differential pass-through to firm-level real outcomes such as employment and investment, letting the strength of the bank-lending channel depend on bank capital.
items.LH: Within the multi-bank borrower design of Khwaja and Mian (2008), we interact the bank liquidity shock with bank capitalization, adding firm fixed effects and a term that lets the supply elasticity vary with the lending bank's capital ratio. This examines whether better-capitalized banks pass shocks through differently. We relate this differential pass-through to firm-level real outcomes such as employment and investment, letting the channel's strength depend on bank capital.
items.HL: We treat the firm fixed effect in the within-firm estimator as absorbing part of the transmitted shock when credit-supply shocks propagate through inter-firm production linkages. A liquidity shock to one firm's bank reduces the trade credit and orders that firm extends to its suppliers and customers, who borrow from other banks, so the shock reappears as a demand shift elsewhere. We combine the multi-bank borrower design of Khwaja and Mian (2008) with firm-to-firm transaction linkages to recover network-propagated credit-supply effects that the within-firm estimator differences away, separating direct from network transmission.
items.HH: We treat the firm fixed effect as absorbing part of the transmitted shock when credit-supply shocks propagate through inter-firm production linkages. A liquidity shock to one firm's bank reduces the trade credit and orders it extends to suppliers and customers, who borrow elsewhere, so the shock re-emerges as a demand shift. We combine the multi-bank borrower design of Khwaja and Mian (2008) with firm-to-firm transaction linkages to recover network-propagated credit-supply effects that the estimator differences away.

items.LL: We extend the standard fixed-cost model of stock-market participation by letting the per-period participation cost depend on household observables, focusing on financial-literacy and education proxies. Using panel data on household portfolios, we estimate the implied distribution of participation costs conditional on these covariates within the usual threshold specification, and test whether households with lower measured literacy face higher implied costs. We then quantify how conditioning the cost on covariates shifts the share of non-participation attributed to costs relative to the share attributed to low financial wealth.
items.LH: We let the per-period participation cost depend on household observables such as financial-literacy and education proxies. Using panel data on household portfolios, we estimate the implied distribution of participation costs conditional on these covariates within the threshold specification, and examine the extent to which lower-literacy households face higher implied costs. We then quantify how conditioning the cost on covariates shifts the non-participation attributed to costs versus low financial wealth.
items.HL: We model non-participation as the delay region of an irreversible entry decision under learning, rather than the outcome of an exogenous per-period cost. Households hold ambiguous beliefs about the return distribution and face costs of re-learning after exit, so waiting to resolve uncertainty carries an option value; entry occurs only once perceived ambiguity falls below a threshold. We estimate this entry-timing model on household portfolio panels and test its predictions: participation responds to perceived volatility and ambiguity holding wealth and monetary costs fixed, and exhibits hysteresis.
items.HH: We model non-participation as the delay region of an irreversible entry decision under learning. Households hold ambiguous beliefs about the return distribution and face costs of re-learning after exit, so waiting to resolve uncertainty carries an option value; entry occurs only once perceived ambiguity falls below a threshold. Estimating this entry-timing model on household portfolio panels, we test the predictions that participation responds to perceived volatility and ambiguity holding wealth and monetary costs fixed, and exhibits hysteresis.

items.LL: We extend the staggered cable-entry design of DellaVigna and Kaplan to additional outcomes and cycles. Using the timing of Fox News availability across markets, we estimate its effect on voter turnout alongside partisan vote share for the 2000 through 2008 presidential and midterm elections. We test whether the availability effect varies with a town's baseline partisan composition and with local cable penetration, and whether implied persuasion rates remain stable once down-ballot races are added. Treatment definition, identification, and the dose-response reading follow the original exposure framework.
items.LH: Using the timing of Fox News availability across markets, we estimate its effect on voter turnout alongside partisan vote share for the 2000 through 2008 presidential and midterm elections. We test whether the availability effect varies with a town's baseline partisan composition and with local cable penetration, and whether implied persuasion rates remain stable once down-ballot races are added. The treatment definition and identification follow the original exposure framework.
items.HL: We treat the availability effect as partly an equilibrium response of political elites rather than solely direct viewer persuasion. When a partisan channel enters a market, local party organizations and candidates observe the shifted information environment and reallocate mobilization effort and reposition platforms in anticipation, so the reduced-form vote shift bundles direct persuasion with endogenous supply-side adaptation. We separate the two channels by exploiting whether a market's Fox entry fell before or after that cycle's candidate-filing and primary deadlines, and by comparing incumbent spending and platform positions across early- versus late-entry markets.
items.HH: We read the availability effect as an equilibrium response of political elites rather than direct viewer persuasion. When a partisan channel enters a market, local party organizations and candidates observe the shifted information environment and reallocate mobilization effort and reposition platforms in anticipation, so the reduced-form vote shift bundles direct persuasion with endogenous supply-side adaptation. We separate the two channels by exploiting whether a market's Fox entry fell before or after that cycle's candidate-filing and primary deadlines, and by comparing incumbent spending and platform positions across early- versus late-entry markets.

items.LL: We apply the close-election regression discontinuity design to U.S. state legislative elections. Using bias-corrected local-polynomial estimation with data-driven bandwidth selection and robust confidence intervals, we estimate the party incumbency advantage at the 50% vote-share threshold. We estimate the effect separately by decade and by chamber (upper and lower houses) and assess whether its magnitude has shifted over time. We report standard validity checks: density continuity at the cutoff, covariate balance, and placebo thresholds. We also report heterogeneity by initial victory margin and by state-level electoral competitiveness.
items.LH: We apply the close-election regression discontinuity design to U.S. state legislative elections. Using bias-corrected local-polynomial estimation with data-driven bandwidth selection and robust confidence intervals, we estimate the party incumbency advantage at the 50% vote-share threshold, separately by decade and by chamber (upper and lower houses), and assess whether its magnitude has shifted over time. We report standard validity checks: density continuity at the cutoff, covariate balance, and placebo thresholds. We also report heterogeneity by initial victory margin and by state-level electoral competitiveness.
items.HL: We interpret the close-election regression discontinuity estimate as the equilibrium effect of a bare win on the entry decisions of high-quality challengers, rather than as a direct effect of office-holding on voters. Using the same 50% vote-share discontinuity, we measure challenger quality and entry in the subsequent election — prior office-holding, candidate experience, and contested-primary status — as mediating outcomes, and decompose the incumbency estimate into an entry-deterrence channel and a residual direct channel. We test whether the estimated advantage attenuates where challenger entry is institutionally constrained, such as open-seat term limits or strong party recruitment.
items.HH: We interpret the close-election regression discontinuity estimate as the equilibrium effect of a bare win on the entry decisions of high-quality challengers, rather than as a direct effect of office-holding on voters. Using the same 50% vote-share discontinuity, we measure challenger quality and entry in the subsequent election — prior office-holding, candidate experience, and contested-primary status — as mediating outcomes, and decompose the incumbency estimate into an entry-deterrence channel and a residual direct channel. We test whether the estimated advantage attenuates where challenger entry is institutionally constrained, such as open-seat term limits or strong party recruitment.

items.LL: We revisit the augmented Solow growth-accounting regression and replace its single school-enrollment proxy with a human-capital measure that separates schooling quantity from schooling quality. Average years of attainment and internationally comparable achievement-test scores enter the Cobb-Douglas production function as distinct inputs, each with its own output elasticity. We re-estimate the cross-country specification on the same steady-state framework, holding the convergence structure and identifying assumptions fixed. The procedure decomposes the estimated human-capital share into quantity and quality components and reports how each contributes to explained income variation across countries.
items.LH: We revisit the augmented Solow growth-accounting regression and separate schooling quantity from schooling quality. Average years of attainment and internationally comparable achievement-test scores enter the Cobb-Douglas production function as distinct inputs, each with its own output elasticity. We re-estimate the cross-country specification on the same steady-state framework, holding the convergence structure and identifying assumptions fixed. The procedure decomposes the estimated human-capital share into quantity and quality components and reports how each contributes to explained income variation across countries.
items.HL: We reconsider how human capital enters cross-country growth accounting. Rather than treating it as an accumulable factor with a fixed output elasticity, we model it as a determinant of the rate at which a country closes its total-factor-productivity gap to the world frontier. Human capital enters through an interaction with distance to the frontier, so a given stock raises income faster the farther a country lies below the leader. We re-estimate the specification, reallocating income variation from factor accumulation to differences in adoption speed, and report the model's revised convergence implications across countries.
items.HH: We reconsider how human capital enters cross-country growth accounting. Rather than treating it as an accumulable factor with a fixed output elasticity, we model it as a determinant of the rate at which a country closes its total-factor-productivity gap to the world frontier. Human capital enters through an interaction with distance to the frontier, so a given stock raises income faster the farther a country lies below the leader. We re-estimate the specification, reallocating income variation from factor accumulation to differences in adoption speed, and report the model's revised convergence implications across countries.

items.LL: We extend the Hsieh-Klenow accounting from a single cross-section to firm panel data. The observed dispersion of log TFPR is decomposed into a persistent firm-level component and a transitory year-to-year component, and the implied aggregate TFP gains are computed separately for each. We report the share of measured misallocation that is persistent and relate that component to firm age, size, and ownership. The CES and monopolistic-competition assumptions are retained, as is the interpretation of TFPR dispersion as firm-specific distortions. Estimates are reported for manufacturing plants in the same countries studied in the original framework.
items.LH: We apply a panel decomposition to the Hsieh-Klenow accounting, extending it from a single cross-section to firm panel data. The observed dispersion of log TFPR splits into a persistent firm-level component and a transitory year-to-year component, and the implied TFP gains are computed separately for each. We report the share of measured misallocation that is persistent and relate it to firm age, size, and ownership. The CES assumptions and the interpretation of TFPR dispersion as distortions are retained.
items.HL: We relax the constant-markup assumption of the Hsieh-Klenow framework by replacing CES demand with a variable-elasticity demand system in which firm markups are heterogeneous and endogenous. Under this structure, part of the observed dispersion of log TFPR reflects optimal markup variation rather than distortions, and the efficient benchmark no longer equalizes TFPR across firms. We re-estimate aggregate misallocation after separating the markup component from the distortion component, and we test whether TFPR dispersion co-moves with market-concentration measures rather than with tax and subsidy proxies. Estimates are reported for the same manufacturing data used in the original accounting.
items.HH: We replace CES demand in the Hsieh-Klenow framework with a variable-elasticity demand system in which firm markups are heterogeneous and endogenous. Under this structure, part of the observed dispersion of log TFPR reflects optimal markup variation rather than distortions, and the efficient benchmark no longer equalizes TFPR across firms. We re-estimate misallocation after separating the markup component from the distortion component, and we examine whether TFPR dispersion co-moves with market-concentration measures rather than with tax and subsidy proxies.

items.LL: We extend the Fehr–Schmidt inequity-aversion model by letting the envy parameter α and the guilt parameter β vary with stake magnitude. In a within-subject design, the same participants play ultimatum and dictator games at low, medium, and high monetary stakes. We estimate α and β at each stake level, separately for proposers and responders, and characterize the stake-elasticity of each parameter. Structural estimation uses maximum likelihood with subject-level random effects. The design tests whether distributional-preference weights are stable traits or attenuate as the amounts at stake increase.
items.LH: We extend the Fehr–Schmidt inequity-aversion model by letting the envy parameter α and the guilt parameter β vary with stake magnitude. In a within-subject design, the same participants play ultimatum and dictator games at low, medium, and high monetary stakes. We estimate α and β at each stake level, separately for proposers and responders, and characterize each parameter's stake-elasticity, using maximum-likelihood structural estimation with subject-level random effects. The design tests whether distributional-preference weights are stable traits or vary as the amounts at stake increase.
items.HL: We modify the Fehr–Schmidt model by replacing its fixed equal-split reference with each responder's elicited prior expectation about the offer. The envy term then penalizes the shortfall of the realized offer relative to what the responder expected, rather than relative to an equal division. In ultimatum and dictator games, we manipulate beliefs about the offer distribution exogenously while holding the realized offer constant, and elicit expectations before play. Maximum-likelihood structural estimation with subject-level random effects recovers whether rejection thresholds and transfers shift with expectations independently of payoff inequality.
items.HH: We modify the Fehr–Schmidt model by replacing its fixed equal-split reference with each responder's elicited prior expectation about the offer. The envy term then penalizes the shortfall of the realized offer relative to what the responder expected, rather than relative to an equal division. In ultimatum and dictator games, we exogenously manipulate beliefs about the offer distribution while holding the realized offer constant, and elicit expectations before play. Maximum-likelihood structural estimation with subject-level random effects recovers whether rejection thresholds and transfers shift with expectations independently of payoff inequality.

items.LL: Consider smooth fictitious play in which the smoothing parameter governing exploration is not held fixed but declines over time along a prescribed schedule. We characterize how the decay rate affects convergence of empirical play and the associated regret bound across the standard stage-game classes, including zero-sum, potential, and 2x2 games. The analysis derives conditions on the schedule under which the established convergence and universal-consistency guarantees continue to hold, and quantifies the tradeoff between the speed of exploration decay and the resulting regret rate.
items.LH: Consider smooth fictitious play in which the smoothing parameter governing exploration declines over time along a prescribed schedule rather than being held fixed. We characterize how the decay rate affects convergence of empirical play and the associated regret bound across zero-sum, potential, and 2x2 games. The analysis derives conditions on the schedule under which convergence and universal-consistency guarantees continue to hold, and quantifies the tradeoff between the speed of exploration decay and the resulting regret rate.
items.HL: We treat each player's belief-updating rule as itself an object of learning. Players maintain a distribution over candidate learning rules, such as fictitious play, smooth fictitious play, and various recency weightings, and reweight these rules over time according to their realized performance, so the effective adaptive procedure is selected endogenously from opponents' play rather than fixed in advance. We characterize the resulting second-order dynamics and their rest points, and identify stage-game classes in which the population of rules concentrates on procedures that converge to equilibrium versus classes in which the rule-distribution itself fails to settle.
items.HH: We treat each player's belief-updating rule as itself an object of learning. Players maintain a distribution over candidate rules, such as fictitious play, smooth fictitious play, and recency weightings, and reweight them by realized performance, so the adaptive procedure is selected endogenously from opponents' play rather than fixed in advance. We characterize the resulting second-order dynamics and their rest points, and identify stage-game classes in which the rule-distribution itself fails to settle.

items.LL: We consider identifying latent causal factors from multi-environment interventional image data without paired counterfactuals. Each environment is modeled as a single-target hard intervention on one latent. We train an encoder-decoder to a fixed-dimensional latent space and match each environment's latent marginal to a shared reference, adding a penalty that concentrates the induced shift in a single coordinate. Under the condition that every latent is intervened in at least one environment, the factors are recovered up to permutation and coordinate-wise reparameterization. Each intervention is localized by the coordinate whose marginal changes.
items.LH: We consider identifying latent causal factors from multi-environment interventional image data without paired counterfactuals. Each environment is modeled as a single-target hard intervention on one latent. We train an encoder-decoder to a fixed-dimensional latent space and match each environment's latent marginal to a shared reference, with a penalty that concentrates the induced shift in one coordinate. When every latent is intervened in at least one environment, the factors are recovered up to permutation and coordinate-wise reparameterization. Interventions are localized by which coordinate's marginal changes.
items.HL: We consider identifying latent causal factors from multi-environment interventional image data without paired counterfactuals. We fit a per-environment score model, the gradient of the latent log-density, in a shared latent space, and recover the factors as the basis that jointly block-diagonalizes the pairwise differences of the score Hessians across environments. Interventions need not target single coordinates and may be soft. Identifiability follows from a genericity condition on the collection of intervention-induced Hessian perturbations. Marginals are never matched, and the number of coordinates each intervention affects is estimated rather than assumed.

items.LL: We modify the multistep consistency sampler by replacing its fixed time discretization with a curvature-adaptive grid. On a calibration set we estimate the local curvature of each probability-flow ODE trajectory from finite differences of the denoiser output, then allocate a fixed budget of sampling steps so that evaluation times are denser where average curvature is largest and sparser where trajectories are near-linear. The resulting schedule is computed once offline and reused for all inputs. Within the EDM and consistency-model framework, this lowers the discretization error of the few-step sampler at a fixed number of function evaluations.
items.LH: We modify the multistep consistency sampler by replacing its fixed time discretization with a curvature-adaptive grid. On a calibration set we estimate the local curvature of each probability-flow ODE trajectory from finite differences of the denoiser output, then allocate a fixed step budget so that evaluation times are denser where average curvature is largest and sparser where trajectories are near-linear. The schedule is computed once offline and reused for all inputs. Within the EDM and consistency-model framework, this reduces discretization error at a fixed number of function evaluations.
items.HL: We formulate the multistep consistency sampler as a fixed-point iteration of the self-consistency operator. Rather than choosing steps to minimize per-step discretization error, we derive the schedule from the local spectral radius of the operator's Jacobian along the trajectory, placing steps so the self-consistency residual contracts by a constant target factor per iteration, per input. This gives a convergence criterion: sampling iterates until the residual falls below a tolerance, yielding an input-dependent step count rather than a fixed schedule. Curvature enters through the Jacobian's linearization, making the sampler a residual-driven fixed-point solve.

items.LL: We extend the Byte Latent Transformer's entropy-based dynamic patching by calibrating the boundary-entropy threshold separately for each language or script. Using a held-out multilingual calibration set, per-language thresholds are set so that expected patch length, and therefore per-language sequence length and compute cost, is approximately equalized across languages. This directly targets the cross-language token-count disparity previously reported for BPE tokenizers. We evaluate on multilingual language-modeling and downstream classification benchmarks, reporting per-language bytes-per-patch, compute, and accuracy, and compare against fixed-threshold BLT and standard BPE tokenizer baselines.
items.LH: We extend the Byte Latent Transformer's entropy-based patching by calibrating the boundary-entropy threshold separately for each language or script. Using a held-out calibration set, thresholds are chosen so that expected patch length, and thus per-language compute, is equalized across languages. This targets the cross-language disparity previously attributed to BPE. We evaluate on multilingual benchmarks, reporting per-language bytes-per-patch, compute, and accuracy against fixed-threshold BLT and BPE baselines.
items.HL: We replace the Byte Latent Transformer's entropy-based boundary criterion with a discrete latent segmentation optimized end-to-end for downstream language-modeling loss. Boundaries are treated as a latent variable and trained via a straight-through relaxation under a Lagrangian constraint that fixes the expected number of patches per byte uniformly across languages. Boundary placement is therefore decoupled from local next-byte predictability and instead allocated to positions that reduce task loss within an equal compute budget. We evaluate on multilingual benchmarks, reporting per-language compute, bytes-per-patch, and accuracy against BLT and BPE baselines.
items.HH: We replace the Byte Latent Transformer's entropy-based boundary criterion with a discrete latent segmentation optimized end-to-end for downstream loss. Boundaries are treated as a latent variable and trained via a straight-through relaxation under a Lagrangian constraint that fixes the expected number of patches per byte uniformly across languages. items.LL: Position interpolation rescales every token's position index uniformly, including the first few tokens that carry the attention sink. We measure attention-sink strength, the attention mass placed on initial tokens, before and after interpolation across a range of sequence lengths, to determine whether interpolation weakens or displaces the sink at extended contexts. We then test a mitigation that keeps the first k tokens' position indices unscaled, or prepends a few dedicated sink tokens, during interpolation fine-tuning, and measure whether preserving the sink reduces long-context perplexity relative to standard interpolation.
items.LH: Position interpolation rescales every token's position index uniformly, including the first few tokens that carry the attention sink. We measure attention-sink strength, the attention mass on initial tokens, before and after interpolation across sequence lengths, to determine whether interpolation weakens or displaces the sink. We then test a modification that keeps the first k tokens' position indices unscaled, or prepends a few dedicated sink tokens, during fine-tuning, and measure whether preserving the sink reduces long-context perplexity relative to standard interpolation.
items.HL: We model the attention sink as a length-adaptive mechanism that regulates total attention mass so the entropy of the attention distribution stays within its trained range as sequence length grows, rather than as a fixed property of initial-token positions. On this account, position interpolation harms length generalization because altering position density mis-calibrates the sink's mass compensation, letting attention entropy drift outside the trained regime at long contexts. We add an explicit length-conditioned scalar that controls off-diagonal attention mass, decoupling mass calibration from positional encoding, so that extending context re-fits a calibration curve rather than relearning positions.
items.HH: We model the attention sink as a length-adaptive mechanism that regulates total attention mass so the entropy of the attention distribution stays within its trained range as sequence length grows, rather than as a fixed property of initial-token positions. On this account, position interpolation harms length generalization because altering position density mis-calibrates the sink's mass compensation, letting attention entropy drift outside the trained regime at long contexts. We introduce a length-conditioned scalar that controls off-diagonal attention mass, decoupling mass calibration from positional encoding, so that extending context re-fits a calibration curve rather than relearning positions.

items.LL: We consider conformal prediction when a model is updated at test time on unlabeled data under covariate shift, seeking prediction sets that keep marginal coverage as the model drifts. After each adaptation step, we treat the updated model as fixed and recompute nonconformity scores on a held-out labeled calibration set. We then apply covariate-shift-weighted conformal prediction: the density ratio between calibration and current test covariates reweights the calibration scores, and the weighted quantile defines the prediction set for the next test point. Repeating this at every adaptation snapshot gives each snapshot approximate coverage under the estimated shift.
items.LH: We consider conformal prediction when a model is updated at test time on unlabeled data under covariate shift, seeking prediction sets that keep marginal coverage as the model drifts. After each adaptation step, we treat the updated model as fixed and recompute nonconformity scores on a held-out labeled calibration set. We then apply covariate-shift-weighted conformal prediction: the density ratio between calibration and current test covariates reweights the calibration scores, and the weighted quantile defines the prediction set for the next test point. Repeating this at every adaptation snapshot gives each snapshot coverage under the estimated shift.
items.HL: We consider conformal prediction when a model is updated at test time on unlabeled data under covariate shift. We feed the union of the calibration covariates and the incoming test covariate through the same adaptation operator, using that common adaptation maps, batch-norm statistic updates and entropy minimization, are equivariant to the ordering of their unlabeled input batch. Because the adapted model is a symmetric function of that batch, the nonconformity scores it assigns to calibration and test points are exchangeable, though each depends on the whole batch. The ordinary conformal quantile of these adapted scores then yields finite-sample coverage without estimating any density ratio.
items.HH: We consider conformal prediction when a model is updated at test time on unlabeled data under covariate shift. items.LL: For each ordered pair of tasks, we train to a minimizer on task t, continue training to a minimizer on task t+1, and measure the loss barrier along the linear path between the two parameter vectors. Forward transfer is quantified as the gain in task t+1 performance attributable to prior training on task t. We regress forward-transfer scores on barrier height across all pairs and use the fitted relationship to rank candidate curriculum orderings, selecting the sequence that minimizes the cumulative barrier. We report predictive accuracy on held-out task pairs.
items.LH: We consider mode connectivity as a predictor of forward transfer. For each ordered task pair we measure the loss barrier along the linear path between the task-t and task-t+1 minimizers and regress forward-transfer gains on this barrier height. The fitted relationship ranks candidate orderings and selects the sequence minimizing cumulative barrier. We validate this predictor on held-out task pairs, relating connectivity geometry to what a curriculum transfers forward.
items.HL: For each task solution we compute the tangent directions of the low-loss curve connecting it to a reference minimizer, and project these onto the parameter subspace carrying transferable features, estimated from cross-task gradient alignment. We predict forward transfer from the overlap between a future task's dominant gradient subspace and the current solution's reachable low-loss tangent cone, rather than from barrier height. This implies a non-monotonic relationship: transfer is low when solutions share one basin (redundant features) and when basins are disconnected, peaking at intermediate connectivity where the connecting path's unstable directions coincide with reusable features. We evaluate against barrier-based prediction.
items.HH: For each solution we compute the tangent directions of the low-loss curve to a reference minimizer and project them onto the transferable-feature subspace from cross-task gradient alignment; transfer is then predicted from the overlap between a future task's dominant gradient subspace and the current reachable tangent cone, rather than from barrier height. This yields a non-monotonic relationship: transfer is low at zero barrier (redundant shared-basin features) and at disconnection, peaking at intermediate connectivity. We benchmark against barrier-based prediction.

items.LL: On event-driven hardware such as BrainScaleS-2, networks trained with surrogate-gradient descent lose accuracy at deployment. We characterize this gap as a function of the surrogate derivative's width and scale, sweeping both across several classification tasks under hardware-in-the-loop training. We also measure each device's threshold jitter and report the relationship between the surrogate width that minimizes the deployment gap and the measured jitter. We then propose selecting the surrogate width to match the measured jitter, and evaluate whether this device-specific rule reduces the train-to-deploy accuracy gap relative to a fixed default width.
items.LH: On event-driven hardware such as BrainScaleS-2, we examine the dependence of the train-to-deploy accuracy gap on the surrogate derivative's width and scale. Sweeping both under hardware-in-the-loop training, we measure the link between each device's measured threshold jitter and the width that minimizes the gap. We propose selecting the surrogate width to match the device's own jitter, and assess whether this device-specific rule reduces the deployment gap relative to a fixed default width.
items.HL: On event-driven hardware such as BrainScaleS-2, the train-to-deploy accuracy gap of surrogate-gradient-trained networks is often attributed to analog mismatch. We treat the deployed neuron's empirical stochastic firing-probability curve - its expected output as a function of input under the device's own analog dynamics - as the operand whose derivative serves as the surrogate. We measure this curve per neuron on hardware and use its derivative during backpropagation in place of the chosen smooth surrogate. Training gradients are then computed against the same input-output map realized at deployment. We evaluate whether identifying the surrogate with the device's measured response eliminates the gap.
items.HH: On event-driven hardware such as BrainScaleS-2, the deployment gap of surrogate-gradient learning is often attributed to analog mismatch. We treat the deployed neuron's empirical stochastic firing-probability curve - its expected output under the device's own analog dynamics - as the operand whose derivative serves as the surrogate. We measure this curve per neuron on hardware and use its derivative during backpropagation in place of the chosen smooth surrogate. This makes training gradients coincide with the deployment-time map by construction, addressing the deployment gap.

items.LL: We train sparse autoencoders on synthetic activations built from a known feature set whose activation frequencies follow a power law, varying the power-law exponent, dictionary width, and L1 coefficient. For each setting we measure the fraction of true features recovered, reconstruction error, and the fraction of dead latents, stratified by feature-frequency decile. We then characterize the capacity curve relating width and sparsity penalty to the number of recoverable features, and report how recovery in the low-frequency tail declines relative to the high-frequency head.
items.LH: We train sparse autoencoders on synthetic activations from a known feature set with power-law activation frequencies, varying the exponent, dictionary width, and L1 coefficient, and measure recovered-feature fraction, reconstruction error, and dead-latent fraction by frequency decile. This characterizes a capacity curve linking width and sparsity to recoverable features, and shows the decline of recovery in the low-frequency tail relative to the head.
items.HL: We model SAE feature recovery under power-law frequencies as limited by per-feature gradient signal-to-noise rather than by dictionary dimensionality. Because high-frequency features dominate the reconstruction gradient, the learning signal for rare features falls below the effective noise floor set by the sparsity penalty and optimization variance, giving a recovery limit largely invariant to dictionary width. We test whether widening the dictionary fails to recover tail features while a frequency-equalizing objective (per-feature gradient reweighting or inverse-frequency importance sampling) recovers them at fixed width, separating optimization dynamics from representational capacity.
items.HH: We model SAE feature recovery under power-law frequencies as limited by per-feature gradient signal-to-noise rather than by dictionary dimensionality. Because high-frequency features dominate the reconstruction gradient, the signal for rare features falls below the effective noise floor set by the sparsity penalty and optimization variance, yielding a recovery limit invariant to dictionary width. We test whether widening the dictionary fails to recover tail features, whereas a frequency-equalizing objective (per-feature gradient reweighting or inverse-frequency importance sampling) recovers them at fixed width, separating optimization dynamics from representational capacity.

items.LL: We extend edge-of-stability self-stabilization analysis to adaptive optimizers by working in the preconditioner-induced metric. Treating the preconditioner as slowly varying relative to the oscillation, we define preconditioned sharpness as the top eigenvalue of P^{-1}H and repeat the third-order Taylor expansion in this metric. The analysis predicts that preconditioned sharpness rises to the adaptive stability threshold and is held there by the cubic coupling between the leading-eigenvector oscillation and the sharpness gradient, with the trajectory constrained to the preconditioned-sharpness manifold. We track preconditioned sharpness and oscillation amplitude under RMSProp and Adam to test the constrained-trajectory prediction.
items.LH: We extend edge-of-stability self-stabilization analysis to adaptive optimizers by working in the preconditioner-induced metric. Treating the preconditioner as slowly varying and defining preconditioned sharpness as the top eigenvalue of P^{-1}H, we repeat the third-order Taylor expansion in this metric. Preconditioned sharpness rises to the adaptive threshold and is held there by the cubic coupling between the leading-eigenvector oscillation and the sharpness gradient, along the constrained manifold. We track preconditioned sharpness and oscillation amplitude under RMSProp and Adam to test the constrained-trajectory prediction.
items.HL: We model the adaptive optimizer's second-moment accumulator as a dynamical participant rather than a fixed metric. Because the accumulator averages squared gradients, the edge-of-stability oscillation along the leading eigenvector injects power into accumulator entries aligned with that direction, inflating the corresponding preconditioner values and lowering the preconditioned sharpness. This defines a second stabilizing feedback loop acting through the optimizer state and its two-timescale coupling to the oscillation, distinct from the landscape-cubic channel. The framework predicts that sharpness is held at the threshold by accumulator adaptation even where progressive-sharpening reversal is weak, so ablating the cubic term should leave stabilization largely intact.
items.HH: We model the adaptive optimizer's second-moment accumulator as a dynamical participant rather than a fixed change of metric. Because the accumulator averages squared gradients, the edge-of-stability oscillation injects power into accumulator entries aligned with the leading eigenvector, inflating those preconditioner values and lowering preconditioned sharpness. This defines a second feedback loop acting through the optimizer state, distinct from the landscape-cubic channel. The framework predicts that sharpness is held at threshold by accumulator adaptation even where progressive sharpening is weak, so ablating the cubic term should leave stabilization intact.

items.LL: We study preference-based RLHF when the Bradley-Terry model is only approximately correct. We assume the true comparison probabilities lie within a sup-norm ball of radius epsilon around the best-fitting Bradley-Terry model. Under this assumption, the maximum-likelihood reward estimate and the induced pessimistic policy retain their guarantees, acquiring an additive bias term that scales linearly in epsilon on top of the usual sqrt(d/n) rate for d-dimensional linear rewards. We identify the misspecification radius below which the suboptimality bound stays non-vacuous, and give a matching lower bound showing the additive penalty cannot be removed.
items.LH: We study preference-based RLHF under misspecification of the Bradley-Terry model. We assume the true comparison probabilities lie within a sup-norm ball of radius epsilon around the best-fitting Bradley-Terry model. The maximum-likelihood reward estimate and the induced pessimistic policy retain their guarantees, acquiring an additive bias term scaling linearly in epsilon on top of the usual sqrt(d/n) rate for d-dimensional linear rewards. We identify the misspecification radius below which the bound stays non-vacuous, and give a matching lower bound showing the additive penalty cannot be removed.
items.HL: We study preference-based RLHF when the Bradley-Terry model is misspecified. Rather than estimating a scalar reward, we treat the comparison data as a symmetric two-player game whose payoff is the probability one response is preferred, and target its Nash equilibrium - the policy preferred to every alternative in expectation. Because this equilibrium is defined from comparison probabilities directly, it stays well-posed when no Bradley-Terry reward fits the data. We characterize the comparisons needed to reach an epsilon-approximate equilibrium by a coverage coefficient of the preference-probability class, a quantity invariant under any monotone link.
items.HH: We study preference-based RLHF when the Bradley-Terry model is misspecified. Rather than estimating a scalar reward, we treat the comparison data as a symmetric two-player game whose payoff is the probability one response is preferred, and target its Nash equilibrium - the policy preferred to every alternative in expectation. Because this equilibrium is defined from comparison probabilities directly, it remains well-posed even when no Bradley-Terry reward fits the data. items.LL: Batch active learning measures diversity in the model's learned embedding, and this measurement can degrade when some feature directions collapse to near-zero variance. We add a decorrelation step before selection: the feature covariance is estimated on the current pool, and PCA-whitening downweights dominant directions and removes near-zero-variance ones, so that core-set coverage or BADGE's k-means++ runs on the rescaled embedding. The acquisition rule is otherwise unchanged, with an optional within-batch cosine-similarity penalty to suppress near-duplicate selections. We measure label efficiency on image classification against unmodified core-set and BADGE selection baselines.
items.LH: Batch active learning measures diversity in the model's learned embedding, and this measurement can degrade when some feature directions collapse to near-zero variance. We add a decorrelation step before selection: the feature covariance is estimated on the current pool, and PCA-whitening downweights dominant directions and removes near-zero-variance ones, so that core-set coverage or BADGE's k-means++ runs on the rescaled embedding. The acquisition rule is otherwise unchanged, with an optional within-batch cosine-similarity penalty to suppress near-duplicate selections. We measure label efficiency on image classification against unmodified core-set and BADGE selection baselines.
items.HL: Batch methods measure diversity within the current embedding, which can be distorted when the representation collapses. We define acquisition on the predicted change in the representation's effective dimensionality. For each candidate, a linearized forward model using last-layer (NTK-style) gradients predicts how a hypothetical training update would shift the feature covariance spectrum; the candidate is scored by its expected increase in effective rank, the extent to which it is predicted to counteract collapse. The batch jointly maximizes this predicted spectral expansion. We measure label efficiency on image classification against core-set, BADGE, and BatchBALD.
items.HH: Batch methods measure diversity within the current embedding, which can be distorted when the representation collapses. We define acquisition on the predicted change in the representation's effective dimensionality. For each candidate, a linearized forward model using last-layer (NTK-style) gradients predicts how a hypothetical training update would shift the feature covariance spectrum; the candidate is scored by its expected increase in effective rank, the extent to which it is predicted to counteract collapse. items.LL: We evaluate an out-of-distribution score computed from a standard cross-entropy classifier at the terminal phase of training. For each test input, we take its penultimate-layer feature and measure cosine similarity to the nearest class mean, optionally normalizing by the within-class covariance. Because training drives in-distribution features toward their class means, in-distribution inputs score high while out-of-distribution inputs score lower. The method requires no auxiliary outlier data and no additional training beyond the original classifier. We report AUROC across standard image benchmarks and compare against Mahalanobis-distance and energy-based baselines.
items.LH: We evaluate an out-of-distribution score computed from a standard cross-entropy classifier at the terminal phase of training. For each test input, we take its penultimate-layer feature and measure cosine similarity to the nearest class mean, optionally normalizing by the within-class covariance. Because training drives in-distribution features toward their class means, in-distribution inputs score high while out-of-distribution inputs score lower. The method requires no auxiliary outlier data and no additional training beyond the original classifier. We report AUROC across standard image benchmarks and compare against Mahalanobis-distance and energy-based baselines.
items.HL: We evaluate an out-of-distribution score derived from the angular structure described by neural collapse. At the terminal phase, normalized class means form a simplex equiangular tight frame, so an in-distribution feature has a closed-form target: near-unit similarity to one mean and equal negative similarities to all others. For each test input, we compute its similarity vector to all K class means and score it by the residual from the nearest ideal simplex-vertex signature. This uses the global simplex geometry rather than the nearest-mean distance, and needs no auxiliary outliers or retraining. We report AUROC against prototype-distance and Mahalanobis-distance baselines.

items.LL: For a fixed, pretrained backbone we define the augmentation covariance as the per-sample covariance of features across augmentations, averaged over the dataset, and compute its eigenspectrum. We compare this spectrum with the eigenspectrum of the learned embedding covariance to test whether directions with high augmentation-induced variance are the ones that collapse. We use the count of augmentation-covariance eigenvalues above a fixed threshold as a predictor of the trained embedding's effective rank, and validate this correlation across augmentation strengths and datasets. The measurement provides a pre-training diagnostic for the amount of dimensional collapse to expect.
items.LH: For a fixed, pretrained backbone we define the augmentation covariance as the per-sample covariance of features across augmentations, averaged over the dataset, and compute its eigenspectrum. We compare this spectrum with the eigenspectrum of the learned embedding covariance to test whether directions with high augmentation-induced variance are the ones that collapse. We use the count of augmentation-covariance eigenvalues above a fixed threshold as a predictor of the trained embedding's effective rank, and validate this correlation across augmentation strengths and datasets. The measurement provides a pre-training diagnostic for the amount of dimensional collapse to expect.
items.HL: We model augmentation as a Markov operator on inputs, following the augmentation-graph view, and characterize dimensional collapse by the joint spectral structure of this operator and the data covariance rather than by augmentation magnitude. Collapse is governed by the eigenvalue gap of the augmentation operator and by the alignment, or commutation, between its eigenvectors and those of the data covariance. Misaligned directions with small invariance eigenvalues are the ones eliminated, with a sharp transition at a critical gap. This predicts that two augmentation policies with equal average strength but different spectral gaps produce different collapse, and identifies which specific directions collapse from eigenvector alignment.
items.HH: We model augmentation as a Markov operator on inputs, following the augmentation-graph view, and characterize dimensional collapse by the joint spectral structure of this operator and the data covariance rather than by augmentation magnitude. Collapse is governed by the eigenvalue gap of the augmentation operator and by the alignment, or commutation, between its eigenvectors and those of the data covariance. items.LL: We estimate training-point influence with the standard inverse-Hessian formulation, but replace the single curvature estimate with an average over an ensemble. For several training checkpoints and independently seeded models, we compute a stochastic Hessian-trace (Hutchinson) estimate and average these to form the curvature term used in the influence approximation. Averaging reduces the variance that non-convexity introduces in deep networks. We evaluate the resulting influence rankings on standard leave-one-out and data-retrieval benchmarks, comparing against single-model influence functions and TRAK under matched compute.
items.LH: We estimate training-point influence with the standard inverse-Hessian formulation, but replace the single curvature estimate with an average over an ensemble. For several training checkpoints and independently seeded models, we compute a stochastic Hessian-trace (Hutchinson) estimate and average these to form the curvature term used in the influence approximation. Averaging reduces the variance that non-convexity introduces in deep networks. We evaluate the resulting influence rankings on standard leave-one-out and data-retrieval benchmarks, comparing against single-model influence functions and TRAK under matched compute.
items.HL: We use the ensemble of Hessian-trace estimates as a per-prediction reliability signal rather than as a correction to the influence estimate. Under the assumption that influence validity is governed by local curvature concentration within the loss basin rather than by global convexity, we derive a computable gate from the dispersion of trace estimates across checkpoints and seeds. When the curvature ensemble is too dispersed, the method abstains from attributing that prediction. We calibrate the abstention threshold against measured leave-one-out ground truth and report coverage-accuracy tradeoffs against single-model influence functions and TRAK.
items.HH: We use the ensemble of Hessian-trace estimates as a per-prediction reliability signal rather than as a correction to the influence estimate. Under the assumption that influence validity is governed by local curvature concentration within the loss basin rather than by global convexity, we derive a computable gate from the dispersion of trace estimates across checkpoints and seeds. When the curvature ensemble is too dispersed, the method abstains from attributing that prediction.
