items.LL: We augment task arithmetic with a pairwise conflict term. For each task we compute the average fine-tuning gradient at the pretrained checkpoint, and for each pair we measure the Euclidean cosine similarity between these gradient vectors as a conflict score. Pairs with more negative cosine similarity receive smaller merging coefficients, so strongly conflicting tasks contribute less to the merged model. We regress the aggregate pairwise conflict score against the observed post-merge accuracy drop to predict interference before merging. The term is added on top of task-vector summation and TIES-style sign resolution, and evaluated across image-classification and language benchmarks.
items.LH: We augment task arithmetic with a pairwise conflict term. For each task we compute the average fine-tuning gradient at the pretrained checkpoint, and for each pair we measure the Euclidean cosine similarity between these gradient vectors as a conflict score. Pairs with more negative cosine similarity receive smaller merging coefficients, so strongly conflicting tasks contribute less to the merged model. We regress the aggregate pairwise conflict score against the observed post-merge accuracy drop to predict interference before merging. The term is added on top of task-vector summation and TIES-style sign resolution, and evaluated across image-classification and language benchmarks.
items.HL: We predict interference from gradient conflict measured in function space. For each task we compute the average fine-tuning gradient at the pretrained checkpoint, and for each pair we measure conflict as the functional inner product induced by the empirical neural tangent kernel: the overlap of their Jacobian-vector images on a shared probe set rather than their parameter-space cosine. Pairs with larger functional overlap receive smaller merging coefficients, and task vectors are orthogonalized under the tangent-kernel metric before summation. We regress the aggregate functional-overlap score against the observed post-merge accuracy drop to predict interference before merging, evaluated across image-classification and language benchmarks.
items.HH: We predict interference from gradient conflict measured in function space. For each task we compute the average fine-tuning gradient at the pretrained checkpoint, and for each pair we measure conflict as the functional inner product induced by the empirical neural tangent kernel: the overlap of their Jacobian-vector images on a shared probe set rather than their parameter-space cosine. Pairs with larger functional overlap receive smaller merging coefficients, and task vectors are orthogonalized under the tangent-kernel metric before summation. We regress the aggregate functional-overlap score against the observed post-merge accuracy drop to predict interference before merging, evaluated across image-classification and language benchmarks.

items.LL: A task-conditioned hypernetwork is augmented with a per-task additive low-rank correction. Alongside the weights amortized from the task embedding, a small set of task-specific rank-r factors is learned, and their product is added to each generated weight matrix. This adds a controllable number of parameters per task while leaving the shared hypernetwork unchanged. The effective rank of the resulting weight matrices is measured across tasks and related to multi-task accuracy and inter-task interference, quantifying how far the low-rank amortization bottleneck is relaxed by the added per-task capacity.
items.LH: A task-conditioned hypernetwork is augmented with a per-task additive low-rank correction. Alongside the weights amortized from the task embedding, a small set of task-specific rank-r factors is learned and added to each generated weight matrix, adding a controllable number of parameters per task while leaving the shared hypernetwork unchanged. The effective rank is measured across tasks against accuracy and interference, quantifying how far the amortization bottleneck can be relaxed.
items.HL: The single continuous embedding-to-weight map is replaced with input-conditioned combinatorial composition over a shared learned dictionary of weight atoms. For each layer, the hypernetwork emits, per input as well as per task, a sparse selection and mixing coefficients over the atoms, so the realized weights are assembled by routing rather than by a smooth low-rank map. Expressivity across the task manifold then scales combinatorially with the number of atoms and the sparsity budget rather than with the embedding dimension, decoupling per-task capacity from the rank of any single amortizing map.
items.HH: The embedding-to-weight map is a generation mechanism based on input-conditioned combinatorial composition over a shared learned dictionary of weight atoms. For each layer, the hypernetwork emits, per input as well as per task, a sparse selection and mixing coefficients, so weights are assembled by routing rather than by a smooth low-rank map. Expressivity across the task manifold then scales combinatorially with the number of atoms and the sparsity budget, not the embedding dimension, decoupling per-task capacity from the rank of any single amortizing map.

items.LL: We study codec-token language models for low-resource speech, where the training sequence mixes semantic and acoustic tokens in a fixed proportion. Because acoustic tokens typically outnumber semantic tokens, and semantic structure is undertrained when data is scarce, we apply a token-type-weighted cross-entropy objective. The weight on semantic versus acoustic tokens is set per language from the measured token-count ratio in the corpus, up-weighting semantic tokens where they are comparatively scarce. We tune the coefficient on held-out intelligibility and add semantic-token oversampling during batching. We evaluate on several low-resource languages against an unweighted baseline.
items.LH: We study codec-token language models for low-resource speech, attributing the difficulty in this setting to the fixed proportion of semantic and acoustic tokens in the training sequence. Because acoustic tokens outnumber the scarce semantic ones, we apply a token-type-weighted cross-entropy objective whose weight is set per language from the measured token-count ratio, up-weighting semantic tokens. The coefficient is tuned on held-out intelligibility and paired with semantic-token oversampling, and compared against unweighted training.
items.HL: We study codec-token language models for low-resource speech, treating the acoustic-semantic imbalance as an information-rate mismatch between the two token streams. Rather than modifying the language-model loss, token composition is made input-adaptive: a learned gate, driven by the model's per-frame semantic predictive uncertainty, controls how many residual acoustic tokens the codec emits at each step. Tokenizer and language model are trained jointly, so acoustic capacity is spent only where semantic content is confidently predicted, and the emitted stream's composition tracks local information density rather than a fixed quantization depth. We evaluate on several low-resource languages.
items.HH: We study codec-token language models for low-resource speech and treat the acoustic-semantic imbalance as an information-rate mismatch between the token streams. Instead of fixed hierarchical tokenization, token composition is made input-adaptive: a learned gate, driven by the model's per-frame semantic predictive uncertainty, controls how many residual acoustic tokens the codec emits, with tokenizer and language model trained jointly. Acoustic capacity is spent only where semantic content is confidently predicted, so the stream's composition tracks local information density rather than fixed quantization depth.

items.LL: Under approximate stationarity over the forecast window, the lag-h conditional mean of a series is close to a fixed linear function of the lookback, so a single linear map is near-optimal and a transformer's extra capacity adds estimation variance without accuracy gain. We test this by computing a per-dataset stationarity index from windowed distribution-shift and unit-root statistics, then checking whether the linear-transformer MSE gap varies monotonically with that index across benchmarks. A synthetic sweep interpolating from stationary to nonstationary generating processes verifies whether the gap narrows as stationarity increases.
items.LH: Under approximate stationarity, the lag-h conditional mean is close to a fixed linear function of the lookback, so a single linear map is near-optimal and a transformer's extra capacity only adds variance. We compute a per-dataset stationarity index from windowed distribution-shift and unit-root statistics and examine whether the linear-transformer MSE gap varies monotonically with it. A synthetic stationary-to-nonstationary sweep checks this relationship as stationarity increases.
items.HL: Optimal long-horizon prediction of a stationary process is convolution with a fixed lag kernel, a phase-sensitive Wiener filter tied to absolute lag. Self-attention aggregates positions by content similarity, which is invariant to absolute lag, so it represents fixed-lag phase-coherent operators inefficiently even when the target is nonlinear. The deciding variable is taken to be the effective rank of each series' lag kernel, not its stationarity: low-rank kernels favor linear or MLP models, and adding an explicit fixed-lag convolutional bias to attention, rather than more capacity or data, closes the gap. A lag-kernel-rank diagnostic is used to explain preference that a stationarity index leaves unexplained.
items.HH: Optimal long-horizon prediction of a stationary process is convolution with a fixed lag kernel, a phase-sensitive Wiener filter tied to absolute lag. Self-attention aggregates by content similarity, invariant to absolute lag, so it represents such operators inefficiently even for nonlinear targets. The deciding variable is the effective rank of each series' lag kernel, not its stationarity: low-rank kernels favor linear or MLP models, and an explicit fixed-lag convolutional bias, not more capacity or data, closes the gap. A lag-kernel-rank diagnostic explains preference that stationarity indices miss.

items.LL: We extend entity-based knowledge-conflict evaluation with a per-query gating classifier that decides whether an answer should be conditioned on the retrieved passage or on the model's parametric prior. The classifier uses retrieval relevance scores, passage-conditioned generation likelihood, closed-book confidence, and lexical overlap as features, and is trained on substituted-entity conflict cases with ground-truth source labels. At inference the gate reweights the two answer distributions. We evaluate on standard conflict benchmarks across several model sizes and report source-selection accuracy and downstream QA accuracy.
items.LH: A per-query gating classifier decides whether an answer should be conditioned on the retrieved passage or on the parametric prior. It uses retrieval relevance scores, passage-conditioned generation likelihood, closed-book confidence, and lexical overlap as features, trained on substituted-entity conflicts with ground-truth source labels, and reweights the two answer distributions at inference. We evaluate on conflict benchmarks across model sizes, reporting source-selection and downstream QA accuracy.
items.HL: We resolve retrieval-context conflicts by intervening on the model's internal computation rather than selecting between output answers. Using causal attribution, we locate the attention heads and MLP components that write the parametric answer into the residual stream while the model reads a conflicting passage. At inference we detect conflict from the disagreement between closed-book and context-conditioned next-token distributions, and apply a targeted, magnitude-scaled suppression of those components so retrieved evidence is integrated without competing parametric interference. We evaluate on substituted-entity conflict benchmarks across several model sizes, reporting source-selection and downstream QA accuracy.
items.HH: Retrieval-context conflicts are resolved by intervening on the model's internal computation rather than arbitrating between output answers. Causal attribution locates the attention heads and MLP components that write the parametric answer into the residual stream while a conflicting passage is read. Conflict is detected from the disagreement between closed-book and context-conditioned next-token distributions, and a targeted, magnitude-scaled suppression of those components is applied so retrieved evidence is integrated without parametric interference. We evaluate on substituted-entity conflict benchmarks across model sizes, reporting source-selection and downstream QA accuracy.

items.LL: We vary task-distribution heterogeneity along a controlled axis, interpolating from within-domain to cross-domain few-shot classification, and evaluate MAML, ANIL (head-only inner loop), and BOIL (body-only inner loop) at each setting. During adaptation we measure layer-wise representation change with CCA and CKA between pre- and post-adaptation activations. We locate the crossover point at which body-only adaptation begins to outperform head-only adaptation and relate it to the measured heterogeneity level. We report which method dominates as a function of heterogeneity and describe how the locus of representational change shifts across the network.
items.LH: We vary task-distribution heterogeneity along a controlled axis, interpolating from within-domain to cross-domain few-shot classification, and evaluate MAML, ANIL (head-only inner loop), and BOIL (body-only inner loop) at each setting. Measuring layer-wise representation change with CCA and CKA between pre- and post-adaptation activations, we identify a crossover at which body-only adaptation overtakes head-only adaptation. Which method dominates depends on the heterogeneity level, and the locus of representational change shifts across the network.
items.HL: We replace the layer-aligned reuse/adaptation split with a meta-learned decomposition of the initialization's parameter tangent space into a shared subspace held fixed across tasks and a complementary subspace within which the inner loop is confined; the projection defining this split is discovered during meta-training rather than tied to architectural depth. We parameterize the adaptation-subspace rank against task-distribution heterogeneity and estimate this intrinsic adaptation dimensionality across a heterogeneity axis. Treating ANIL and BOIL as fixed low-rank projections onto head or body, we measure how the discovered subspace's dimension and layer support change with heterogeneity.
items.HH: We replace the layer-aligned reuse/adaptation split with a meta-learned decomposition of the initialization's parameter tangent space into a shared subspace held fixed across tasks and a complementary subspace within which the inner loop is confined, with the projection discovered during meta-training rather than dictated by architectural depth. We parameterize the adaptation-subspace rank against heterogeneity and estimate this intrinsic dimensionality across a heterogeneity axis. Recasting ANIL and BOIL as fixed low-rank projections, we measure how the discovered subspace's dimension and layer support shift with heterogeneity.

items.LL: We measure the data-pruning scaling exponent across several image and text datasets under multiple difficulty metrics, including margin, EL2N, and forgetting scores. For each dataset-metric pair we fit pruning curves at matched retention fractions and extract the exponent, then regress it on the variance and skewness of the empirical difficulty distribution. This produces a fitted mapping from low-order shape statistics to the observed scaling improvement, and lets us test whether the same statistics predict the exponent when the difficulty metric is changed. We report goodness of fit and the cross-metric transfer of the regression.
items.LH: We measure the data-pruning scaling exponent across several image and text datasets under multiple difficulty metrics — margin, EL2N, and forgetting — and ask whether it is governed by the variance and skewness of the empirical difficulty distribution. Fitting pruning curves at matched retention fractions, we regress the extracted exponent on these low-order moments and test this mapping for cross-metric transfer. This treats difficulty-distribution shape as a predictor of the scaling improvement.
items.HL: We model data pruning as importance sampling that reshapes the effective difficulty density, and hypothesize that the pruning scaling exponent is determined by the local power-law index with which this density vanishes at the decision margin, rather than by global moments. A self-similar reshaping argument yields discrete regimes — faster-than-power-law, power-law, and no-improvement — selected by whether the margin-tail index lies above, at, or below a critical threshold, together with a closed-form map from that index to the exponent. We derive the critical index and test it by estimating the difficulty density near the margin across datasets.
items.HH: We model data pruning as importance sampling that reshapes the effective difficulty density, and propose that the scaling exponent is governed by the local power-law index with which this density vanishes at the decision margin, not by global moments. A self-similar argument yields discrete universality classes — faster-than-power-law, power-law, and no-improvement — selected by whether the margin-tail index sits above, at, or below a critical threshold, with a closed-form index-to-exponent map. It predicts a limit below which no pruning fraction can beat power-law scaling, which we test by estimating the difficulty density near the margin across datasets.

items.LL: We compare the accuracy-robustness Pareto frontier under a perceptual threat model against Lp threat models on standard image classifiers. Using LPIPS-bounded perceptual adversarial training and PGD with L-infinity and L2 balls, we sweep a range of perturbation budgets. For each budget we record clean accuracy and robust accuracy against a common held-out attack suite, then fit the frontiers and compare their slopes. This quantifies how much of the tradeoff persists when the threat model is perceptual rather than Lp. We report results across several datasets and architectures under matched training compute.
items.LH: We compare the accuracy-robustness Pareto frontier under a perceptual threat model against Lp threat models on standard image classifiers, using LPIPS-bounded perceptual adversarial training and PGD with L-infinity and L2 balls across a sweep of perturbation budgets. For each budget we record clean and robust accuracy against a common held-out attack suite, then fit and compare frontier slopes to quantify how much of the tradeoff persists under a perceptual threat model. We report this across several datasets and architectures under matched compute.
items.HL: We model the robustness-accuracy tradeoff as a function of the alignment between the threat-model metric and the local geometry of the data's semantic manifold, rather than as a fixed cost of robustness. We learn a threat-model metric as the pullback of a self-supervised feature encoder, so its unit ball tracks label-preserving directions. Under adversarial training with this learned metric, adversarial examples become on-manifold, semantics-preserving perturbations that act as consistency regularization. We predict and test a crossover budget beyond which increasing the aligned-robustness radius raises clean accuracy, yielding a negative tradeoff slope, and relate that slope to a measurable metric-manifold alignment score.
items.HH: We model the robustness-accuracy tradeoff as governed by the alignment between the threat-model metric and the local geometry of the data's semantic manifold, not by an inherent cost of robustness. We learn a threat-model metric as the pullback of a self-supervised feature encoder, so its unit ball tracks label-preserving directions, turning adversarial examples into on-manifold, semantics-preserving perturbations that act as consistency regularization. We predict and test a crossover budget beyond which greater aligned-robustness radius raises clean accuracy, a negative tradeoff slope tied to a measurable alignment score.

items.LL: We study a budgeted variant of subgraph aggregation. Instead of encoding the full bag of node-deleted subgraphs, we select a fixed number b of them using a precomputed importance score such as node degree or betweenness centrality, then aggregate the sampled subgraphs with the standard equivariant readout. The budget b interpolates between a single whole-graph pass and the complete bag. On standard graph-classification and isomorphism benchmarks, we measure how distinguishing accuracy and runtime change as b grows, and report the accuracy-per-subgraph curve for each heuristic to identify which selection is most cost-efficient.
items.LH: We study a budgeted variant of subgraph aggregation. Rather than encoding every node-deleted subgraph, we select b of them using a precomputed importance score such as degree or betweenness centrality, then aggregate with the standard equivariant readout. The budget b interpolates between a single whole-graph pass and the complete bag. On graph-classification and isomorphism benchmarks we measure how accuracy and runtime change as b grows, and report the accuracy-per-subgraph curve.
items.HL: We treat the beyond-1-WL gain as an input-dependent symmetry-breaking budget rather than a fixed subgraph count. We first run color refinement to locate node groups sharing a stable Weisfeiler-Leman color, and compute subgraphs only for those groups, since separation is needed only where 1-WL leaves nodes indistinguishable. The selected subgraphs are processed sequentially while maintaining a running certificate of which color classes are resolved, stopping once no ambiguous class remains. We show that expected compute scales with the number of nontrivial automorphism orbits of the input rather than its node count, on inputs whose symmetry is sparse.
items.HH: We frame expressivity as an input-dependent symmetry-breaking budget. We first run color refinement to locate node groups sharing a stable Weisfeiler-Leman color and compute subgraphs only for those groups, since separation is needed only where 1-WL leaves nodes indistinguishable. The selected subgraphs are processed sequentially with a running certificate of resolved color classes, stopping once none remain ambiguous. Expected compute scales with the number of nontrivial automorphism orbits rather than node count.

items.LL: We study the amortization gap for normalizing-flow variational posteriors on targets with multiple modes. An inference network predicts the flow parameters for each observation; we take these as initialization and run a fixed number of per-datapoint gradient ascent steps on the ELBO. Using synthetic targets with a controllable number of modes, we measure the amortization gap as the ELBO difference between the network's output and the refined parameters, and report how it varies with flow depth, the number of refinement steps, and the number of modes.
items.LH: We study the amortization gap for normalizing-flow variational posteriors on targets with multiple modes. An inference network predicts the flow parameters for each observation; we take these as initialization and run a fixed number of per-datapoint gradient ascent steps on the ELBO. Using synthetic targets with a controllable number of modes, we measure the amortization gap as the ELBO difference between the network's output and the refined parameters, and report how it varies with flow depth, the number of refinement steps, and the number of modes.
items.HL: We attribute the amortization gap under multimodal targets to discontinuity in the map from observations to optimal flow parameters. Where the posterior's mode structure changes across observation space — modes appearing, merging, or exchanging labels — this map has branch points, so a single continuous inference network incurs a gap that does not close with added capacity. We replace the single network with a set of amortization heads selected by a discrete routing variable that partitions observations by posterior mode structure. On synthetic targets, we measure the gap against mode count and routing granularity.
items.HH: We attribute the amortization gap under multimodal targets to discontinuity in the map from observations to optimal flow parameters. Where the posterior's mode structure changes — modes appearing, merging, or exchanging labels — this map has branch points, so a single continuous inference network incurs a gap that no added capacity can close. We replace it with a set of amortization heads selected by a discrete routing variable that partitions observations by posterior mode structure. On synthetic targets, we measure the gap against mode count and routing granularity.

items.LL: We introduce a scalar index of the degree of complementarity in a firm's valuation, defined as the largest increase in a worker's marginal value caused by adding another worker; the index is zero exactly when preferences are substitutable. Using a setwise approximate-stability concept, we derive an upper bound on the price of stability — the welfare ratio between the best stable outcome and the unconstrained optimum — as a function of this index and market size. The bound is monotone in the index and recovers zero welfare loss at the substitutable limit. Comparative statics and simulations characterize intermediate regimes.
items.LH: We introduce a scalar index of the degree of complementarity in a firm's valuation — the largest increase in a worker's marginal value caused by adding another worker, zero exactly under substitutability. Using a setwise approximate-stability concept, we establish a bound on the price of stability, the welfare ratio between the best stable outcome and the unconstrained optimum, as a function of this index and market size. The bound is monotone and recovers zero welfare loss at the substitutable limit. Comparative statics and simulations characterize intermediate regimes.
items.HL: We treat the complementarity structure as endogenous, an object of institutional design rather than a fixed preference primitive. Each many-to-one agent can partition its positions into semi-autonomous units, each carrying substitutable preferences, at an organizational cost that rises with the strength of the complementarities severed. Stability is defined relative to a chosen partition, and the price of stability is reformulated as the minimum-cost reorganization that restores a nonempty stable set — an equilibrium balancing the welfare gains of complementary bundling against the cost of restoring stability. Existence is characterized by properties of the partition rather than the primitive valuations.
items.HH: We treat the complementarity structure as endogenous, an object of institutional design. Each many-to-one agent can partition its positions into semi-autonomous units, each carrying substitutable preferences, at an organizational cost that rises with the strength of the complementarities severed. The price of stability is reformulated as the minimum-cost reorganization that restores a nonempty stable set — an equilibrium balancing the welfare gains of complementary bundling against the cost of restoring stability. Existence is governed by the partition rather than the primitive valuations.

items.LL: This project uses linked customs microdata to estimate how bilateral trade-cost shocks affect firm-level supplier switching. For each importing firm, we treat each foreign supplier relationship as a separable extensive-margin choice governed by pair match quality and importer productivity. We measure the elasticity of link dissolution and replacement-link formation to tariff changes within product categories, and test whether more productive importers adjust their supplier set more readily. Identification exploits variation in tariff exposure across product categories within firms over time. The output is a set of supplier-switching elasticities disaggregated by firm productivity.
items.LH: This project uses linked customs microdata to study how firm-level supplier switching responds to bilateral trade-cost shocks. We treat each foreign supplier relationship as a separable extensive-margin choice governed by pair match quality and importer productivity, and measure the elasticity of link dissolution and replacement-link formation to tariff changes within product categories. The design tests whether more productive importers adjust their supplier set more readily, exploiting within-firm variation in tariff exposure over time.
items.HL: This project uses linked customs microdata to model an importing firm's sourcing decisions across its full input basket as jointly determined rather than separable, because inputs are complements in production and supplier relationships share managerial and relationship-specific fixed investments. Under this structure, a bilateral trade-cost shock confined to one input category can induce rewiring of supplier links in other, cost-unaffected categories within the same firm. We identify these within-firm cross-input spillovers by testing whether a tariff shock isolated to one category predicts link dissolution and formation in unrelated categories, beyond firm-level scale effects, and estimate the resulting network multiplier.
items.HH: This project uses linked customs microdata to model an importing firm's entire input basket as jointly determined, because inputs are complements in production and supplier relationships share managerial and relationship-specific fixed investments. A bilateral trade-cost shock confined to one input category induces rewiring of supplier links in other, cost-unaffected categories within the same firm. The design identifies these within-firm cross-input spillovers by testing whether a tariff shock isolated to one category predicts link dissolution and formation in unrelated categories, beyond firm-level scale effects, and estimates the resulting network multiplier.

items.LL: We extend empirical estimation of carbon-pricing leakage to incorporate transmission constraints in cross-border re-dispatch. Using a competitive dispatch model of two interconnected markets, one carbon-priced and one not, we estimate the leakage rate as a function of interconnector capacity and the carbon price. The model adds line-flow limits and losses to the standard merit-order reallocation, quantifying how binding transmission constraints attenuate leakage relative to unconstrained trade. We decompose observed leakage into an unconstrained-trade component and a transmission-driven attenuation. We calibrate to a single interconnection and report the sensitivity of the leakage rate to interconnector expansion and the price level.
items.LH: We estimate carbon-pricing leakage while incorporating transmission constraints in cross-border re-dispatch. Using a competitive dispatch model of two interconnected markets, one carbon-priced and one not, we estimate the leakage rate as a function of interconnector capacity and the carbon price. Adding line-flow limits and losses to the standard merit-order reallocation shows how binding transmission constraints attenuate leakage relative to unconstrained trade. We decompose observed leakage into an unconstrained-trade component and a transmission-driven attenuation. We calibrate to a single interconnection and report the leakage rate's sensitivity to interconnector expansion and the price level.
items.HL: We characterize interconnection leakage as strategic re-dispatch by firms holding generation portfolios on both sides of the regulatory border. We model an oligopoly in which multiplant generators own both carbon-priced and unpriced capacity and choose output and withholding across the interconnector to maximize joint profit. The carbon price raises the marginal cost of a firm's priced plants, inducing it to withhold priced output and substitute imports from its own unpriced plants, producing leakage as a byproduct of market-power exercise. The leakage rate becomes endogenous to cross-border ownership concentration, so structural remedies affect it independently of interconnector capacity.
items.HH: We characterize interconnection leakage as strategic re-dispatch by firms holding generation portfolios on both sides of the regulatory border. We model an oligopoly in which multiplant generators own both carbon-priced and unpriced capacity and choose output and withholding across the interconnector to maximize joint profit. The carbon price raises the marginal cost of a firm's priced plants, inducing it to withhold priced output and substitute imports from its own unpriced plants, producing leakage as a byproduct of market-power exercise. The leakage rate becomes endogenous to cross-border ownership concentration, so structural remedies affect it independently of interconnector capacity.

items.LL: We extend the standard balance-sheet contagion model to trade-credit networks, representing firms as nodes and accounts-payable obligations as directed edges. A firm defaults when unpaid receivables from defaulted counterparties exceed its liquidity buffer, using the same fixed-threshold cascade applied to interbank exposures and tracing how losses propagate step by step along directed payment chains. We let buffers and obligation sizes vary across firms and calibrate the network to firm-level supply-chain data. We then characterize how the critical shock size at which cascades become system-wide depends on the in-degree distribution and buffer heterogeneity, and report comparative statics with respect to trade-credit concentration.
items.LH: We apply the balance-sheet contagion model to trade-credit networks, representing firms as nodes and accounts-payable obligations as directed edges. A firm defaults when unpaid receivables from defaulted counterparties exceed its liquidity buffer, using the same fixed-threshold cascade to trace how losses propagate step by step along directed payment chains. Letting buffers and obligation sizes vary and calibrating to firm-level supply-chain data, we characterize how the critical shock size at which cascades turn system-wide depends on the in-degree distribution and buffer heterogeneity, and report comparative statics on trade-credit concentration.
items.HL: We model default in the trade-credit network as a strategic decision rather than a mechanical balance-sheet outcome. Each firm chooses whether to withhold payment based on the expected fraction of its counterparties that default, so one firm's default raises others' incentive to default, a strategic complementarity that generates multiple self-fulfilling equilibria. We introduce incomplete information about the common shock and apply a global-games selection to obtain a unique threshold equilibrium, yielding a belief-driven default run in which solvent firms default preemptively. We characterize how network structure shifts the selected threshold and the magnitude of coordination-failure amplification beyond mechanical loss propagation.
items.HH: We model default in the trade-credit network as a strategic decision rather than a mechanical outcome. Each firm chooses whether to withhold payment based on the expected fraction of its counterparties that default, so one firm's default raises others' incentive to default, a strategic complementarity that generates multiple self-fulfilling equilibria. Introducing incomplete information about the common shock, we apply a global-games selection to obtain a unique threshold equilibrium, yielding a belief-driven default run in which solvent firms default preemptively. We characterize how network structure shifts the threshold and the magnitude of coordination-failure amplification beyond loss propagation.

items.LL: We extend the standard validity checks for shares-based shift-share instruments. The pre-trend balance tests are weighted by the Rotemberg weights, concentrating the correlated-trends diagnostic on the industries that contribute most to identification. Pre-period outcome growth is included as a region-level control and interacted with the instrument, and the second-stage estimate is reported across these specifications. A sensitivity bound maps the magnitude of residual pre-trend imbalance among high-weight industries into a range for the bias in the estimated coefficient. We apply the procedure to a standard regional labor-market design.
items.LH: We describe validity checks for shares-based shift-share instruments. The pre-trend balance tests are weighted by the Rotemberg weights, concentrating the correlated-trends check on the industries that drive identification. Pre-period outcome growth enters as a region-level control interacted with the instrument, and the second-stage estimate is reported across specifications. A sensitivity bound maps residual pre-trend imbalance among high-weight industries into a range for the bias. We apply it to a regional labor-market design.
items.HL: We allow the exposure shares to be correlated with unobserved regional trends, assuming those trends are low-frequency relative to the national industry shocks. The shift component is band-pass filtered to retain only its high-frequency variation, and the shift-share instrument is built from this filtered component. Identification rests on a separation-of-timescales condition rather than on exogeneity of the shares or of the shock levels, since the high-frequency instrument is orthogonal to slow-moving, trend-driven share endogeneity. We assess validity by testing for a frequency gap between estimated trends and shocks, and apply the method to a regional labor-market design.
items.HH: We allow the exposure shares to be correlated with unobserved regional trends, assuming those trends are low-frequency relative to the national shocks. The shift component is band-pass filtered to retain only its high-frequency variation, from which the instrument is built. Identification rests on a separation-of-timescales condition rather than on the exogeneity of the shares or shocks: the high-frequency instrument is orthogonal to slow-moving, trend-driven share endogeneity. Validity is assessed by testing for a frequency gap between trends and shocks in a regional labor-market design.

items.LL: We extend the moment-inequality approach to set-identified entry games. Beyond the count-based inequalities of Ciliberto and Tamer (2009), we add inequalities from finer market-structure configurations and let the competitive-effect and fixed-cost parameters depend on observed firm and market covariates, such as airport presence and market size. Inference uses the conditional moment-inequality method of Andrews and Shi (2013) with instrument functions on the continuous covariates. The added moments and covariate interactions yield a tighter identified set for the payoff parameters, holding the equilibrium-selection rule unspecified.
items.LH: We tighten the moment-inequality approach for set-identified entry games. Beyond the count-based inequalities of Ciliberto and Tamer (2009), we add inequalities from finer market-structure configurations and let the competitive-effect and fixed-cost parameters depend on observed firm and market covariates, such as airport presence and market size. Inference uses the method of Andrews and Shi (2013) with instrument functions on the continuous covariates. These additions deliver a tighter identified set for the payoff parameters, leaving equilibrium selection unspecified.
items.HL: We model observed entry outcomes as a correlated equilibrium of the entry game rather than a Nash equilibrium. The public randomization device that coordinates firms, which generates multiplicity in the Nash model, is then part of the equilibrium object, so no separate selection rule is specified. Correlated-equilibrium outcome distributions satisfy linear incentive-compatibility inequalities in the payoff parameters, giving moment inequalities that are linear with a convex identified set. Inference uses the method of Andrews and Shi (2013). The competitive-effect and fixed-cost parameters are bounded without any assumption on equilibrium selection.
items.HH: We model observed entry outcomes as a correlated equilibrium of the entry game rather than a Nash equilibrium. The public randomization device that generates multiplicity becomes part of the equilibrium itself, so no separate selection rule is specified. Correlated-equilibrium outcome distributions satisfy linear incentive-compatibility inequalities in the payoff parameters, yielding linear moment inequalities with a convex identified set. Inference uses the method of Andrews and Shi (2013). The competitive-effect and fixed-cost parameters are bounded without any assumption on equilibrium selection.

items.LL: We extend Approximate Competitive Equilibrium from Equal Incomes by adding time blocks as priced, capacity-constrained resources alongside course seats. Each meeting time carries its own clearing price, and a student's payment for a schedule equals the sum of its course prices and the prices of the blocks it occupies. The price-adjustment procedure is extended to raise prices on oversubscribed blocks until block demand approximately meets capacity. Students report cardinal preferences over feasible schedules as before. We re-establish the envy-bounded-by-one-course and approximate-efficiency guarantees for the augmented set of course and time-block resources.
items.LH: We extend competitive course allocation (A-CEEI) by adding time blocks as priced, capacity-constrained resources alongside course seats. Each meeting time carries its own clearing price, so a schedule's payment sums its course prices and the prices of the blocks it occupies. The price-adjustment procedure raises prices on oversubscribed blocks until demand meets capacity. Students report cardinal preferences over feasible schedules as before. The envy-bounded-by-one-course and approximate-efficiency guarantees hold for the augmented resource set.
items.HL: We replace additively separable course and time prices with congestion tolls defined on time-block conflict patterns. A student's payment includes a nonlinear term equal to the marginal crowding externality their schedule imposes on shared blocks, priced over co-occupancy patterns rather than individual resources. Budgets are endogenous: toll revenue is rebated as equal per-student income, so congestion prices and redistributed budgets are jointly determined at a fixed point. We define fairness as no-envy in externality-adjusted budgets and re-derive existence and bounded-envy guarantees for this congestion economy rather than for the underlying exchange economy.
items.HH: We replace additively separable course prices with congestion tolls defined on time-block conflict patterns. A student's payment carries a nonlinear term equal to the marginal crowding externality their schedule imposes on shared blocks, priced over co-occupancy patterns rather than individual resources. Budgets are endogenous: toll revenue is rebated as equal income, so prices and redistributed budgets are jointly determined at a fixed point. We redefine fairness as no-envy in externality-adjusted budgets and re-derive existence and bounded-envy guarantees for this congestion economy.

items.LL: Carroll (2015) derives worst-case-optimal linear contracts when the principal knows only a subset of the agent's available actions. We add a bound: the unknown additional actions can produce output at most a known multiple of the specified actions' range. We re-optimize the linear slope against this constrained worst case and characterize the resulting robust share. The guaranteed principal payoff and the optimal share vary monotonically with the bound, and Carroll's unbounded result is recovered as the bound grows large. The contract stays linear; only the slope changes with the deviation limit.
items.LH: Carroll (2015) derives worst-case-optimal linear contracts when the principal knows only a subset of the agent's available actions. We bound the unknown additional actions to at most a known multiple of the specified actions' output range and re-optimize the robust linear slope against this constrained worst case. The guaranteed payoff and optimal share move monotonically with the bound, and Carroll's unbounded result emerges as a limiting case. The contract stays linear; only the slope changes.
items.HL: We model ambiguity about the action set as ambiguity about the agent's own perception. The agent does not fully know which actions are available and discovers them through costly search, so the effective action set is endogenous and shaped by the incentive scheme. The principal chooses a contract jointly governing search for new actions and effort on discovered ones; richer incentives induce the agent to expand the set in ways the principal cannot foresee. The robustness objective becomes worst-case over the agent's search technology rather than over a fixed hidden set, yielding contracts that dampen exploration incentives to control the ambiguity the principal itself generates.
items.HH: We model ambiguity about the action set as ambiguity about the agent's own perception. The agent does not know which actions exist and discovers them through costly search, making the effective action set endogenous and shaped by the incentives offered. The principal's contract jointly governs search and effort; richer incentives expand the set. The robustness objective becomes worst-case over the agent's search technology rather than over a fixed hidden set, yielding contracts that dampen exploration to control the self-generated ambiguity.

items.LL: This paper estimates firm-level labor-supply elasticities from online vacancy and application data, using the responsiveness of application counts to posted wages within occupation-by-locality markets. The estimated elasticities are converted into implied wage markdowns through the inverse-elasticity relationship. We document how markdowns vary with firm size, industry, and local labor-market concentration, measured by a vacancy-share Herfindahl index. As a check, the vacancy-based elasticity estimates are compared with separation-based elasticities from administrative employment records covering the same markets, and their cross-market correlation with concentration is reported. The exercise applies markdown estimation to a posting-level data source.
items.LH: This paper estimates firm-level labor-supply elasticities from online vacancy and application data, using how application counts respond to posted wages within occupation-by-locality markets. These elasticities are converted, through the inverse-elasticity relationship, into wage markdowns. We document how markdowns vary with firm size, industry, and vacancy-share concentration, and benchmark them against separation-based elasticities from the same markets, reporting their cross-market correlation with concentration.
items.HL: This paper treats the firm-level labor-supply elasticity as an endogenous choice rather than a fixed market primitive. Firms shape the elasticity they face through vacancy-posting design (non-wage amenity bundling, differentiated requirements, and application frictions), investing in differentiation that lowers own-wage elasticity and widens markdowns. Using posting text and application-process features, we identify the design-responsive component of elasticity and estimate how firms adjust posting differentiation when local competition shifts. Under this account, measured markdowns reflect an equilibrium of elasticity-shaping choices rather than fixed local market structure.
items.HH: This paper treats the firm-level labor-supply elasticity as a choice rather than a fixed market primitive. Through vacancy-posting design (non-wage amenity bundling, differentiated requirements, and application frictions), firms depress the own-wage elasticity they face and widen markdowns. Using posting text and application-process features, we isolate the design-responsive component of elasticity and estimate how firms recalibrate differentiation when local competition shifts. Under this account, measured markdowns reflect an equilibrium of elasticity-shaping choices rather than fixed market structure.

items.LL: We extend the Calvano et al. (2020) Q-learning Bertrand duopoly by adding stochastic demand shocks whose distribution is asymmetric, with negative shocks larger in magnitude than positive ones and firms differing in shock exposure. Using the standard training protocol, we compare the average collusive markup, price dispersion, and the frequency and depth of reward-punishment episodes against the stationary-demand baseline. We report whether supracompetitive pricing persists under the added volatility and how varying the degree of asymmetry shifts the average price level across independent training runs.
items.LH: We extend the Calvano et al. (2020) Q-learning Bertrand duopoly with asymmetric stochastic demand shocks, with negative shocks larger than positive ones and firms differing in exposure. Using the standard training protocol, we compare the average collusive markup, price dispersion, and the frequency and depth of reward-punishment episodes against the stationary baseline. We report whether supracompetitive pricing persists under the added volatility and how the degree of asymmetry shifts the average price level across independent training runs.
items.HL: We treat the realized demand shock as a publicly observable signal that Q-learning pricing agents can condition on, and ask whether asymmetric shocks act as an endogenous monitoring device rather than as noise. Under imperfect monitoring, a rival's price cut is ambiguous between defection and a demand-driven response; shock asymmetry lets agents attribute cuts to demand and suspend punishment during justified cuts. We test whether agents learn a shock-contingent scheme yielding state-contingent supracompetitive pricing that Pareto-dominates the unconditioned collusive outcome and lowers the detection threshold that otherwise limits collusion.
items.HH: We treat the realized demand shock as a publicly observable signal that Q-learning pricing agents can condition on, and ask whether asymmetric shocks act as an endogenous monitoring device rather than as noise. Under imperfect monitoring, a rival's price cut is ambiguous between defection and a demand-driven response; shock asymmetry lets agents attribute cuts to demand and suspend punishment during justified cuts. We test whether agents learn a shock-contingent scheme yielding state-contingent supracompetitive pricing that Pareto-dominates the unconditioned collusive outcome and lowers the imperfect-monitoring detection threshold that otherwise limits collusion.

items.LL: We extend the saturation-randomized cash-transfer design to estimate heterogeneity in general-equilibrium effects by baseline market integration. Using village-level measures of market access — distance to the nearest market town, road density, and baseline enterprise counts — we interact market access with treatment saturation. We estimate whether local price responses, enterprise-revenue spillovers, and the implied fiscal multiplier are larger in thinner or more isolated markets, where limited arbitrage and inelastic local supply amplify adjustment. The saturation-by-integration interaction is the primary parameter, reported alongside its share of the cross-village variation in estimated multipliers.
items.LH: We use the saturation-randomized cash-transfer design to estimate heterogeneity in general-equilibrium effects by baseline market integration, interacting market access — distance to the nearest market town, road density, and baseline enterprise counts — with treatment saturation. We estimate whether local price responses, enterprise-revenue spillovers, and the implied multiplier are larger in thinner, more isolated markets where arbitrage is limited. The saturation-by-integration interaction, rather than the cross-village average, is the primary reported parameter.
items.HL: We model the local general-equilibrium response to cash transfers as operating through endogenous firm entry and market power rather than an aggregate expenditure multiplier. We model the saturation shock as relaxing entrepreneurs' liquidity and credit constraints, inducing firm entry that raises product variety and erodes incumbent markups. Using the saturation-randomized design, we estimate treatment effects on firm counts, product variety, market concentration, and firm-level markups recovered from price-cost margins. We decompose non-recipient welfare gains into a demand-transfer component and a pro-competitive variety component, and test whether higher saturation lowers markups while raising nominal local demand.
items.HH: We model cash-transfer spillovers as operating through endogenous firm entry and market power rather than an aggregate expenditure multiplier. The saturation shock relaxes entrepreneurs' credit constraints, triggering entry that raises product variety and erodes incumbent markups. Using the saturation-randomized design, we estimate treatment effects on firm counts, product variety, market concentration, and firm-level markups from price-cost margins. We decompose non-recipient welfare gains into a demand-transfer component and a pro-competitive variety component, and test whether higher saturation lowers markups even as it raises nominal local demand.

items.LL: We elicit participants' probabilistic beliefs about an upcoming monetary payoff at several points before resolution, using an incentivized scoring rule. Treating the Kőszegi–Rabin expectations-based reference point as a distributed lag of these elicited beliefs, we estimate the weights that recent versus earlier expectations receive in forming the reference. Gain-loss responses to the realized outcome are measured through choices and reported affect. The design recovers the decay profile of expectation weighting and tests whether directly measured beliefs predict reference-dependent behavior as well as the model assumes.
items.LH: We elicit participants' probabilistic beliefs about an upcoming payoff at several points before resolution via an incentivized scoring rule. Treating the Kőszegi–Rabin reference point as a distributed lag of these beliefs, we estimate the weights recent versus earlier expectations receive. This lets us test whether measured beliefs predict reference-dependent behavior. Gain-loss responses are captured through choices and reported affect.
items.HL: We elicit participants' probabilistic beliefs about an upcoming monetary payoff at several points before resolution, using an incentivized scoring rule. Rather than treating the reference point as a function of expectation levels, we model it as a salience-weighted function of belief revisions — the magnitude and recency of updates — so that the operative anchor is set by the path of revisions rather than the current expected value. We estimate these revision weights and test path-dependence: whether participants with identical final expectations but different revision histories show different gain-loss responses, measured through choices and reported affect.
items.HH: We model the reference point as set by the path of belief revisions rather than by expectation levels. Eliciting probabilistic beliefs about an upcoming payoff at several points via an incentivized scoring rule, we model the reference point as a salience-weighted function of the magnitude and recency of updates, not the current expected value. This implies that participants with identical final expectations but different revision histories show different gain-loss responses. We estimate these revision weights and test this path-dependence through choices and reported affect.

items.LL: We study optimal execution when temporary price impact follows the square-root function documented for meta-orders rather than the linear form assumed in Almgren and Chriss (2001). Substituting the concave impact term into the mean-variance execution objective, we solve for the optimal child-order schedule and its dependence on order size, horizon, volatility, and risk aversion. We characterize the expected implementation cost and the cost-variance frontier and compare the resulting trajectory to the linear-impact benchmark. Calibration of the impact coefficient uses standard participation-rate and volatility normalizations, with the exponent held fixed at one-half.
items.LH: We study optimal execution with the square-root impact function documented for meta-orders. Substituting this concave impact term into the mean-variance execution objective, we solve for the optimal child-order schedule and trace its dependence on order size, horizon, volatility, and risk aversion. We characterize the expected cost and the cost-variance frontier and compare the trajectory to the linear-impact benchmark of Almgren and Chriss (2001). Calibration of the impact coefficient uses standard participation-rate and volatility normalizations, with the exponent held fixed at one-half.
items.HL: We model limit-order-book depth recovery as governed by a heterogeneous distribution of liquidity-provider reaction latencies. When a meta-order is split into child orders spaced in time, only the fraction of providers whose latency has elapsed replenishes depth before the next child arrives. We derive the realized cumulative impact as a functional of the latency distribution and show that a power-law tail in reaction times yields a square-root dependence of impact on order size, with the concavity exponent determined by the latency tail index. Predictions relate impact curvature to measurable dispersion in provider response times.
items.HH: We model limit-order-book depth recovery as governed by a heterogeneous distribution of liquidity-provider reaction latencies. When a meta-order is split into child orders spaced in time, only the fraction of providers whose latency has elapsed replenishes depth before the next child arrives. We derive the realized cumulative impact as a functional of this latency distribution and show that a power-law tail in reaction times generates a square-root dependence of impact on order size, with the concavity exponent determined by the latency tail index. The model relates impact curvature to measurable dispersion in provider response times.

items.LL: We extend recombination-based ensemble analysis by encoding additional state-specific legal constraints—county and municipal split limits and Voting Rights Act majority-minority thresholds—as acceptance filters during Markov chain sampling, so the neutral baseline reflects each state's actual redistricting rules. Instead of testing one partisan metric at a time, we combine the efficiency gap, the mean-median difference, and partisan bias into a single Mahalanobis distance from the ensemble center. This yields one calibrated outlier statistic per plan and a per-state significance threshold, which we evaluate across several recently enacted maps.
items.LH: We calibrate ensemble analysis by encoding state-specific constraints—county and municipal split limits and Voting Rights Act majority-minority thresholds—as acceptance filters during Markov chain sampling to build the baseline. We then combine the efficiency gap, mean-median difference, and partisan bias into a single Mahalanobis distance from the ensemble center. This composite yields one calibrated outlier statistic per plan and a per-state threshold, which is applied to enacted maps.
items.HL: Rather than comparing an enacted plan against a neutral ensemble, we treat the plan as the solution to an unknown constrained optimization and infer the objective its drafters implicitly maximized. Using inverse optimization over the space of legal plans, we estimate the weights of a parametric objective—compactness, community preservation, and partisan advantage—that make the plan approximately optimal. Detection rests on the recovered partisan weight and its confidence interval, derived from the plan's revealed structure rather than a tail probability from a sampled distribution. We evaluate the estimator on maps with known provenance.
items.HH: We treat the enacted plan not as a point to compare against a neutral ensemble but as the solution to an unknown constrained optimization, inferring the objective its drafters implicitly maximized. Using inverse optimization over the space of legal plans, we estimate the weights of a parametric objective—compactness, community preservation, and partisan advantage—that make the plan approximately optimal. Detection rests on the recovered partisan weight and its confidence interval, derived from revealed structure rather than a tail probability from a sampled distribution. We evaluate the estimator on maps with known provenance.

items.LL: We extend the density-wage agglomeration regression of Combes et al. (2010), keeping their history and geology instruments and worker fixed effects, by adding an interaction between local employment density and occupation-level remote-work feasibility. Using post-2020 linked employer-employee data, we estimate whether the agglomeration elasticity differs between high- and low-WFH-feasibility occupations. To account for amenity-driven residential resorting, we include origin-destination migration flows and local amenity indices. We expect measured elasticities to fall in occupations where remote work reduces the need for physical co-location, and we report the magnitude of the resulting heterogeneity.
items.LH: We extend the density-wage regression of Combes et al. (2010), keeping their history and geology instruments and worker fixed effects, by adding an interaction between local employment density and occupation-level remote-work feasibility. Using post-2020 linked employer-employee data, we ask whether the elasticity differs between high- and low-WFH-feasibility occupations, including origin-destination migration flows and local amenity indices to absorb amenity-driven resorting. Measured elasticities should fall where remote work weakens physical co-location, yielding heterogeneity across occupations.
items.HL: We treat the agglomeration elasticity as an equilibrium object generated by two-sided sorting rather than a structural parameter of place. Remote work severs residential density from productive-interaction density: high-amenity locations accumulate workers who co-locate for consumption amenities but interact little for production, while a shrinking set of hubs concentrates production spillovers. We model the measured elasticity as a sorting-weighted composite of a positive interaction channel and a near-zero amenity channel, with weights pinned down by an endogenous amenity-versus-interaction sorting equilibrium. Identical density then yields different productivity, so cross-place elasticity heterogeneity is endogenous and can locally invert.
items.HH: We treat the agglomeration elasticity as an equilibrium object generated by two-sided sorting rather than a structural parameter of place. Remote work severs residential from productive-interaction density: high-amenity locations amass workers who co-locate for consumption but interact little for production, while a shrinking set of hubs concentrates spillovers. We model the measured elasticity as a sorting-weighted composite of a positive interaction channel and a near-zero amenity channel, with weights set by an endogenous amenity-versus-interaction equilibrium. Identical density yields different productivity, so heterogeneity is endogenous and can locally invert.
